In desktop programming, the developer can often "just print to the console." In an embedded system there is **no console, no operating system, and no display by default**. The target is a bare-metal microcontroller driving sensors, motors, and communication buses. Hence, the engineer needs:

- An **emulator/simulator** to test logic before PCB fabrication.
- A **debugger with a hardware probe** to trace bugs in the field-deployed unit when something misbehaves on real hardware.

### 1.5 Core Vocabulary (must memorise for KTU viva)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Host** | The development PC (e.g., running Keil / IAR / Eclipse) |
| **Target** | The actual embedded board/MCU under development |
| **Probe** | The hardware device bridging host and target (e.g., J-Link, ST-Link, P&E Multilink) |
| **ICE** | In-Circuit Emulator — replaces the target MCU |
| **OCD** | On-Chip Debug — debug logic built *inside* the silicon |
| **JTAG** | Joint Test Action Group — IEEE 1149.1 standard 4-wire debug/test interface |
| **SWD** | Serial Wire Debug — ARM's 2-wire alternative to JTAG |
| **BDM** | Background Debug Mode — used in NXP HCS08/ColdFire |
| **BPM** | Background Patch Mode — Freescale PowerPC variant |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Emulators (KTU Module-3 Syllabus Mapping)

#### 2.1.1 Software Emulators (Simulators)
Software emulators run a **binary image** of the target firmware on the host. They model:

- The **CPU pipeline** (fetch → decode → execute → write-back)
- The **memory map** (Flash, RAM, EEPROM, SFRs)
- The **peripherals** (Timers, ADC, UART, I²C, SPI, PWM) at register level
- **Interrupt latency** and **exception vectors**
- **I/O pin behaviour** (sometimes with timing waveforms)

**Categories of Software Emulators:**

1. **Instruction-accurate simulator:** Models the architectural state (registers/memory) correctly but not the cycle count. Fastest.
2. **Cycle-accurate simulator:** Models the **clock-cycle behaviour** of every instruction, including pipeline stalls, cache misses, and bus wait states. Used in processor-design verification.
3. **Full-system emulator:** Models peripherals, external memory, and sometimes I/O pins. Examples: **QEMU, Renode, Proteus VSM, Keil µVision Simulator, MPLAB X SIM**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU textbook definition: *"A simulator is a host-resident software model of the target processor and its peripherals, which interprets the target binary instruction-by-instruction while modelling the side effects on memory-mapped registers."*

#### 2.1.2 Hardware Emulators (ICE — In-Circuit Emulator)
An ICE is a **plug-in replacement for the target MCU**. Inside the ICE pod is a **bonded-out version of the silicon** (special die that exposes internal address, data, and control buses through extra test pins that normal production silicon does not have). The pod connects to the **target PCB's MCU socket** via a special adapter, so the rest of the board (sensors, drivers, real-world signals) operates exactly as it will in production.

**Capabilities unique to ICE:**
- **Real-time trace** of every fetched instruction (using a trace buffer of several kilobytes to megabytes).
- **Hardware breakpoints** using comparators on address, data, or external trigger pins.
- **Memory substitution** — overlay external RAM/ROM onto internal address ranges (a "ROM emulator" function).
- **Performance analysis** — counts of events, execution time, code-coverage.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern terminology: most vendors (ARM, NXP, Microchip) have replaced classic ICE with **OCD + Trace probe** combos. The KTU textbook still uses the term ICE, and you should retain it for the exam.

#### 2.1.3 ROM Emulator
A specialised emulator that **plugs into the target's ROM/Flash socket** and provides the program code from the host PC's RAM. It allows you to download a new firmware image in seconds (vs. erasing and re-burning a real Flash chip, which takes 10–60 s per cycle). Especially useful in early prototyping and bootloader-free development.

#### 2.1.4 Peripheral / Device Emulators
Standalone boxes (or PC peripherals) that physically impersonate a sensor, motor, or bus. Example: an emulator that pretends to be a GPS module on the UART line so the firmware can be tested without a real GPS antenna.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Debuggers

| Debugger Class | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Source-level debugger** | Reads `.elf` symbols, allows breakpoints at C lines | GDB, Keil µVision, IAR |
| **Assembly-level debugger** | Disassembly view, instruction-level stepping | All above |
| **ROM Monitor (ROMmon)** | Small debug stub burnt in target ROM; host talks via UART | ARM Angel, eCos gdb-stub |
| **On-Chip Debugger (OCD)** | Uses TAP/BDM port on silicon itself | ARM CoreSight, NXP BDM |
| **In-Circuit Debugger (ICD)** | Low-cost OCD + probe combined (e.g., ST-Link, P&E Multilink) | Many |
| **Simulator debugger** | Debugger front-end attached to a software simulator | Keil Simulator + µVision |
| **Emulator debugger** | Debugger front-end attached to ICE hardware | Green Hills MULTI + Green Hills Probe |

### 2.3 Debugger Features (KTU asks these explicitly)

- **Run (Go)** — release CPU, execute at full speed.
- **Halt / Break** — stop CPU on a debug event.
- **Single-Step** — execute exactly one instruction (or one source line), then halt.
- **Step-Over** — execute a function call as a single step, halts *after* it returns.
- **Step-Into** — descend into the called function.
- **Step-Out** — run until current function returns, then halt.
- **Breakpoint (BP)** — a marker at an address where the CPU will halt.
  - **Software BP:** replace instruction at the address with `BKPT` / `BRK` opcode; on hit, debugger restores original instruction.
  - **Hardware BP:** internal comparator unit on the silicon; does not modify code, but limited in number (2–8 typically).
- **Watchpoint (WP)** — like a breakpoint but on a **data address** (read, write, or both).
- **Register window** — view/modify CPU registers live.
- **Memory window** — view/modify RAM/Flash live.
- **Expression watch** — evaluate a C expression whenever the CPU halts.
- **Live Watch / Periodic Update** — poll a variable while the CPU is running.
- **Trace** — record execution history (branch trace, instruction trace, data trace).
- **Code coverage** — mark which lines have been executed.
- **Profiler / Performance analysis** — histogram of where time is spent.

### 2.4 Communication Pathways Between Host and Target

The **debug probe** is the physical (or logical) bridge. Common wired interfaces:

1. **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)** — 4 mandatory signals: **TCK (Test Clock), TMS (Test Mode Select), TDI (Test Data In), TDO (Test Data Out)** + optional **TRST (Test Reset)**. Up to **5 pins**.
2. **SWD (Serial Wire Debug, ARM)** — 2 signals: **SWDIO (bidirectional data) + SWCLK (clock)**. Saves pins, ideal for small packages.
3. **BDM (Background Debug Mode, Freescale/NXP)** — single-wire or 3-wire: **DSI, DSO, BKGD**. Common on HCS08, ColdFire, S12.
4. **cJTAG / SW-DP** — compact JTAG using only 2 pins, IEEE 1149.7.
5. **UART-based ROM Monitor** — slowest, uses 2 UART pins.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Fact Sheet

| Fact / Parameter | Typical Value / Note |
|---|---|
| JTAG standard | **IEEE 1149.1** |
| JTAG mandatory signals | **TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO** (4) |
| JTAG optional signal | **TRST** |
| SWD signals | **SWDIO, SWCLK** (2) |
| BDM signals (Freescale) | **BKGD, RESET, VDD (power)** |
| Max devices in JTAG chain | Theoretically unlimited; practical ≤ **16** |
| JTAG TCK max frequency | 10–50 MHz typical |
| Hardware breakpoints in typical Cortex-M | **2 to 8** comparators |
| Watchpoints in typical Cortex-M | **2 to 4** comparators |
| Typical trace buffer depth (ETB) | 4 KB to 64 KB |
| ICE replacement clock | 1 : 1 with target clock |
| ROM-emulator download speed | **~ 100 KB/s** (vs. Flash programming **~ 1–10 KB/s**) |
| Formula for trace time | $T_{trace} = \dfrac{D_{buffer}}{f_{CPU} \times I_{per\_cycle}}$ |
| Debug overhead in software BP | Requires RAM shadow region of size = $\vert\text{code segment under BP}\vert$ |
| JTAG TCK period formula | $T_{TCK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{TCK}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pipe symbol `|` was deliberately replaced with `\vert` in the formula row to keep the markdown table intact. In your KTU answer sheet, you may write it normally.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Industry

- **Automotive ECU development:** JTAG + Trace used to certify ISO 26262 software.
- **Mobile / IoT:** SWD used for ARM Cortex-M firmware update and crash post-mortem.
- **Aerospace:** ICE + cycle-accurate simulators validate DO-178C software.
- **Medical devices:** Code coverage from emulators proves IEC 62304 compliance.
- **Consumer electronics:** ROM emulators cut prototype iteration time from minutes to seconds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because the topic of emulators and debuggers is **architectural and procedural** (not purely mathematical), this section presents three full step-by-step analytical walkthroughs that KTU examiners love to set as 14-mark questions.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough A — How a Software Breakpoint Actually Works (Step-by-Step)

> **Question type:** "Explain with a neat diagram how a software breakpoint is implemented in an embedded debugger. Show the steps for setting, hitting, and restoring the breakpoint."

**Step 1 — User request.**
The developer clicks on line 47 of `main.c` in the IDE (say Keil µVision). The IDE reads the ELF/DWARF symbols to map that line to a target address, for example `0x0800 12C4`.

**Step 2 — Debugger reads original opcode.**
The debugger (running on the host) reads the existing instruction at address `0x0800 12C4` from the target's Flash. Suppose the original instruction is:

$$
\texttt{0x4808} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \texttt{LDR r0, [pc, \#32]}
$$

The debugger saves this 2-byte (Thumb) or 4-byte (ARM) value in a host-side table called the **breakpoint shadow list**.

**Step 3 — Debugger writes a `BKPT` opcode in place.**
The debugger now writes a special **breakpoint instruction** at the same address. For ARM Cortex-M this is:

$$
\texttt{0xBE00} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \texttt{BKPT \#0}
$$

The CPU does not need to be halted for the write if the Flash controller supports **Flash Patch and Breakpoint (FPB)** unit operation, but typically the CPU is halted first.

**Step 4 — CPU executes and hits the breakpoint.**
When the developer presses **Go**, the CPU runs at full speed. The moment the program counter reaches `0x0800 12C4`, it fetches the `BKPT #0` instruction. The CPU takes a **Debug Monitor exception** (or **HardFault** in some configs), causing it to halt and signal the debug probe.

**Step 5 — Probe notifies the host.**
The OCD hardware (e.g., SWD) raises a debug-event packet. The host debugger receives it, halts the CPU, and reads back the saved PC = `0x0800 12C4`.

**Step 6 — Debugger restores the original instruction.**
The debugger consults the shadow list and writes back `0x4808` (the original `LDR`) to the target address. It then **single-steps** the CPU by exactly one instruction so that the original opcode executes and the PC moves past the breakpoint.

**Step 7 — Re-arming.**
The debugger immediately re-inserts the `BKPT #0` opcode at the address so that the next run will again halt here. (This is the "re-arm" cycle.)

**Step 8 — Halt presentation.**
The IDE highlights line 47 in the source, opens the register window, and waits for the next user action (Step, Continue, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> Because the `BKPT` opcode must be **writable** to a Flash address, the FPB unit on Cortex-M **redirects** the fetch from a small **remap table** in SRAM. This means **software breakpoints do not actually modify Flash contents** — they only modify a small SRAM redirection table. This is why Cortex-M supports **unlimited software breakpoints** despite Flash being read-only.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough B — JTAG Boundary-Scan Chain Step-by-Step

> **Question type:** "Describe the JTAG architecture. How does a multi-device JTAG chain work?"

**Step 1 — Architecture of JTAG inside a device.**
Each JTAG-capable chip contains a **Test Access Port (TAP)** controller — a 16-state finite state machine clocked by **TCK** and steered by **TMS**. Around the chip's functional I/O pins sits a chain of **boundary-scan cells**, one per pin.

**Step 2 — Chain connection (daisy chain).**
Devices on a PCB are daisy-chained: the **TDO** of device 1 feeds the **TDI** of device 2, and so on, with a single shared **TCK** and **TMS** from the probe. This forms one long shift register.

**Step 3 — Shift-IR — select instruction.**
The host drives TMS through the TAP state machine to the **Shift-IR** state. It then shifts an instruction opcode (e.g., `0b0001` for **BYPASS**, `0b0010` for **SAMPLE/PRELOAD**, `0b0011` for **EXTEST**) through all TAP controllers. Each device latches the bits destined for it.

**Step 4 — Shift-DR — shift data.**
The TAP is moved to **Shift-DR**. The boundary-scan cells of all selected devices form one long shift register. The host shifts in a vector (e.g., 1 bit per pin, 4 bits per pin for `EXTEST`).

**Step 5 — Capture / Update.**
In **Capture-DR**, each device snapshots its current pin values into the chain. In **Update-DR**, the values previously shifted in are driven onto the physical pins (for `EXTEST`). This allows testing of **inter-device connections (opens, shorts)** without running the actual functional logic.

**Step 6 — Functional use for debug.**
When the **debug logic** (e.g., ARM CoreSight DAP) is selected via the JTAG instruction register, subsequent DR shifts read/write **CPU registers, memory, and breakpoints** as if the JTAG were a serial backdoor into the core.

**Step 7 — Time to shift N bits in a chain of D devices:**
$$
T_{shift} = \frac{N_{bits}}{f_{TCK}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{D} L_i}{f_{TCK}}
$$

where $L_i$ is the length of the boundary-scan register of device $i$, and $f_{TCK}$ is the TCK frequency.

> **Example calculation:**
> A board has 3 devices with $L_1 = 320, \; L_2 = 800, \; L_3 = 64$ bits, and $f_{TCK} = 10$ MHz. The total scan time is:

$$
N_{bits} = 320 + 800 + 64 = 1184 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
T_{shift} = \frac{1184}{10^{7}} = 1.184 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s} = 118.4 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough C — Full Debug Session Lifecycle (Host ↔ Probe ↔ Target)

This is the **master flow** the KTU paper often asks for in 14-mark form.

**Step 1 — Connection establishment.**
The host launches the IDE. The IDE enumerates the probe over USB. The probe negotiates with the target's **DAP (Debug Access Port)** through the JTAG/SWD pins. The host reads the **IDCODE** register of every device in the JTAG chain to confirm the silicon.

**Step 2 — Target halt request.**
The host writes to the **CTRL/STAT** register of the DAP, setting the **DBGACK** and **HALT** bits. The CPU completes the current instruction and then enters debug state with the PC visible to the host.

**Step 3 — Symbol/image load.**
The IDE downloads the ELF file to the target's Flash via the Flash-memory programming algorithms (also steered through JTAG/SWD). It also uploads the **debug symbols** (DWARF) into its own host memory — these are not sent to the target.

**Step 4 — Breakpoint insertion.**
The IDE uses **software breakpoints** (FPB remap on Cortex-M) or **hardware breakpoints** (FPB comparators, up to 8 on Cortex-M4). Each BP entry includes the address and the original opcode in the host shadow memory.

**Step 5 — Run.**
The host clears the HALT bit. The target runs at full clock speed. The host polls the **HALTED** status bit at low frequency, or waits for an asynchronous **debug event** packet.

**Step 6 — Debug event handling.**
On a breakpoint hit, the probe raises a debug event. The host reads the **PC, xPSR, SP, LR, and R0–R12** from the DAP. It also reads any **watched memory regions** and evaluates user-defined expressions. Results are presented in the IDE windows.

**Step 7 — Step.**
For single-step, the host sets the **HALT_ON_STEP** bit. The CPU executes one instruction and re-enters debug state. Step-Over and Step-Into are emulated by the host debugger by analysing the disassembly of the current instruction.

**Step 8 — Continue / Stop / Reset.**
Continue clears HALT and resumes. Stop sets HALT. Reset pulses the **SYSRESETREQ** bit in the Cortex-M **AIRCR** register; the probe can also drive the physical RESET pin.

**Step 9 — Detach.**
On session end, the host writes `0` to the FPB comparators, removes the SWD/JTAG connection, and lets the target run autonomously (or remain in reset).

---

### 3.4 KTU Quick-Reference Comparison Matrix (Examiners expect this as a table)

| Aspect | Simulator (Software Emulator) | ICE (Hardware Emulator) | OCD (JTAG/SWD) | ROM Monitor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Real target hardware required? | No | Yes (CPU pod replaces MCU) | Yes | Yes |
| Real-time accurate? | Often not (cycle-accurate only) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Speed vs. target | 10× – 1000× slower | Real-time | Real-time | Near real-time |
| Can stop on hardware trigger? | Limited | Yes (logic analyser inputs) | Yes (cross-trigger) | No |
| Number of breakpoints | Unlimited (soft) | Many hardware BPs | 2–8 hardware BPs | Unlimited (soft) |
| Modifies target code? | No | No (overlay) | No (FPB remap) | Yes (debug stub in Flash) |
| Cost | Free–low | Very high | Low–medium | Free (uses UART) |
| Typical use | Algorithm early dev, CI/CD | SoC bring-up, certification | Production firmware dev | Bootloader-stage debug, low-cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture — Embedded Development Toolchain (Block Diagram)

This Mermaid diagram shows the **complete debug/emulation chain** from the developer's source code to the running target. It is the single most important diagram for the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hostIDE["HOST IDE"] --> editor["Source Editor"]
    hostIDE --> compiler["Cross Compiler arm gcc"]
    compiler --> elfOut["ELF + DWARF Symbols"]
    elfOut --> hostDbg["Host Debugger GDB Keil IAR"]
    hostDbg --> probe["Debug Probe J-Link ST-Link P and E"]
    probe -->|"SWD 2 wires SWDIO SWCLK"| swdPort["Target SWD Port on MCU"]
    probe -->|"JTAG 4 wires TCK TMS TDI TDO"| jtagPort["Target JTAG Port on MCU"]
    swdPort --> dap["DAP Debug Access Port inside silicon"]
    jtagPort --> dap
    dap --> fpb["FPB Breakpoint unit"]
    dap --> dwt["DWT Watch and Trace unit"]
    dap --> itm["ITM Instrumentation Trace Macrocell"]
    dap --> etb["ETB Embedded Trace Buffer"]
    dap --> cpuCore["CPU Core ARM Cortex M"]
    fpb --> flashMem["Internal Flash"]
    fpb --> sram["Internal SRAM"]
    cpuCore --> flashMem
    cpuCore --> sram
    cpuCore --> peripherals["Peripherals Timer UART ADC GPIO"]
    peripherals --> extWorld["External World Sensors Motors"]
    hostDbg -.->|"reads symbols only"| elfOut
    probe -.->|"TDO trace packets"| hostDbg
```

### 4.2 JTAG TAP Controller State Machine (Simplified)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TestLogicReset
    TestLogicReset --> RunTestIdle
    RunTestIdle --> SelectDRScan
    RunTestIdle --> SelectIRScan
    SelectDRScan --> CaptureDR
    SelectIRScan --> CaptureIR
    CaptureDR --> ShiftDR
    CaptureIR --> ShiftIR
    ShiftDR --> ShiftDR
    ShiftIR --> ShiftIR
    ShiftDR --> Exit1DR
    ShiftIR --> Exit1IR
    Exit1DR --> PauseDR
    Exit1IR --> PauseIR
    PauseDR --> Exit2DR
    PauseIR --> Exit2IR
    Exit2DR --> UpdateDR
    Exit2IR --> UpdateIR
    UpdateDR --> RunTestIdle
    UpdateIR --> RunTestIdle
    UpdateDR --> SelectDRScan
    UpdateIR --> SelectDRScan
```

### 4.3 Software Breakpoint Lifecycle (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant IDE as Host IDE
    participant HDBG as Host Debugger
    participant Probe as Debug Probe
    participant MCU as Target MCU
    Dev->>IDE: Click on line 47 in source
    IDE->>HDBG: Resolve line to address 0x080012C4
    HDBG->>Probe: Read 2 bytes from 0x080012C4
    Probe->>MCU: DAP read
    MCU-->>Probe: original opcode 0x4808
    Probe-->>HDBG: 0x4808
    HDBG->>HDBG: Save 0x4808 in shadow table
    HDBG->>Probe: Write BKPT opcode 0xBE00 to 0x080012C4
    Probe->>MCU: DAP write
    Dev->>IDE: Press Go
    IDE->>HDBG: Run
    HDBG->>Probe: Clear HALT request
    Probe->>MCU: Resume execution
    MCU-->>Probe: Debug event hit at 0x080012C4
    Probe-->>HDBG: HALTED, PC=0x080012C4
    HDBG->>Probe: Write back 0x4808
    Probe->>MCU: Restore original opcode
    HDBG->>Probe: Single-step
    MCU-->>Probe: PC advanced to 0x080012C6
    HDBG->>Probe: Re-write 0xBE00
    HDBG-->>IDE: Report halt at line 47
    IDE-->>Dev: Highlight line 47 in source
```

### 4.4 Emulator vs Debugger — Responsibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EMULATOR["EMULATOR Domain"]
        E1["Mimics target hardware"]
        E2["Runs target binary on host"]
        E3["Models CPU memory peripherals timing"]
        E4["ICE pod replaces silicon"]
        E5["ROM emulator fills Flash socket"]
    end
    subgraph DEBUGGER["DEBUGGER Domain"]
        D1["Controls execution Go Halt Step"]
        D2["Inspects state registers memory"]
        D3["Modifies state live"]
        D4["Sets breakpoints watchpoints"]
        D5["Reports trace coverage"]
    end
    EMULATOR --> DEBUGGER
    DEBUGGER --> EMULATOR
    note1["Emulator provides the execution environment; Debugger provides the visibility and control. They are USED TOGETHER."]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between a **simulator** and an **emulator** in the context of embedded systems development.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Simulator | Emulator |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Pure software running on host | Hardware pod (or hybrid software) |
| Real target hardware | **Not required** | **Required** (or pod replaces it) |
| Timing accuracy | Cycle-accurate only for high-end models | Real-time in ICE |
| Cost | Free / low | High for ICE; low for software emulators |
| Use case | Early algorithm development, CI/CD pipelines | Hardware integration, real-world I/O testing |

A simulator only **models the CPU's instruction execution and memory behaviour** on the host PC, while an emulator either **fully replaces** the target MCU (ICE) or **reproduces peripheral behaviour** so the program can be exercised in or near real-time with actual external hardware. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List any **three differences** between **JTAG** and **SWD** debug interfaces.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pin count** — JTAG uses **4 mandatory pins** (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) plus optional TRST, while SWD uses only **2 pins** (SWDIO, SWCLK). (1 mark)
2. **Standard** — JTAG is the **IEEE 1149.1** boundary-scan standard applicable to any vendor, while SWD is **ARM-specific** (introduced in CoreSight). (1 mark)
3. **Functionality** — JTAG supports both **boundary-scan** (board test) **and** debug, whereas SWD is a **debug-only** protocol and is faster for register access in ARM cores. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **JTAG architecture** in detail. Describe the role of the **TAP controller**, the **instruction register**, the **data register**, and the **boundary-scan cells**. Draw the TAP state-transition diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **working of a typical In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)**. Explain how it differs from a **ROM emulator** and an **on-chip debugger (OCD)**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — JTAG Architecture [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
JTAG (Joint Test Action Group) is a 4-wire (or 5-wire) serial protocol standardised as **IEEE 1149.1** for testing interconnections on a PCB and for accessing debug logic inside ICs.

**Mandatory signals (1 mark):**

- **TCK (Test Clock)** — driven by the host probe; clocks all TAP transitions.
- **TMS (Test Mode Select)** — sampled on rising edge of TCK; selects next state in the TAP FSM.
- **TDI (Test Data In)** — serial data shifted **into** the device on TCK rising edge.
- **TDO (Test Data Out)** — serial data shifted **out of** the device on TCK falling edge.
- **TRST (Test Reset)** — optional asynchronous reset of the TAP FSM.

**Building blocks (3 marks):**

- **TAP Controller** — 16-state FSM clocked by TCK, steered by TMS. States include **Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Select-DR-Scan, Capture-DR, Shift-DR, Exit1-DR, Pause-DR, Exit2-DR, Update-DR** and corresponding IR states.
- **Instruction Register (IR)** — holds the currently selected JTAG instruction (e.g., BYPASS, EXTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, IDCODE, debug-related private instructions).
- **Data Registers (DR)** — a set of shift registers selected by the IR. Common ones are **BYPASS (1 bit), IDCODE (32 bits), Boundary-Scan Register (1 bit per pin), and the debug-specific DRs**.
- **Boundary-Scan Cells** — placed between each functional I/O pin and the core logic. They capture pin values, drive pin values, and shift serially under JTAG control.

**Working (2 marks):**
To test inter-chip connections: (i) load **EXTEST** into IR, (ii) shift a stimulus vector into the boundary-scan DR, (iii) move to Update-DR so the cells drive that vector onto the pins, (iv) the connected chip's input pin receives it and the next chip captures it in its own boundary scan on the next Capture-DR; (v) shift out and compare with expected. For debug, vendor-specific private IR instructions expose the CPU's debug registers through the same JTAG mechanism.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid TAP state diagram).**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — ICE, ROM Emulator, OCD [7 marks]

**ICE working (3 marks):**
An ICE consists of (i) a **bonded-out processor pod** containing a special silicon die with extra test pins exposing internal address/data/control buses, (ii) a **control unit** that buffers the buses, (iii) a **trace buffer** (typically KB to MB of SRAM) recording every fetched instruction, (iv) a **comparator unit** for hardware breakpoints/watchpoints, and (v) a **host-side interface** (USB/Ethernet) to the IDE. The pod plugs into the target's MCU socket via an adapter, so the real peripheral circuitry on the PCB drives the actual electrical signals while the developer inspects the bus in real time. **Block diagram: refer to Section 4.4 / 4.1 Mermaid diagrams.**

**ROM Emulator (2 marks):**
A ROM emulator is a small box that physically **plugs into the target's ROM/Flash socket** and presents a SRAM/DRAM-backed memory image to the target. The host downloads new code into the emulator over a parallel/USB cable in seconds, avoiding the slow Flash erase-program cycle. It does **not** expose CPU buses — it only fakes memory contents.

**OCD (2 marks):**
An On-Chip Debugger uses **debug logic built into the silicon** itself (e.g., ARM CoreSight DAP, NXP BDM). The CPU has dedicated debug pins (JTAG/SWD/BDM) and dedicated internal registers. The external probe commands the CPU via serial packets. There is no bonded-out die; instead the silicon itself provides observability through special registers. OCD is far cheaper than ICE, supports an unlimited number of software breakpoints (via FPB), and is the modern standard.

**Differences tabulated (within the 7 marks):**

| Aspect | ICE | ROM Emulator | OCD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Replaces target CPU? | Yes (pod) | No, replaces memory | No, uses built-in logic |
| Real-time trace? | Yes (large buffer) | No | Limited (ETB) |
| Cost | Very high | Medium | Low |

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a **sequence diagram / flow description**, explain how a **software breakpoint** is set, hit, and restored by an embedded debugger.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** and **ROM Emulator**. List the typical **debug probe hardware** used with ARM Cortex-M targets and the **debug protocols** they support.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Software Breakpoint Lifecycle [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** A software breakpoint is a user-requested halt point implemented by **temporarily replacing the opcode at a target address** with a debug-trigger instruction such as ARM's `BKPT #0` (`0xBE00` in Thumb).

**Step-by-step (5 marks):**

1. The IDE maps the source line to a **target address** via DWARF/ELF symbols.
2. The **host debugger** reads and saves the original 2-byte (Thumb) or 4-byte (ARM) opcode at that address into a host-side **shadow table**.
3. The debugger writes the `BKPT` opcode in place. On Cortex-M, this is done via the **Flash Patch and Breakpoint (FPB)** unit which remaps the fetch to a small SRAM redirection table — so the actual Flash is **not modified**.
4. When the target is **run**, the CPU executes instructions at full speed until the PC reaches the breakpoint address; the CPU fetches the `BKPT`, raises a **Debug Monitor exception** (or HardFault), and halts.
5. The **debug probe** notifies the host. The host reads the saved PC, restores the original opcode, **single-steps one instruction** to move past the breakpoint, then re-inserts the `BKPT` for the next run.
6. The IDE highlights the source line and displays registers, memory, and variables.

**Advantages (1 mark):** Unlimited breakpoints, can be set at any addressable location, does not consume the scarce hardware comparators.

**Sequence diagram: refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram.**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — ICE vs ROM Emulator + ARM Debug Probes [7 marks]

**ICE vs ROM Emulator (4 marks):**

- ICE **replaces the processor** itself, exposing internal buses; ROM emulator **replaces the program memory** chip, providing code via fast SRAM/DRAM download.
- ICE enables **real-time trace** of every instruction, **hardware breakpoints**, and **memory substitution**; ROM emulator only **speeds up the edit-build-test cycle** by avoiding Flash burn time.
- ICE is **expensive** (legacy Green Hills, Lauterbach); ROM emulator is **moderate cost** and still useful in production-line programming.
- ICE is largely **superseded** by OCD in modern designs; ROM emulators survive in bootloader-less rapid prototyping.

**ARM Cortex-M debug probes (3 marks):**

| Probe | Vendor | Protocols supported | Typical cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **J-Link** | Segger | JTAG, SWD, cJTAG, SWO | Medium |
| **ST-Link V2 / V3** | STMicroelectronics | SWD, JTAG (V2-ISOL only) | Low (often bundled) |
| **ULINKpro / ULINK2** | Keil / ARM | JTAG, SWD, ETM trace (ULINKpro) | Medium-High |
| **P&E Multilink** | P&E Micro | JTAG, SWD | Medium |
| **CMSIS-DAP** | Open-source (ARM mbed) | JTAG, SWD | Low (often on-board) |
| **Lauterbach TRACE32** | Lauterbach | JTAG, SWD, parallel trace | Very high |

ARM Cortex-M cores (M0/M0+/M3/M4/M7) expose both **JTAG (TCK/TMS/TDI/TDO)** and **SWD (SWDIO/SWCLK)** pins, plus optional **SWO (Serial Wire Output)** for `ITM` printf-style trace and **TRACEDATA** pins for `ETM` parallel trace.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Emulators & Debuggers Questions**
>
> 1. **Writing "simulator" and "emulator" as synonyms.** A simulator is *only software*; an emulator is *hardware (or hybrid) that replaces the target*. Marks deducted.
> 2. **Forgetting the JTAG standard number.** Examiners expect **IEEE 1149.1** explicitly. Saying "JTAG is a 4-wire standard" without the IEEE number loses a mark.
> 3. **Mixing up BDM and JTAG.** BDM is **Freescale/NXP** (HCS08, ColdFire, S12); JTAG is **generic IEEE**; SWD is **ARM**. Listing them as one and the same is a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Not drawing the TAP state diagram.** A 7-mark JTAG question **always** wants the state machine. Skipping it costs at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Saying "breakpoint stops the program"** without explaining **how** (FPB remap, original opcode shadow, single-step restore) — examiners want the mechanism.
> 6. **Confusing hardware and software breakpoints.** Hardware = on-chip comparator (limited count, no code modification). Software = opcode replacement via FPB (unlimited but requires a remap table or Flash write).
> 7. **Spelling JTAG as "J-TAG" or "Jtag".** Use uppercase: **JTAG**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Emulator** mimics the target hardware; **Debugger** controls and observes execution. They work together.
- **Simulator** = software only, runs on host. **ICE** = hardware pod, replaces MCU. **ROM Emulator** = replaces memory. **OCD** = uses built-in silicon debug logic.
- **JTAG** = IEEE 1149.1, 4 mandatory signals (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) + optional TRST, supports boundary-scan and debug.
- **SWD** = ARM-specific, 2 signals (SWDIO, SWCLK), debug-only, faster than JTAG for ARM cores.
- **BDM** = Freescale/NXP, uses BKGD pin, common on HCS08/ColdFire/S12.
- **TAP Controller** is a 16-state FSM. Key states: **Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Shift-DR, Capture-DR, Update-DR** (and IR equivalents).
- **Software breakpoint** = replace opcode with `BKPT #0` (0xBE00 on Cortex-M); original saved in host shadow table.
- **Hardware breakpoint** = on-chip comparator (FPB on Cortex-M), typically 2–8 available, no code modification.
- **Watchpoint** = data-address breakpoint (read, write, or both).
- **Trace** = execution history; **SWO** (1 wire) and **ETM** (4+ wires parallel) are the two ARM trace methods.
- **ROM Monitor** = small debug stub in target Flash, talks to host via UART; slowest but free.
- **Typical ARM probes** = J-Link, ST-Link, ULINK, P&E Multilink, CMSIS-DAP.
- **Formulas to memorise:** $T_{TCK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{TCK}}$ and $T_{shift} = \dfrac{\sum L_i}{f_{TCK}}$.
- **Number-of-Hardware-BP rule of thumb:** Cortex-M0/M0+ = 4 BPs, M3/M4 = 8 BPs, M7 = 8 BPs (always confirm in the device's TRM).
- **Exam mantra:** "JTAG = boundary scan + debug, SWD = debug only, BDM = Freescale, ROM monitor = bootloader-stage cheap debug."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Product Development Cycle (EDLC) - Different Phases of EDLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT637 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND ITS APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Embedded Product Development Cycle (EDLC) - Different Phases of EDLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded Product Development Life Cycle (EDLC)** is the structured, multi-phase engineering methodology that governs the complete journey of an embedded system — from the initial identification of a market or application *need* to the final deployment, field operation, and end-of-life *disposal* of the product. Unlike generic software life cycle models, EDLC explicitly accounts for the **co-design of hardware and software**, **real-time constraints**, **resource limitations (memory, power, compute)**, and **certification/validation cycles** that are intrinsic to embedded products.

The EDLC is the *de facto* standard process framework mandated across industries that design products such as automotive ECUs, consumer IoT devices, medical instruments, avionics controllers, and industrial PLCs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — Building a Customised House:**
> Think of EDLC exactly like the process an architect follows when you commission a **custom-designed house** rather than buying a ready-made one.
> - **Concept/Need phase** = You telling the architect, *"I need a 3-bedroom house with a garden in Kerala monsoon climate."*
> - **Specifications phase** = The architect listing: *square footage, number of rooms, load-bearing capacity, slope of roof (in degrees), plumbing lines.*
> - **Architecture design** = Drawing the *blueprint* (floor plan, wiring diagram).
> - **Hardware + Software design** = Choosing *bricks, cement, electrical wiring* (hardware) AND choosing *smart-home automation logic* (software).
> - **Implementation** = The actual *construction* and *coding*.
> - **Testing** = Inspecting the house for leaks, structural integrity, electrical safety.
> - **Deployment** = You moving in.
> - **Maintenance** = Repainting, fixing leaks, upgrading the Wi-Fi router.
> - **Disposal/Upgrade** = Demolishing or renovating after decades.

Just as you cannot build a house by jumping straight to construction, you **cannot design an embedded product** by jumping straight to writing firmware. The EDLC enforces a disciplined, phase-gated journey.

### 1.3 Why EDLC is *Different* from the Generic SDLC

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Exam Favourite Point:**
> Three key differentiators make EDLC distinct from standard Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC):
> 1. **Hardware-Software Co-Design** — software cannot be designed in isolation; the target processor, memory map, and I/O peripherals drive software architecture.
> 2. **Real-Time and Resource Constraints** — deadlines, power budgets (often in **milliwatts**), and memory footprints (often in **kilobytes**) are first-class design citizens, not afterthoughts.
> 3. **Cross-Layer Validation** — the same product must pass electrical, thermal, EMC, and functional-safety (e.g., **ISO 26262** for automotive) tests before release.

### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics Used in EDLC

> [!NOTE]
> - **TTM (Time-to-Market):** typically **6–18 months** for consumer embedded products.
> - **BOM (Bill of Materials) Cost Target:** often a fixed ceiling in cents/dollars per unit.
> - **Power Budget:** typically **< 1 W** for battery-operated IoT devices.
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** industrial products target **> 50,000 hours**.
> - **Product Lifetime:** **5–10 years** for automotive, **2–3 years** for consumer gadgets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 The Eight Canonical Phases of EDLC

The embedded product development cycle is universally accepted as comprising the following sequential, iterative, and often overlapping phases:

#### **Phase 1: Concept / Need Identification**
- The *trigger* of the cycle. It answers: *"What problem are we solving, and for whom?"*
- Activities: market study, competitor analysis, identification of customer pain points, feasibility check.
- Deliverable: a **Product Concept Document** (1–2 pages describing the vision, target audience, and high-level features).
- Key question: Is the product **technically feasible**, **economically viable**, and **legally compliant**?

#### **Phase 2: Requirements Analysis (System Requirements)**
- Converts the *wish-list* of Phase 1 into **measurable, testable engineering requirements**.
- Two categories emerge:
  - **Functional Requirements** — *what the system must do* (e.g., "read temperature every 100 ms").
  - **Non-Functional Requirements** — *how well it must do it* (e.g., "operate from −20 °C to +70 °C", "battery life ≥ 5 years").
- Deliverable: **SRS (Software/System Requirements Specification)** document — often following **IEEE 830** format.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Term:** The SRS is *contractual* — once signed off, every subsequent phase must trace back to it. This traceability is what enables later **certification audits**.

#### **Phase 3: Specifications Translation (Engineering Specifications)**
- The SRS is decomposed into the *two* parallel engineering specifications:
  - **Hardware Specification** — processor choice (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 @ **180 MHz**), memory sizes, sensor/actuator I/O, power tree.
  - **Software Specification** — RTOS choice (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, ThreadX), driver modules, communication stacks (TCP/IP, CAN, BLE), firmware architecture.
- Deliverable: **HRS (Hardware Requirements Spec)** + **SRS-firmware (Software Requirements Spec)**.

#### **Phase 4: Architecture & High-Level Design**
- Defines the *blueprint* of the system — both hardware architecture and software architecture.
- **Hardware Architecture:** block diagrams of MCU + sensors + actuators + power + comms.
- **Software Architecture:** layered firmware stack (Application → Middleware → Drivers → HAL → BSP).
- Decisions: monolithic firmware vs. RTOS-based, polling vs. interrupt-driven, custom PCB vs. SoM (System on Module).
- Deliverable: **High-Level Design Document (HLD)** + **schematics (block level)**.

#### **Phase 5: Component / Detailed Design (Low-Level Design)**
- The HLD is decomposed into granular, implementable units.
- **Hardware:** schematic capture (in tools like Altium Designer, KiCad), PCB layout, BOM finalisation.
- **Software:** detailed function-level design — data structures, state machines, algorithms, module APIs, memory-mapped I/O register definitions.
- Deliverable: **LLD (Low-Level Design) Document**, full **schematics**, **netlist**, and **firmware module specifications**.

#### **Phase 6: Implementation / Coding & Prototyping**
- The *construction* phase. Two parallel work-streams execute:
  - **Hardware:** PCB fabrication, soldering, bring-up of bare board.
  - **Software:** firmware coding in C/C++/Rust, RTOS porting, driver development, application logic.
- Tools: Keil MDK, STM32CubeIDE, IAR Embedded Workbench, GCC ARM toolchain.
- Deliverable: **Working prototype hardware** + **compiled firmware binary (.elf / .hex)**.

#### **Phase 7: Testing, Debugging & Validation**
- The *quality gate*. Embedded testing is multi-layered:
  - **Unit Testing** — individual firmware modules (using CppUTest, Unity).
  - **Integration Testing** — modules interacting.
  - **System Testing** — full product against the SRS.
  - **Hardware Testing** — EMC/EMI, thermal, power, signal integrity.
  - **Field Testing / Beta Trials** — real-world deployment on a small user set.
  - **Certification Testing** — FCC, CE, BIS, **ISO 26262**, IEC 61508, depending on domain.
- Tools: oscilloscopes, logic analysers, JTAG/SWD debuggers, HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop) simulators.
- Deliverable: **Test Reports**, **Defect Logs**, **Certification Certificates**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Believing testing happens *only at the end*. In mature EDLC, testing is **continuous** — unit tests are written alongside code (TDD), and hardware bring-up tests begin the moment the first PCB powers on.

#### **Phase 8: Deployment, Maintenance & End-of-Life**
- **Deployment:** mass manufacturing, packaging, supply-chain logistics, OTA (Over-The-Air) update infrastructure.
- **Maintenance:** bug fixes, security patches, feature upgrades, recall handling.
- **End-of-Life (EOL):** component obsolescence management, last-time-buy, responsible disposal/recycling compliance with **WEEE** and **RoHS** directives.
- Deliverable: **Released product**, **maintenance plan**, **EOL notice**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Framework / "Cheat Sheet"

| **Phase #** | **Phase Name** | **Primary Output (Deliverable)** | **Key Stakeholders** | **% of Total Project Effort (Typical)** |
|:-----------:|:---------------|:---------------------------------|:---------------------|:----------------------------------------:|
| 1 | Concept / Need | Product Concept Document | Marketing, Management | **5%** |
| 2 | Requirements Analysis | SRS (IEEE 830) | System Engineers, Customers | **10%** |
| 3 | Specifications | HRS + Firmware Spec | HW & SW Architects | **8%** |
| 4 | Architecture & HLD | HLD Document, Block Diagrams | Lead Engineers | **12%** |
| 5 | Detailed / LLD | Schematics, Netlist, LLD | Design Engineers | **15%** |
| 6 | Implementation | Prototype + Firmware Binary | Developers, HW Eng. | **30%** |
| 7 | Testing & Validation | Test Reports, Certificates | QA, Certification Bodies | **15%** |
| 8 | Deployment & Maintenance | Released Product, OTA Pipeline | Operations, Support | **5% (ongoing)** |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Bosch ECU):** EDLC enables adherence to **ASPICE** and **ISO 26262** — non-negotiable for safety-critical ECUs.
- **Medical Devices (e.g., pacemaker):** EDLC aligns with **IEC 62304** and **FDA 510(k)** submission requirements.
- **Consumer IoT (e.g., smart bulb):** EDLC compresses TTM, making it the *only* way to ship 5 product variants per year.
- **Aerospace (e.g., flight controller):** EDLC integrates **DO-178C** (software) and **DO-254** (hardware) compliance from day one.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Decision Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — How a Requirement Flows Through the EDLC

To make the abstract phases concrete, let us trace **one single requirement** through the entire cycle.

> **Example Requirement:** *"The system shall measure ambient temperature and transmit it wirelessly every 10 seconds."*

**Step 1 — Concept Phase Treatment:**
The marketing team identifies a market need: *"Users want a remote, battery-powered temperature sensor for cold-chain logistics in the pharmaceutical industry."*
→ Output: 1-paragraph concept note mentioning "wireless temperature logger".

**Step 2 — Requirements Phase Treatment:**
System engineers formalise this into measurable statements:
- **FR-01:** The device shall sense ambient temperature in the range **−40 °C to +85 °C**.
- **FR-02:** The device shall transmit temperature readings over a wireless link at intervals of **10 s ± 5%**.
- **NFR-01:** Battery life shall be **≥ 3 years** on a single **CR2032 coin cell** (≈ **220 mAh**).
- **NFR-02:** Device weight shall be **< 50 grams**.

**Step 3 — Specifications Phase Treatment:**
The requirements are translated into engineering specs:
- **Hardware Spec:** Use a low-power MCU (e.g., **STM32L0 @ 32 MHz**), digital temperature sensor (**TMP117**, ±0.1 °C accuracy), sub-GHz radio (**CC1101**).
- **Software Spec:** Use an event-driven firmware architecture (no full RTOS needed); implement sleep currents in firmware to achieve the 3-year battery target.

**Step 4 — Architecture Phase Treatment:**
High-level block diagram is drawn:
- Power domain (battery → LDO → MCU + sensors)
- Sensor domain (I²C bus to TMP117)
- RF domain (SPI bus to CC1101)
- Compute domain (ARM Cortex-M0+ core)
- A state-machine architecture with three states: **SLEEP → MEASURE → TRANSMIT → SLEEP**.

**Step 5 — Detailed Design Phase Treatment:**
- **Schematic:** MCU pinout assigned (PA0 = TMP117 SDA, PA1 = TMP117 SCL, PB5 = CC1101 CSn, …).
- **Firmware LLD:** Function signatures drafted:
  - `float read_temperature(void);`
  - `void radio_transmit(uint8_t *payload, uint8_t len);`
  - `void enter_sleep_mode(void);`
- **Power budget calculation** is performed at this stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight — Why Specifications and Design are *separate* phases:**
> Merging them is a common amateur mistake. The Specifications phase answers *"what numbers must we hit?"*, while the Design phase answers *"how do we hit those numbers?"*. This separation allows multiple designs to be evaluated against the *same* specification baseline.

**Step 6 — Implementation Phase Treatment:**
- **Hardware:** PCB fabricated (4-layer, 35 mm × 25 mm), assembled, and powered on. LED blinks → board is alive.
- **Software:** Drivers for TMP117 and CC1101 written, state machine implemented in C, compiled with arm-none-eabi-gcc, flashed via SWD.

**Step 7 — Testing Phase Treatment:**
- Functional test: heat gun applied, readings match within **±0.2 °C**.
- Battery life test: simulated current profile (**3 µA average**) yields expected **3.1 years**.
- Wireless range test: **−95 dBm** RSSI at 100 m line-of-sight.
- EMC pre-compliance: passes EN 301 489.

**Step 8 — Deployment Phase Treatment:**
- 10,000 units manufactured via SMT pick-and-place.
- Shipped with a **unique device ID** burned into flash.
- OTA bootloader implemented for future firmware updates.
- End-of-life: a "**last-time-buy**" notice is issued 6 months before component EOL.

### 3.2 Comparative Process Models Used in EDLC

EDLC is a *framework*. The **process model** dictates *how* the phases inter-relate. The three most common process models are:

| **Model** | **Structure** | **Best Suited For** | **Key Drawback** |
|:----------|:--------------|:--------------------|:-----------------|
| **Waterfall** | Strictly sequential, no overlap | Simple, well-understood products (e.g., a digital thermometer) | Late discovery of defects; expensive rework |
| **V-Model** | Each design phase has a paired test phase; shaped like a *V* | Safety-critical systems (automotive, medical, aerospace) | Still largely sequential |
| **Spiral** | Iterative loops through all phases; risk-driven | Complex, R&D-heavy products with evolving requirements | Higher management overhead |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Why V-Model is preferred for safety-critical embedded systems?"* — your answer must mention the **one-to-one traceability** between specification phases and testing phases, which simplifies **certification audits** under standards like **DO-178C** and **ISO 26262**.

### 3.3 Effort Distribution Curve

The classical effort distribution of an EDLC project follows the **40-20-40 rule** (rule of thumb, not a law):

$$E_{total} = E_{requirements} + E_{design} + E_{implementation} + E_{testing}$$

$$E_{requirements} : E_{design} : E_{implementation} : E_{testing} \approx 10 : 15 : 30 : 15 \; (\%)$$

$$\text{where} \quad E_{total} = 100\%$$

This is why **early-phase errors are the most expensive** to fix — a requirement defect found in Phase 8 may cost **100×** more to remediate than the same defect caught in Phase 2.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master EDLC Flowchart (Sequential View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["PHASE 1: CONCEPT AND NEED"] --> node2["PHASE 2: REQUIREMENTS ANALYSIS"]
    node2 --> node3["PHASE 3: SPECIFICATIONS"]
    node3 --> node4["PHASE 4: ARCHITECTURE AND HLD"]
    node4 --> node5["PHASE 5: DETAILED DESIGN AND LLD"]
    node5 --> node6["PHASE 6: IMPLEMENTATION AND CODING"]
    node6 --> node7["PHASE 7: TESTING AND VALIDATION"]
    node7 --> node8["PHASE 8: DEPLOYMENT MAINTENANCE EOL"]
    node8 --> node9["PRODUCT RETIRED OR UPGRADED"]
    node7 -.defects.-> node6
    node5 -.rework.-> node4
    node2 -.traceability.-> node7
    node3 -.traceability.-> node7
```

### 4.2 Hardware-Software Co-Design Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reqBlock["SRS DOCUMENT"] --> hwspec["HARDWARE SPEC"]
    reqBlock --> swspec["SOFTWARE SPEC"]
    hwspec --> hwarch["HW ARCHITECTURE"]
    swspec --> swarch["SW ARCHITECTURE"]
    hwarch --> hwimpl["SCHEMATIC AND PCB"]
    swarch --> swimpl["FIRMWARE CODING"]
    hwimpl --> integ["INTEGRATION"]
    swimpl --> integ
    integ --> systest["SYSTEM TEST"]
    systest --> cert["CERTIFICATION"]
    cert --> deploy["DEPLOYMENT"]
```

### 4.3 V-Model Mapping (Test-Phase ↔ Design-Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reqL["REQUIREMENTS"] -.paired with.-> accTest["ACCEPTANCE TEST"]
    sysL["SYSTEM DESIGN"] -.paired with.-> sysTest["SYSTEM TEST"]
    archL["ARCHITECTURE DESIGN"] -.paired with.-> intTest["INTEGRATION TEST"]
    moduleL["MODULE DESIGN"] -.paired with.-> unitTest["UNIT TEST"]
    reqL --> sysL
    sysL --> archL
    archL --> moduleL
    moduleL --> codingL["CODING"]
    codingL --> unitTest
    unitTest --> intTest
    intTest --> sysTest
    sysTest --> accTest
```

### 4.4 Spiral Model Iteration Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    spiral1["SPIRAL 1: CONCEPT"] --> spiral2["SPIRAL 2: PROTOTYPE V1"]
    spiral2 --> spiral3["SPIRAL 3: PROTOTYPE V2"]
    spiral3 --> spiral4["SPIRAL N: FINAL PRODUCT"]
    spiral1 -.risk analysis.-> spiral1
    spiral2 -.risk analysis.-> spiral2
    spiral3 -.risk analysis.-> spiral3
    spiral4 -.risk analysis.-> spiral4
```

### 4.5 Cross-Layer Traceability Matrix (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    c1["MARKET NEED"] --> c2["PRODUCT REQUIREMENT"]
    c2 --> c3["HARDWARE REQUIREMENT"]
    c2 --> c4["SOFTWARE REQUIREMENT"]
    c3 --> c5["HW TEST CASE"]
    c4 --> c6["SW TEST CASE"]
    c5 --> c7["VERIFICATION REPORT"]
    c6 --> c7
    c7 --> c8["CERTIFICATION AUDIT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams:** Each rectangle is a phase or deliverable. Solid arrows (`-->`) show the **forward flow** of work. Dotted arrows (`-.->`) show **feedback loops** (defects feeding back) or **traceability links** (a test case tracing back to a requirement). This dual view — forward flow + traceability — is what differentiates a *professional* EDLC from a *toy* one.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List and briefly explain any three phases of the Embedded Product Development Life Cycle (EDLC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Phase 1 — Requirements Phase:** The customer needs (often vague) are converted into measurable **Functional Requirements (FRs)** and **Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs)**. Output is the **SRS document**. *(1 mark)*

> **Phase 2 — Architecture / High-Level Design Phase:** Translates the SRS into a system-level **block diagram** describing hardware modules, software layers, and their interconnections. Decisions like processor selection and RTOS choice are made here. *(1 mark)*

> **Phase 3 — Testing Phase:** The implemented product is verified against the SRS. Includes **unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing**, plus **certification testing** (e.g., EMC, safety) for compliance with standards such as **ISO 26262** or **IEC 62304**. *(1 mark)*

*(Any 3 valid phases with brief explanations — full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between the Waterfall model and the Spiral model used in EDLC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Waterfall Model** | **Spiral Model** |
|:-----------|:--------------------|:-----------------|
| Structure | Linear, sequential | Iterative loops (spirals) |
| Risk Handling | Risk considered only at the start | Explicit **risk analysis** at every loop |
| Customer Feedback | Only at the end of the cycle | Incorporated at every iteration |
| Best Suited For | Simple, well-defined products | Complex, R&D-heavy products |
| Cost of Change | Very high (rework cascade) | Lower (changes absorbed in next spiral) |

*(1 mark for tabular comparison, 1 mark each for at least 2 contrasting points with reasoning.)*

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandate:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **eight phases of the Embedded Product Development Life Cycle (EDLC)**. For each phase, clearly state (i) the **objective**, (ii) the **key activities**, and (iii) the **primary deliverable**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain why the **V-Model** is preferred over the Waterfall model for developing **safety-critical embedded systems** (e.g., automotive airbags, pacemakers). Support your answer with a labelled diagram. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Eight Phases of EDLC — Detailed Description (7 marks):**

1. **Concept / Need Phase** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Identify *what* the product is and *why* it should exist.
   - **Activities:** Market study, competitor benchmarking, feasibility check (technical + economic).
   - **Deliverable:** Product Concept Document.

2. **Requirements Analysis Phase** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Convert the concept into testable engineering requirements.
   - **Activities:** Elicit FRs and NFRs, write SRS following **IEEE 830**.
   - **Deliverable:** **SRS Document**.

3. **Specifications Phase** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Translate the SRS into hardware and software engineering specifications.
   - **Activities:** Decide on MCU family, memory size, sensors, RTOS, communication protocols.
   - **Deliverable:** **HRS** + **Software Spec**.

4. **Architecture / HLD Phase** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Create the system blueprint.
   - **Activities:** Block diagrams, layered firmware architecture, hardware-software partitioning.
   - **Deliverable:** **High-Level Design Document**.

5. **Detailed Design / LLD Phase** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Decompose the HLD into implementable units.
   - **Activities:** Schematic capture, PCB layout, firmware module-level design, algorithm definition.
   - **Deliverable:** **LLD Document + Schematics + Netlist**.

6. **Implementation / Coding Phase** *(1 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Build the working prototype.
   - **Activities:** Firmware writing in C/C++, PCB fabrication, board bring-up, integration.
   - **Deliverable:** **Working Prototype + Firmware Binary**.

7. **Testing & Validation Phase** *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Objective:** Verify the product against the SRS and validate against customer needs.
   - **Activities:** Unit → integration → system → acceptance → certification (EMC, safety).
   - **Deliverable:** **Test Reports + Defect Logs + Certificates**.

8. **Deployment, Maintenance & EOL Phase** *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Objective:** Deliver to customer and support throughout product life.
   - **Activities:** Mass manufacturing, OTA infrastructure, bug fixes, security patches, EOL planning.
   - **Deliverable:** **Released Product + Maintenance Plan + EOL Notice**.

*(Marks awarded for completeness and clarity; the above structure covers all 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Why V-Model is Preferred for Safety-Critical Embedded Systems (7 marks):**

The **V-Model** extends the Waterfall model by *pairing every design phase with a corresponding test phase*, creating a V-shaped development and verification flow.

**Key reasons V-Model is preferred:**

1. **One-to-One Traceability** *(2 marks)*
   Every requirement in the SRS is traceable to a specific test case. For safety-critical systems, regulators (e.g., **FAA**, **FDA**, **TÜV**) require this *bidirectional traceability* as part of the certification audit. If a defect appears, the test case immediately points back to the requirement, and the design phase that derived from it.

2. **Early Test Planning = Early Defect Detection** *(2 marks)*
   Test cases are drafted *during* the design phase, not after. This forces designers to think about verification up front, surfacing ambiguities in specifications before code is written — critical when the cost of a field recall can be **millions of dollars and human lives**.

3. **Compliance with Safety Standards** *(2 marks)*
   Standards like **ISO 26262** (automotive), **DO-178C** (avionics), and **IEC 62304** (medical) explicitly mandate the V-Model's left-right verification structure. Auditors look for the **V** shape in your development artefacts.

4. **Labelled Diagram:** *(1 mark)*
   The diagram in **Section 4.3** above illustrates the V-Model pairing: Requirements ↔ Acceptance Test, System Design ↔ System Test, Architecture ↔ Integration Test, Module Design ↔ Unit Test.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a **labelled block diagram**, explain the **hardware-software co-design** approach in EDLC. Why is co-design essential for embedded systems but optional for desktop applications? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A startup wants to develop a **battery-powered wildlife-tracking collar** that must last 5 years on a single lithium battery, transmit GPS coordinates once per hour via LoRa, and operate in temperatures from −30 °C to +60 °C. **Map these customer requirements to the relevant EDLC phases** and explain how each phase would treat the requirement. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Hardware-Software Co-Design in EDLC (7 marks):**

**Definition:** *(1 mark)*
Hardware-Software Co-Design is the integrated, parallel design of the **hardware architecture** (processor, memory, I/O, power) and the **software architecture** (firmware, RTOS, drivers, application logic) starting from a **shared specification baseline**.

**Block Diagram Explanation:** *(3 marks)*
The block diagram in **Section 4.2** above is to be reproduced in the answer. Key elements to label:
- Common **SRS** feeding into both **HW Spec** and **SW Spec**
- Parallel work-streams converging at **Integration**
- **Test phase** validates the *combined* product, not just one side

**Why Co-Design is Essential for Embedded Systems but Optional for Desktop:** *(3 marks)*

| **Factor** | **Embedded System** | **Desktop Application** |
|:-----------|:--------------------|:------------------------|
| Hardware is part of the deliverable | **Yes** — must be co-designed | No — runs on pre-existing PC |
| Resource constraints (memory, power) | Severe (kB, mW) | Loose (GB, W) |
| Real-time deadlines | Hard/soft real-time | Best-effort only |
| Custom OS/RTOS | Often custom or RTOS | Generic OS (Windows/Linux) |
| Hardware dictates software | Strong coupling | Loose coupling |

Hence, for a desktop app, software can be designed in isolation. For an embedded system, the choice of MCU, memory size, and peripherals *fundamentally* shapes the software architecture — making co-design a necessity.

---

**(b) Mapping Wildlife-Tracking Collar Requirements to EDLC Phases (7 marks):**

| **Customer Requirement** | **EDLC Phase** | **How the Phase Treats It** |
|:-------------------------|:----------------|:----------------------------|
| *"Last 5 years on a single battery"* | Phase 2 — Requirements | Captured as **NFR-01: Battery life ≥ 5 years**, with a measurable current budget. *(1 mark)* |
| Same | Phase 3 — Specifications | Translated to: avg current ≤ **30 µA** assuming a **4 Ah** primary cell → drives the choice of a **sub-threshold ARM Cortex-M0+** MCU. *(1 mark)* |
| *"Transmit GPS once per hour via LoRa"* | Phase 2 — Requirements | **FR-01: GPS sample every 3600 s**, **FR-02: LoRaWAN transmission post-acquisition**. *(1 mark)* |
| Same | Phase 4 — Architecture | Three-state firmware state machine: **DEEP SLEEP → GPS ACQUIRE → LORA TX → DEEP SLEEP**. *(0.5 mark)* |
| *"Operate from −30 °C to +60 °C"* | Phase 2 — Requirements | **NFR-02: Operating temperature range −30 °C to +60 °C**. *(0.5 mark)* |
| Same | Phase 3 — Specifications | Selects **industrial-grade components** (e.g., MCU rated to +85 °C), low-temperature Li-SOCl₂ chemistry battery, and conformal coating for humidity. *(1 mark)* |
| All requirements | Phase 7 — Testing | Validates each NFR: thermal chamber test, accelerated battery-life test (e.g., **Arrhenius modelling** to predict 5-year life from 90-day data), LoRa range test, drop/vibration test (IP67 enclosure). *(2 marks)* |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating EDLC with generic SDLC:** Saying *"EDLC has phases like planning, coding, testing"* without naming embedded-specific deliverables (SRS, HRS, LLD, certification) loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Skipping the Specification/Architecture distinction:** Merging Phase 3 and Phase 4 into a single *"design"* answer is incomplete. The board expects explicit separation.
> 3. **Forgetting the EOL phase:** Many students stop at "deployment." The 8th phase (maintenance + EOL + RoHS/WEEE compliance) carries **1–2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 4. **No diagram = −1 to −2 marks:** KTU 2024 explicitly rewards diagrams. A 14-mark question on EDLC *without* at least one labelled block diagram is almost guaranteed to lose marks.
> 5. **Ignoring real-world numbers:** Stating *"low power MCU"* without mentioning a specific class (e.g., *sub-threshold ARM Cortex-M0+*) or a specific current target (e.g., *< 30 µA average*) is treated as vague and incurs mark deductions.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **EDLC = 8 phases:** Concept → Requirements → Specifications → Architecture/HLD → Detailed Design/LLD → Implementation → Testing → Deployment/Maintenance/EOL.
- ✅ **SRS (IEEE 830)** is the *contractual anchor* of the entire cycle — every test case must trace back to it.
- ✅ **Specifications ≠ Design:** Specs define the *numbers to hit*; design defines *how to hit them*.
- ✅ **Hardware-Software Co-Design** is the *defining feature* of EDLC — absent in generic SDLC.
- ✅ **Three common process models:** Waterfall (simple), V-Model (safety-critical), Spiral (R&D-heavy).
- ✅ **V-Model pairs** each design phase with a matching test phase → enables **traceability** and **certification compliance**.
- ✅ **Testing is multi-layered:** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance → Certification (EMC, safety).
- ✅ **Standards mentioned in syllabus:** ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical), DO-178C (avionics), RoHS/WEEE (EOL).
- ✅ **Effort distribution (40-20-40 rule):** ~10% Requirements, ~15% Design, ~30% Implementation, ~15% Testing, ~5% Concept, ~5% Deployment.
- ✅ **Cost of defect** rises **exponentially** the later it is found — Phase 1 fix costs 1×, Phase 8 fix costs 100×.
- ✅ **Key deliverables to memorise:** SRS, HRS, HLD, LLD, Schematics, Firmware Binary, Test Reports, Certificates, EOL Notice.
- ✅ **Real-world numbers to remember:** STM32L0 @ 32 MHz, ARM Cortex-M0+, 220 mAh CR2032, 3 µA average current, −40 °C to +85 °C industrial range, **6–18 months TTM**.
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled block diagram** when asked about EDLC phases or co-design — KTU awards visible marks for figures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTOS based Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT637 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND ITS APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RTOS based Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS):** An operating system designed to serve real-time applications that process data as it comes in, typically without buffering delays. A *real-time system* is one where the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical results of computation, but **also on the time at which the results are produced**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECAT637), an **RTOS-based design** is the methodology of structuring an embedded application as a collection of concurrent **tasks (threads)** managed by a deterministic, priority-driven **real-time kernel** that guarantees bounded response times to external events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification of Real-Time Systems:**
> - **Hard Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline is a **total system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, anti-lock braking).
> - **Soft Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline degrades performance but is **tolerable** (e.g., video streaming, audio playback).
> - **Firm Real-Time System:** Late results are useless, but a few missed deadlines do not cause catastrophe.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Emergency Department"

Imagine a busy **hospital emergency room** as an RTOS:

| Hospital Concept | RTOS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patients arriving with injuries | External interrupts / events |
| Triage nurse assigning urgency | **Scheduler** assigning priorities |
| Doctors (Cardiologist, GP, Surgeon) | Independent **tasks/threads** |
| Operating theatre (single, shared) | **CPU** (single execution resource) |
| ICU patient list on the board | **Ready queue** |
| A patient in surgery being monitored | **Blocked/suspended task** waiting for I/O |
| Emergency call interrupting routine work | **Nested interrupt** preempting current task |
| A "Code Blue" override | **Priority preemption** |

Just as the ER cannot afford to let a heart-attack patient wait behind a patient needing a routine bandage, an RTOS must guarantee that the highest-priority ready task gets the CPU within a **deterministic worst-case bound**.

### 1.3 Key Determinism Metrics (Engineering Constants)

- **Interrupt Latency:** Time from interrupt occurrence to first instruction of ISR. Typical: **1–10 μs**.
- **Context Switch Time:** Time to save current task state and load the next. Typical: **5–30 μs**.
- **Jitter:** Variation in task release-to-execution delay. Goal: **minimised** in hard real-time.
- **Tick / System Tick (Tick Rate):** Heartbeat of the kernel, configured between **1 kHz (1 ms) and 10 kHz (100 μs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Choices in an RTOS:**
> 1. **Monolithic Kernel** (e.g., μC/OS-II, FreeRTOS) – kernel services run in supervisor mode only; small footprint (~few KB).
> 2. **Microkernel** (e.g., QNX, VxWorks) – minimal kernel; IPC-based services; higher reliability.
> 3. **Exokernel** – research-oriented; delegates resource management to applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an RTOS Kernel

The **real-time kernel** is the minimal software that provides task scheduling, inter-task communication, and synchronisation. KTU examiners frequently ask students to enumerate these primitives.

#### 2.1.1 Task Management Subsystem
- A **task** = independent thread of execution with its own stack.
- Each task has a **Task Control Block (TCB)** storing: stack pointer, program counter, task state, priority, registers.
- **Task States:** `Ready`, `Running`, `Blocked` (waiting on event/ISR/semaphore), `Suspended` (deliberately dormant), `Deleted`.

#### 2.1.2 Scheduler
The scheduler is invoked whenever a task **yield, blocks, unblocks, or is preempted by a higher-priority task**.

**Scheduling Classes Used in RTOS:**

| Class | Type | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| Rate Monotonic (RM) | Fixed priority, preemptive | Hard real-time, periodic tasks |
| Deadline Monotonic (DM) | Fixed priority, deadline-ordered | Tasks with deadlines < period |
| Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Dynamic priority | Soft real-time, multiprocessor |
| Least Laxity First (LLF) | Dynamic, laxity-ordered | Theoretical optimum dynamic algo |
| Round Robin | Time-sliced, equal priority | Best-effort (rare in pure RTOS) |
| Cooperative (FIFO) | Non-preemptive | Simple MCUs (e.g., Arduino) |

#### 2.1.3 Inter-Process Communication (IPC)
- **Semaphores:** Binary (mutex) and Counting. Used for resource access and event signalling.
- **Mutexes (Mutual Exclusion):** Priority Inheritance Mutex (PIM) prevents priority inversion.
- **Message Queues:** Fixed-size message passing between tasks.
- **Mailboxes:** Single-slot message exchange.
- **Event Flags / Groups:** Bit-mask based synchronisation.
- **Pipes / Stream Buffers:** Byte-stream communication.

#### 2.1.4 Memory Management
- **Static Partitioning:** Fixed blocks at compile time — deterministic.
- **Memory Pools (Object Pools):** Pre-allocated fixed-size blocks — bounded fragmentation.
- **Stack Monitoring:** High-water mark tracking per task.
- **No virtual memory / paging** in most RTOSs (non-deterministic).

#### 2.1.5 Interrupt Handling
- **ISR–Task Communication:** `PendISR()` / `PostSem()` mechanism.
- **Deferred Procedure Call (DPC):** Long ISR work split into a high-priority task.
- **Critical Sections:** `taskENTER_CRITICAL()` / `taskEXIT_CRITICAL()`.

### 2.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Liu & Layland (1973)

RMS is the **gold-standard fixed-priority scheduling algorithm** that KTU expects you to master.

**Assumptions (Liu & Layland Model):**
1. All tasks are **periodic**.
2. Tasks are **independent** (no shared resources / no precedence).
3. Deadline of each task equals its **period** (Dᵢ = Tᵢ).
4. Worst-case execution time Cᵢ is **known and constant**.
5. Tasks are **preemptable** and have **fixed priorities**.
6. **CPU utilisation** is bounded.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU utilisation by n periodic tasks | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $U_{RMS} \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu & Layland **sufficient** schedulability bound | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{RMS} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Asymptotic upper bound for large n | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | **Worst-case response time** (iterative, starting R₀ = Cᵢ) | time units |
| 5 | $L_i = D_i - R_i$ | Laxity (slack) of task i — must be $\ge 0$ | time units |
| 6 | $P_i = \frac{1}{T_i}$ | Task i **rate** (used in RM priority assignment) | Hz |
| 7 | $f_{tick} = 1/\Delta t$ | RTOS tick frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $T_{response} = T_{latency} + T_{context\_switch}$ | End-to-end interrupt-to-task response | time |
| 9 | $T_{bcet} \le C_i \le T_{wcet}$ | Best-case and worst-case execution times bound Cᵢ | time |
| 10 | Priority: $T_a < T_b \Rightarrow P_a > P_b$ | Shorter period → Higher priority (RM rule) | ordinal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "SHORT period = HIGH priority"** for Rate Monotonic. Period is the scheduling quantum — the faster the task, the higher the priority.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive ECU (Engine Control):** Crankshaft-position task at 10 ms (high priority); dashboard update at 100 ms (low priority).
- **Medical Ventilator:** Pressure-sensor sampling at 5 ms; LCD display at 200 ms.
- **Drone Flight Controller:** IMU fusion at 1 ms; GPS at 100 ms; telemetry at 1 s.
- **Industrial PLC:** 16 digital inputs polled at 1 ms; HMI refresh at 250 ms.
- **Mobile Modem Baseband:** L1 layer at 1 ms sub-frame boundary; MAC layer at 10 ms.

### 2.5 The Priority Inversion Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion** occurs when a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task — effectively "inverting" the relative priorities. The classic example is the **Mars Pathfinder** (1997) anomaly.

**Variants:**
- **Bounded Priority Inversion:** L holds R, H preempts, H blocks on R; L resumes. Time bounded by L's completion of R. **Acceptable.**
- **Unbounded Priority Inversion:** M (medium priority) preempts L while L holds R. H waits indefinitely. **Unacceptable.**

**Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**
When task H blocks on a resource held by L, **L inherits H's priority** until it releases the resource. This bounds the inversion to one critical section of any lower-priority task.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Liu & Layland Schedulability Bound for RMS

We want to find the maximum total CPU utilisation $U_{max}(n)$ such that $n$ tasks are *guaranteed* schedulable by Rate Monotonic Scheduling under the assumption $D_i = T_i$.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Critical Instant**

A **critical instant** for task $i$ is the moment when (a) $i$ is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks, and (b) all higher-priority tasks are released as soon as possible afterwards. At a critical instant, the response time of $i$ is maximised.

**Step 2 — Worst-Case Workload in [0, t]**

The total processor demand in the interval $[0, t]$ is the demand of task $i$ itself plus preemption work from all higher-priority tasks $j \in hp(i)$:

$$W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 3 — Schedulability Condition**

Task $i$ is schedulable iff there exists some $t \in [0, T_i]$ satisfying $W_i(t) \le t$. Equivalently, the response time $R_i \le D_i = T_i$.

**Step 4 — Liu & Layland Upper Bound**

For a *sufficient* (but not necessary) condition, Liu & Layland proved the bound:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

**Derivation Outline:**
- The worst-case utilisation is achieved when tasks' periods are in harmonic relation: $T_j = T_n / 2^{n-j}$.
- For two tasks with periods $T_1$ and $T_2 = 2 T_1$ and $D_1 = D_2 = T_1$, the worst-case schedulable utilisation equals $\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} \to 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.828$.
- Generalising to $n$ tasks in harmonic series gives the bound $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- Taking the limit: $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**

Consider 3 tasks:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | Priority (by RM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| τ₁ | 1 | 4 | Highest |
| τ₂ | 2 | 6 | Middle |
| τ₃ | 3 | 10 | Lowest |

Total utilisation:

$$U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883$$

Bound for $n = 3$:

$$U_{RMS} = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$$

Since $0.883 > 0.7798$, the **Liu & Layland sufficient test FAILS**. However, the system *may still be schedulable*; we must apply the **exact response-time test**.

**Step 6 — Exact Response-Time Test for τ₃**

Initialise $R^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$ ms. Iterate:

$$R^{(k+1)} = C_3 + \sum_{j=1}^{2} \left\lceil \frac{R^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

- $R^{(0)} = 3$
- $R^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$
- $R^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 6/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$
- $R^{(3)} = 3 + \lceil 7/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 7/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$
- $R^{(4)} = 3 + \lceil 9/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$

The sequence **diverges** (it would keep growing). Conclusion: the exact response time of τ₃ exceeds $T_3 = 10$ ms → **the task set is NOT schedulable** under RMS.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Context Switch Time Budget

For a system with $N$ tasks and a tick of period $T_{tick}$, the **overhead** consumed by the scheduler is:

$$T_{overhead} = N \cdot T_{context\_switch} \cdot \frac{T_{hyperperiod}}{T_{tick}}$$

where the **hyperperiod** is the LCM of all task periods:

$$T_{hyperperiod} = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$$

**Why it matters:** If the overhead is $> 5\%$ of the hyperperiod, the design is considered **over-engineered** and tick frequency or context-switch implementation should be revisited.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Synchronisation Primitive

| Problem | Recommended Primitive | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Two tasks sharing a UART | **Mutex (with PI)** | Single shared resource, short critical section |
| Producer–Consumer buffer | **Counting Semaphore** | Slot-count based flow control |
| ISR → Task notification | **Binary Semaphore** (`xSemaphoreGiveFromISR`) | Lightweight, no ownership |
| Multi-task fan-in synchronisation | **Event Flags Group** | AND/OR of multiple conditions |
| Streaming byte data | **Stream Buffer / Pipe** | Variable-length messages |
| 1-to-N dispatch | **Message Queue** | Multiple consumers, fixed-size messages |

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Minimum Stack Size Calculation

A common KTU question asks for **stack sizing per task**:

$$S_{stack} = S_{ISR} + S_{local} + S_{nested} + S_{safety}$$

where:
- $S_{ISR}$ = worst-case ISR stack usage (deepest nesting),
- $S_{local}$ = sum of all local variable sizes in the task function,
