- $S_{nested}$ = stack used by called functions,
- $S_{safety}$ = 25–50% safety margin.

FreeRTOS provides `uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark()` to measure the **high-water mark** at runtime.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RTOS Kernel Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HW["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        CPU["CPU Core"]
        MEM["ROM / RAM / Flash"]
        IO["I/O Peripherals"]
        TIM["Hardware Timers"]
    end

    subgraph KERN["REAL-TIME KERNEL"]
        SCHED["Scheduler\n(RM / EDF)"]
        DISP["Dispatcher\n(Context Switch)"]
        IPC["IPC Primitives\nSemaphores, Mutex,\nMsg Queues, Events"]
        MEMM["Memory Manager\n(Pools / Static)"]
        TMR["Tick Timer / SW Timers"]
    end

    subgraph TASKS["APPLICATION TASKS"]
        T1["Task1\nHigh Priority\nSensor Read"]
        T2["Task2\nMid Priority\nControl Loop"]
        T3["Task3\nLow Priority\nLogging"]
        ISR["ISR Routines\n(HW Interrupt)"]
    end

    CPU --> SCHED
    TIM --> TMR
    TMR --> SCHED
    SCHED --> DISP
    DISP --> T1
    DISP --> T2
    DISP --> T3
    DISP --> ISR
    T1 -.uses.-> IPC
    T2 -.uses.-> IPC
    T3 -.uses.-> IPC
    T1 -.alloc.-> MEMM
    T2 -.alloc.-> MEMM
    T3 -.alloc.-> MEMM
    MEM --> MEMM
    IO --> ISR
```

### 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Ready: TaskCreate
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler selects
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by higher priority
    Running --> Blocked: Wait for event / semaphore / delay
    Blocked --> Ready: Event occurs / timeout
    Running --> Suspended: Suspend
    Suspended --> Ready: Resume
    Running --> [*]: TaskDelete
```

### 4.3 RMS Scheduling — Critical Instant Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic Schedule at Critical Instant
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section High Priority
    Task1 period1    :a1, 0, 2
    Task1 period2    :a2, 4, 2
    Task1 period3    :a3, 8, 2

    section Mid Priority
    Task2 period1    :b1, 2, 3
    Task2 period2    :b2, 7, 3

    section Low Priority
    Task3 period1    :c1, 5, 4
```

### 4.4 Priority Inversion — With and Without Inheritance

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as Task H (High Prio)
    participant M as Task M (Mid Prio)
    participant L as Task L (Low Prio)
    participant R as Shared Resource

    Note over L: Acquires R
    L->>R: Lock
    Note over H: Preempts L
    H->>R: Lock (BLOCKS)
    Note over M: Preempts L (no PI)
    M-->>M: Runs to completion
    Note over L: Resumes, finishes R
    L->>R: Unlock
    H->>R: Lock (succeeds)
    Note over H,M,L: UNBOUNDED INVERSION

    Note right of H: --- WITH Priority Inheritance ---
    L->>R: Lock
    H->>R: Lock (BLOCKS, L inherits P_H)
    Note over M: L still runs at P_H, M cannot preempt
    L->>R: Unlock (back to P_L)
    L-->>H: Now H runs immediately
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between hard real-time and soft real-time systems. Give two examples of each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- A **hard real-time system** has deadlines whose **miss leads to catastrophic failure** (total system failure). The response time must be guaranteed deterministically. *Examples:* Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), aircraft flight control, pacemaker.
- A **soft real-time system** tolerates **occasional deadline misses** with only a **degradation in quality of service**. *Examples:* Video streaming, online gaming, audio playback.
- *Key difference:* Hard real-time requires **worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis** and **schedulability proofs**; soft real-time accepts **average-case performance** optimisation.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is priority inversion? How does the Priority Inheritance Protocol solve it?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Priority inversion** is a scheduling anomaly where a **higher-priority task is forced to wait** for the execution of a lower-priority task, often because of a shared resource or medium-priority preemption.
- The classic **unbounded priority inversion** scenario: Task L holds resource R; Task H blocks on R; Task M preempts L → H is forced to wait for *both* L and M, even though H has higher priority.
- **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When H blocks on resource R held by L, **L temporarily inherits H's priority**. M can no longer preempt L. L runs to completion of its critical section, releases R, and reverts to its original priority. **H then runs immediately.** The inversion is bounded to one critical section of any lower-priority task.
- *Real-world example:* The Mars Pathfinder (1997) lander suffered repeated system resets due to unbounded priority inversion; resolved by enabling priority inheritance in the VxWorks RTOS.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Real-Time Operating System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **scheduler, dispatcher, and IPC primitives** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

An **RTOS architecture** is organised in hierarchical layers with the **hardware** at the bottom, the **real-time kernel** in the middle, and the **application tasks** at the top.

**1. Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Provides portable interfaces to CPU, timers, and I/O peripherals; isolates the kernel from hardware specifics.

**2. Real-Time Kernel:** The minimal core that provides:
- **Scheduler** — Selects the next task to run. Uses a priority queue (e.g., bitmap-based for O(1) selection in μC/OS). Types: **Rate Monotonic, EDF, Round-Robin.**
- **Dispatcher** — Performs the **context switch**: saves current task's registers, stack pointer, and PC into its TCB; loads the next task's context; restores registers; jumps to its PC.
- **Tick Timer** — Generates a periodic interrupt (typically 1–10 kHz) used for time-slicing, delays, and timeouts.
- **IPC Primitives:**
  - *Semaphores* (binary/counting) — signalling and resource counting.
  - *Mutexes with Priority Inheritance* — exclusive resource access.
  - *Message Queues* — fixed-size message passing.
  - *Event Flags* — bit-mask synchronisation.
  - *Mailboxes* — single-message exchange.
- **Memory Manager** — Pre-allocated pools, no paging; deterministic allocation.

**3. Application Layer:** A set of independent **tasks (threads)**, each with its own TCB and stack. Tasks interact *only* through kernel IPC — never via shared global variables without protection.

**Significance:** This architecture guarantees **determinism** (bounded response), **modularity** (independent task development), and **predictability** (WCET analysis per task).

**(b)** Three periodic tasks are scheduled using Rate Monotonic Scheduling with the following parameters:

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) | Period $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| τ₁ | 2 | 6 |
| τ₂ | 3 | 12 |
| τ₃ | 4 | 24 |

Apply (i) the **Liu & Layland sufficient test** and (ii) the **exact response-time analysis** to determine if the task set is schedulable. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Priority Assignment (RM rule: shorter period → higher priority):**
- τ₁: T = 6 ms → Highest
- τ₂: T = 12 ms → Middle
- τ₃: T = 24 ms → Lowest

**Step 2 — Liu & Layland Sufficient Test:**

$$U = \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{12} + \frac{4}{24} = 0.333 + 0.250 + 0.167 = 0.750$$

Bound for $n = 3$:

$$U_{RMS} = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

Since $0.750 \le 0.7798$ → **Task set PASSES the sufficient test.** Schedulable.

**Step 3 — Exact Response-Time Analysis (verification):**

For τ₁: $R_1 = C_1 = 2$ ms. Since $2 \le T_1 = 6$ → **schedulable**.

For τ₂: Iterate $R_2 = C_2 + \lceil R_2/T_1 \rceil \cdot C_1$.

- $R_2^{(0)} = 3$
- $R_2^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 3/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 = 5$
- $R_2^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 5/6 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 = 5$ (converged)

$R_2 = 5 \le T_2 = 12$ → **schedulable**.

For τ₃: Iterate $R_3 = C_3 + \sum_{j=1}^{2} \lceil R_3/T_j \rceil C_j$.

- $R_3^{(0)} = 4$
- $R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \lceil 4/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 4/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9$
- $R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \lceil 9/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 9/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 4 + 3 = 11$
- $R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \lceil 11/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 11/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 4 + 3 = 11$ (converged)

$R_3 = 11 \le T_3 = 24$ → **schedulable**.

**Conclusion:** Task set is **fully schedulable** under RMS. Both tests confirm schedulability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** What is an **RTOS**? Compare **preemptive** and **cooperative** scheduling. List the features of a commercial RTOS (e.g., VxWorks, FreeRTOS, μC/OS-II). *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An RTOS is an operating system whose correctness depends on both the **logical result** and the **time at which** the result is delivered. It provides deterministic, priority-based task management, bounded interrupt latency, and predictable inter-task communication.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Preemptive Scheduling | Cooperative Scheduling |
|---|---|---|
| **Task switch trigger** | Kernel preempts running task | Task voluntarily calls `yield()` / `delay()` |
| **Response to high-prio event** | Immediate (bounded) | Delayed until current task yields |
| **Determinism** | High (hard real-time suitable) | Low (soft real-time only) |
| **Kernel complexity** | Higher (port, TCB save/restore) | Very low (no port needed) |
| **Stack usage per task** | Independent stack per task | Single shared stack possible |
| **Example RTOS** | VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, μC/OS-II | TinyOS, simple Arduino sketches |
| **Risk** | Priority inversion (needs PI mutex) | One stuck task blocks entire system |

**Features of a Modern RTOS (using μC/OS-II / FreeRTOS as template):**
1. **Preemptive priority-based scheduler** with up to 256 priority levels.
2. **Task Management** — create, delete, suspend, resume, query.
3. **Semaphores** (binary, counting), **Mutexes with priority inheritance**, **Event Flags**, **Message Mailboxes**, **Message Queues**, **Mutexes**.
4. **Memory Management** — fixed-size block allocation.
5. **Time Management** — `OSTimeDly()`, software timers.
6. **Interrupt Management** — `OSIntEnter()` / `OSIntExit()`; ISR-to-task signalling.
7. **Portability** — written mostly in ANSI C; small assembly for context switch.
8. **Determinism** — All kernel calls have O(1) or bounded execution time.
9. **Footprint** — Kernel as small as 4–10 KB ROM.
10. **Certification** — VxWorks, QNX are DO-178C / IEC 61508 certifiable for safety-critical systems.

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how a **Priority Inheritance Mutex (PIM)** avoids unbounded priority inversion. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**The Unbounded Inversion Scenario (without PI):**

1. Task L (priority 1) acquires mutex M and enters critical section.
2. Task H (priority 10) becomes ready and preempts L; H tries to lock M and **blocks**.
3. Task M (priority 5) becomes ready and preempts L (since L is the only running task and has lower priority). M runs to completion.
4. H is still blocked — even though H has the highest priority, it is **delayed by both L and M**.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIM) Solution:**

1. Task L (priority 1) acquires mutex M.
2. Task H (priority 10) tries to lock M and **blocks**.
3. **Kernel detects the blocking**: L's priority is **temporarily raised** to 10 (H's priority). L is said to "inherit" H's priority.
4. Task M (priority 5) tries to preempt — but L now has priority 10, so **M cannot preempt L**.
5. L continues its critical section (now at priority 10), finishes, releases M.
6. L's priority reverts to 1; H immediately acquires M and runs to completion.

**Result:** The inversion is bounded to **one critical section of the lowest-priority task** that holds the resource — *not* to an arbitrary medium-priority task.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
Without PI:   L(low) --lock M-- H(high) blocks -- M(mid) preempts L -- M runs to end -- L finishes -- H runs
With PI:      L(low) --lock M-- H(high) blocks -- L inherits H prio -- L finishes CS -- H acquires M -- H runs
```

**Real-world case study:** The NASA Mars Pathfinder spacecraft (1997) experienced unplanned system resets caused by unbounded priority inversion between the bus-management task, the meteorological task, and the communication task. **Switching on priority inheritance** in the VxWorks kernel fixed the issue.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the sufficient test with the exact test.** A FAIL on Liu & Layland's bound does NOT mean the task set is unschedulable — it only means the *sufficient* test is inconclusive. Always follow up with **exact response-time analysis** (RTA) for full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the RTA iteration; writing $\frac{R}{T}$ instead of $\lceil R/T \rceil$ is a common error and costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mis-stating priority assignment for RM:** Priority is inversely proportional to period — *shorter* period ⇒ *higher* priority. Reversing this loses marks.
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram** in the architecture question — examiners award up to **2 marks** for a labelled diagram.
> 5. **Omitting units** in formula calculations (e.g., "R = 11" without "ms") — KTU valuation strictly penalises missing units.
> 6. **Forgetting Priority Inheritance** when discussing mutexes; simply stating "use mutex" is incomplete; you must specify **Priority Inheritance Mutex (PIM)**.
> 7. **Conflating deadline and period:** RMS assumes $D_i = T_i$; if not, the algorithm becomes **Deadline Monotonic**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTOS-Based Design — Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Prep)**

- [ ] **RTOS Definition:** OS where correctness depends on **logical result + timing**.
- [ ] **Hard vs Soft vs Firm** real-time — know the difference and examples.
- [ ] **Kernel Primitives:** Scheduler, Dispatcher, IPC (semaphores, mutex, queues, events, mailboxes), Memory Manager, Tick Timer.
- [ ] **Task States:** Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended, Dormant.
- [ ] **TCB** holds stack pointer, PC, registers, priority, state.
- [ ] **Preemptive vs Cooperative** scheduling — preemptive = hard RT; cooperative = soft RT.
- [ ] **Rate Monotonic (RM):** Shorter period → Higher priority; **static** priority.
- [ ] **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- [ ] **Exact Response-Time Analysis:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$, iterate until convergence.
- [ ] **Schedulability:** $R_i \le D_i$ (for RM, $D_i = T_i$).
- [ ] **Hyperperiod:** $\text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$ — repeat interval of schedule.
- [ ] **Priority Inversion:** Unbounded when medium-priority task preempts; bounded when low-priority holds resource.
- [ ] **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** Blocking task inherits blocker's priority; bounds inversion to one critical section.
- [ ] **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):** Resource assigned the priority of the highest-priority task that may lock it — **prevents deadlocks**.
- [ ] **ISR Conventions:** Keep ISR short; use `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR()` / `PendISR` to defer work to a task.
- [ ] **Critical Sections:** `taskENTER_CRITICAL()` / `taskEXIT_CRITICAL()` — disables context switch but NOT nested interrupts.
- [ ] **Stack Sizing:** $S_{stack} = S_{ISR} + S_{local} + S_{nested} + S_{safety}$.
- [ ] **Memory:** Use **static allocation** or **memory pools**; avoid `malloc` in hard RTOS.
- [ ] **Tick Rate:** 1 kHz typical; overhead must stay < 5% of CPU.
- [ ] **Deadlock Conditions (Coffman):** Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. PC mutex violates (3).
- [ ] **Context Switch Time** = save state + restore state; budget for $N$ tasks per tick.
- [ ] **Commercial RTOS examples:** FreeRTOS (open-source, ~10 KB), VxWorks (certifiable), QNX (microkernel), μC/OS-II (textbook-friendly), RTX (ARM CMSIS), ThreadX.
- [ ] **Watchdog Timer:** Last-resort recovery from task deadlock in RTOS designs.
- [ ] **POSIX Compliance:** Some RTOSs expose `pthread` API for portability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Operating System Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT637 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND ITS APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Operating System Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an **Embedded Operating System (EOS)** or a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**, *Basic Operating System Services* refers to the fundamental, kernel-level functionalities and abstractions that the OS provides to the application software and hardware. These services decouple the application programmer from the low-level hardware complexities, enabling deterministic and efficient execution of embedded tasks.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECAT637, the core OS services in an embedded environment are classified into the following major functional blocks:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> An embedded OS provides core services such as **Task (Process) Management**, **Memory Management**, **I/O Device Management**, **Interrupt Handling**, **Timer Management**, and **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** to abstract hardware resources from the embedded application layer.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a highly efficient **Specialty Restaurant** that operates on a tight 15-minute ticket time:

- **The Kitchen (OS Kernel)** is the central authority.
- **The Customers (Tasks)** place orders (system calls).
- **The Head Chef (Scheduler)** decides which dish to prepare first based on priority and timing.
- **The Pantry (Memory Manager)** keeps ingredients (data) on specific shelves and retrieves them on demand.
- **The Waiter (Interrupt Handler)** stops the chef immediately when a VIP order comes in.
- **The Kitchen Timer (Timer Manager)** ensures the steak is cooked exactly for 4 minutes, not 5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU Exams:** Embedded systems often operate under **hard real-time constraints**. Missing a deadline (e.g., in an anti-lock braking system) is a system failure. The OS services ensure that the *Head Chef* never loses track of time, ingredients, or order priorities.

### 1.3 Core Operating System Services in Embedded Systems

| Service Category | Function | Embedded-Specific Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Task Management** | Creation, scheduling, and termination of tasks | Multiplexes the single CPU core across multiple control loops |
| **Memory Management** | Allocation, deallocation, and protection of RAM | Often uses static allocation in safety-critical systems |
| **Interrupt Management** | Handling asynchronous hardware events | Ensures deterministic response to external stimuli |
| **Timer Management** | System tick, delays, and timeouts | Heartbeat of the RTOS scheduler |
| **I/O Subsystem** | Abstracting peripherals (UART, SPI, I2C) | Provides uniform API for sensor/actuator access |
| **IPC Services** | Queues, semaphores, mailboxes, mutexes | Synchronizes concurrent tasks without race conditions |
| **Error & Fault Handling** | Watchdog, stack overflow detection | Critical for long-running embedded nodes (IoT) |
| **File System (Optional)** | FAT, LittleFS, JFFS2 | Used when persistent storage (Flash/SD) is present |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Hint:** A common 3-mark KTU question asks to *"List any six basic services provided by an embedded OS."* Memorizing the table above in a structured, hierarchical format typically earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Each OS Service

#### A. Task (Process) Management
- **Why it exists:** Embedded MCUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) have only **1 physical CPU core**, but must seemingly run motor control, sensor polling, and UI updates *simultaneously*.
- **How it works:** The OS maintains a **Task Control Block (TCB)** for each task, containing:
  - Program Counter (PC) saved state
  - Stack Pointer (SP)
  - Task priority (0-255 in typical RTOS)
  - Task state (Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended)
- **Service APIs:** `osTaskCreate()`, `osTaskDelete()`, `osTaskDelay()`.

#### B. Memory Management
- **Why it exists:** Prevents one faulty task from corrupting the memory used by another (memory protection).
- **How it works:** Two approaches:
  1. **Static Partitioning:** Memory regions fixed at compile time (used in AUTOSAR, safety-critical).
  2. **Dynamic Allocation:** Heap-based `malloc`/`free` (used in Linux-based embedded systems).
- **Key Equation:** Total available RAM must satisfy:
$$M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (S_{stack,i} + S_{heap,i}) + S_{kernel}$$

#### C. Interrupt Management
- **Why it exists:** Sensors and communication peripherals need immediate attention, not waiting for the next scheduler tick.
- **How it works:** Hardware triggers an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. The kernel saves context, executes the ISR, and triggers a context switch.
- **Latency Equation:** The maximum time from interrupt assert to first ISR instruction:
$$t_{latency} = t_{pipeline\_flush} + t_{context\_save}$$

#### D. Timer Management
- **Why it exists:** Provides the "heartbeat" of the OS and enables time-based services.
- **How it works:** A **SysTick** timer (e.g., on ARM Cortex-M) generates periodic interrupts every 1 ms or 10 µs. The OS uses this tick to:
  - Wake up blocked tasks
  - Decrement software timers
  - Trigger timeouts

#### E. Inter-Process Communication (IPC)
- **Why it exists:** Tasks must exchange data (e.g., sensor reading → controller task).
- **How it works:** Three primary primitives:
  - **Semaphores:** Signaling between tasks (binary, counting, mutex).
  - **Message Queues:** Fixed-size data packet passing (FIFO).
  - **Mailboxes:** Pointer-based variable message passing.

#### F. I/O Device Management
- **Why it exists:** Writing low-level register code for every sensor is unproductive.
- **How it works:** Provides a **Device Driver Layer** that exposes uniform functions like `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `ioctl()`.

#### G. Error & Fault Management
- **Why it exists:** Embedded systems often run unattended for years (e.g., remote weather stations).
- **How it works:** Uses a **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** — an independent hardware counter that resets the MCU if not "kicked" by software within a timeout window.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations and metrics are **frequently tested** in KTU numerical/analytical questions for the Embedded Systems module.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total RAM Requirement** | $M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (S_{stack,i} + S_{heap,i}) + S_{kernel}$ | Bytes | Sum of all task stacks + heap + kernel footprint |
| **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Dimensionless (0-1) | Liu & Layland utilization bound test |
| **Liu & Layland Bound (n tasks)** | $U_{bound} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Dimensionless | Maximum schedulable utilization for Rate Monotonic |
| **Interrupt Latency** | $t_{latency} = t_{flush} + t_{context\_save}$ | Microseconds (µs) | Worst-case time from IRQ assert to ISR entry |
| **Context Switch Time** | $t_{cs} = 2 \cdot t_{save} + t_{load} + t_{schedule}$ | Microseconds (µs) | Time to swap between two tasks |
| **Tick Period** | $T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{tick}}$ | Seconds | Inverse of SysTick frequency |
| **Watchdog Timeout** | $T_{WDT} = \frac{C_{prescaler} \cdot C_{reload}}{f_{clk}}$ | Seconds | Hardware WDT reset window |
| **Priority Inversion (PIP)** | $t_{block} = \frac{R_{resource}}{C_{low\_prio}} \cdot T_{high\_prio}$ | Time units | Worst-case blocking for Priority Inheritance Protocol |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Restriction:** When transcribing these formulas in your answer booklet, **never use the vertical bar `|` symbol** (e.g., for absolute value). Use the notation `mod(x)` or `abs(x)` or write it as a fraction. This prevents markdown rendering issues in digital KTU scripts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR OS):** Uses static memory partitioning and OSEK OS services for ECU isolation.
- **Aerospace (VxWorks, RTEMS):** Memory protection and priority inheritance protocols prevent catastrophic software faults.
- **IoT Edge Devices (Zephyr, FreeRTOS):** Lightweight memory management and IPC enable 10+ year battery life on sensor nodes.
- **Consumer Electronics (Embedded Linux in Smart TVs):** Full file system, network stack, and multi-process support.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: CPU Utilization Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling

The **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm, by Liu & Layland (1973), assigns higher priority to tasks with shorter periods. For $n$ independent, preemptable tasks with deadlines equal to periods, the schedulable utilization bound is derived as follows:

**Step 1: Define the worst-case response time scenario.**
Consider the critical instant — when all tasks are released simultaneously. The longest response time for the lowest-priority task occurs at this instant.

**Step 2: Express total processor demand in a hyperperiod.**
The processor is busy with higher-priority tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_{n-1}$ before it can service $\tau_n$:

$$W_n(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i$$

**Step 3: Apply the critical time instant test.**
The task set is schedulable if and only if there exists a $t \leq T_n$ such that $W_n(t) \leq t$ and $C_n \leq t$. For the utilization bound, we simplify by taking the continuous ceiling (lower bound):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1 - \frac{C_n}{T_n}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the worst-case utilization.**
The classic Liu-Layland bound for $n$ tasks (using the inductive proof with the identity $2^{1/n} \approx 1 + \frac{\ln 2}{n}$ for large $n$):

$$U_{LL}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$

**Step 5: Verify with sample task sets.**

For $n = 1$:

$$U_{LL}(1) = 1 \cdot (2^1 - 1) = 1 \cdot 1 = 1.00 \; (100\%)$$

For $n = 2$:

$$U_{LL}(2) = 2 \cdot (2^{0.5} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.4142 = 0.8284$$

For $n = \infty$:

$$U_{LL}(\infty) = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A utilization $U \leq 0.6931$ is **guaranteed** schedulable under RMS for any number of tasks. Between $0.6931$ and $1.0$, the task set *may* still be schedulable but must be checked case-by-case.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Interrupt Latency for ARM Cortex-M

**Step 1: Identify the latency sources.**
When an interrupt request (IRQ) is asserted, the following events occur sequentially:
1. Pipeline flush of the current instruction.
2. Stacking of 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the active stack.
3. Vector fetch from the Vector Table (in Flash or RAM).
4. First instruction of the ISR begins.

**Step 2: Express the total interrupt latency.**

$$t_{latency} = t_{flush} + t_{stack} + t_{vector}$$

**Step 3: Substitute typical Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz values.**
With a clock period of $T_{clk} = \frac{1}{168 \text{ MHz}} \approx 5.95 \text{ ns}$:

$$t_{flush} = 2 \cdot T_{clk} = 2 \cdot 5.95 \text{ ns} = 11.9 \text{ ns}$$

$$t_{stack} = 8 \cdot T_{clk} = 8 \cdot 5.95 \text{ ns} = 47.6 \text{ ns}$$

$$t_{vector} = 2 \cdot T_{clk} = 2 \cdot 5.95 \text{ ns} = 11.9 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4: Compute the total.**

$$t_{latency} = 11.9 + 47.6 + 11.9 = 71.4 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5: Contextualize the result.**
A typical automotive ECU tolerance is $t_{latency} \leq 10 \text{ µs}$. The 71.4 ns figure is well within bounds, which is why Cortex-M dominates embedded motor-control applications.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing IPC Primitives

| IPC Mechanism | Blocking Behavior | Data Carried | Typical Use Case | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Binary Semaphore** | Task suspends on `take()` | None (signal only) | Event flag, ISR-to-task signaling | 7 marks (frequent) |
| **Counting Semaphore** | Task suspends if count = 0 | Integer count | Resource pool (e.g., 3 UART ports) | 7 marks |
| **Mutex (with PIP)** | Task suspends on lock | None (lock guard) | Shared SPI bus, global variable | 14 marks (full Q) |
| **Message Queue** | Task suspends if full/empty | Fixed-size struct | Sensor data → actuator task | 7–14 marks |
| **Mailbox** | Task suspends on empty | Pointer to variable | Variable-length messages | 7 marks |
| **Event Flags** | `OR`/`AND` of bits | Bitmask (8/16/24/32 bits) | Synchronizing multiple conditions | 7 marks |

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Memory Budget for a 4-Task RTOS

**Problem:** Design the RAM budget for an embedded sensor hub running 4 tasks on FreeRTOS. Cortex-M4 with 192 KB SRAM.

| Task | Stack Size (S_stack) | Heap Size (S_heap) |
|---|---|---|
| `taskSensor` (1 ms tick) | 256 B | 512 B |
| `taskComm` (UART handling) | 512 B | 1024 B |
| `taskCtrl` (PID controller) | 1024 B | 2048 B |
| `taskUI` (button polling) | 256 B | 256 B |
| **FreeRTOS Kernel** | — | 4096 B (configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE × 2) |

**Step 1: Sum all task memory.**

$$M_{tasks} = (256 + 512 + 1024 + 256) + (512 + 1024 + 2048 + 256)$$

**Step 2: Compute task stacks.**

$$M_{stack} = 256 + 512 + 1024 + 256 = 2048 \text{ B}$$

**Step 3: Compute task heaps.**

$$M_{heap} = 512 + 1024 + 2048 + 256 = 3840 \text{ B}$$

**Step 4: Add kernel footprint.**

$$M_{kernel} = 4096 \text{ B}$$

**Step 5: Total memory demand.**

$$M_{total} = 2048 + 3840 + 4096 = 9984 \text{ B} \approx 9.75 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 6: Check feasibility against 192 KB SRAM.**

$$M_{free} = 192 \text{ KB} - 9.75 \text{ KB} = 182.25 \text{ KB} \text{ (available)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The design fits comfortably. The remaining 182 KB can be used for buffers, log storage, or future feature expansion. This is a **typical 14-mark derivation** in KTU Module 4 questions.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Embedded OS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Layer User Tasks] --> B[OS API Layer posix cmsis rtos]
    B --> C[Kernel Core Services]
    C --> C1[Task Scheduler RMS EDF Round Robin]
    C --> C2[Memory Manager Static Heap Pool]
    C --> C3[IPC Engine Queues Semaphores Mutex]
    C --> C4[Timer Subsystem SysTick Software Timers]
    C --> C5[Interrupt Manager NVIC Vector Table]
    C --> D[HAL Device Drivers UART SPI I2C ADC GPIO]
    D --> E[Hardware MCU Peripherals and Sensors]
    C5 --> F[Watchdog Timer Fault Handler]
    F --> G[System Reset or Safe State]

    classDef kernel fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff;
    classDef api fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff;
    classDef app fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff;
    classDef hw fill:#374151,stroke:#374151,color:#ffffff;
    classDef safety fill:#991b1b,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff;

    class A app
    class B api
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 kernel
    class D,E hw
    class F,G safety
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The blue *Kernel Core* block houses all five basic services. The red *Safety* branch (Watchdog + Fault Handler) is an **embedded-specific** addition not present in desktop OS — it guarantees the system never silently hangs.

### 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready: osTaskCreate
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preemption or Time Slice End
    Running --> Blocked: IPC Wait Delay Suspend
    Blocked --> Ready: IPC Signal Timeout Resume
    Running --> Suspended: osTaskSuspend
    Suspended --> Ready: osTaskResume
    Running --> [*]: osTaskDelete
    Blocked --> [*]: osTaskDelete
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** When asked *"Explain the various states of a task,"* draw this exact diagram with 5 states: **Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended, Terminated**. Use **solid arrows** for transitions triggered by the kernel and **dashed arrows** (or annotations) for user-API invocations.

### 4.3 IPC Mechanism Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Producer Task Sensor] -->|osMessagePut| Q1[Message Queue 10 slots]
    Q1 -->|osMessageGet| P2[Consumer Task Actuator]
    P2 -->|osSemaphoreRelease| S1[Binary Semaphore Done Flag]
    S1 -->|osSemaphoreAcquire| P1
    P2 -.->|osMutexLock| M1[Shared SPI Bus]
    P3[Logger Task] -.->|osMutexLock| M1
    M1 -.->|osMutexUnlock| P2
    M1 -.->|osMutexUnlock| P3

    classDef task fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff;

    class M1 shared
```

> [!TIP]
> **Solid arrows** represent data flow (Queues). **Dotted arrows** represent mutual exclusion (Mutex). This distinction is **frequently tested** as a 7-mark diagram question.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any six basic services provided by an embedded operating system.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

The six basic services provided by an embedded OS are:
1. **Task (Process) Management** — creation, scheduling, deletion of concurrent tasks.
2. **Memory Management** — static and dynamic allocation of RAM regions.
3. **Interrupt Management** — handling asynchronous hardware events via ISRs.
4. **Timer Management** — providing system tick, delays, and timeouts.
5. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** — semaphores, queues, mailboxes, mutexes.
6. **I/O Device Management** — abstracting peripherals (UART, SPI, I2C, ADC) via drivers.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award **0.5 mark per correctly identified service with a one-line explanation**. Listing 6 services with one-line descriptions each is the safe strategy.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is a Task Control Block (TCB)? Mention any four fields it contains.**

**Model Answer (1 + 2 marks):**

A **Task Control Block (TCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores the complete execution context of a task. When a task is preempted, its state is saved into its TCB.

Four essential fields are:
1. **Stack Pointer (SP)** — points to the task's current top of stack.
2. **Program Counter (PC)** — saved return address for context restoration.
3. **Task Priority** — fixed integer (e.g., 0-31) used by the scheduler.
4. **Task State** — one of {Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended}.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` (Module 4, Full 14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the various memory management techniques used in embedded operating systems. Compare static and dynamic allocation with suitable examples. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Embedded systems use two principal memory management techniques:

**1. Static Memory Allocation:**
- Memory partitions are fixed at **compile time**.
- Each task is assigned a dedicated stack and a fixed-size global buffer.
- **Advantages:** Deterministic, no fragmentation, no `malloc` overhead. Used in safety-critical systems (AUTOSAR, DO-178C).
- **Example:**
```c
#define STACK_SIZE 256
uint32_t taskStack[STACK_SIZE]; // 1 KB reserved at compile time
```

**2. Dynamic Memory Allocation:**
- Memory is allocated from a **heap** at runtime via `malloc`/`free`.
- **Advantages:** Flexibility, efficient for variable workloads.
- **Disadvantages:** Fragmentation, non-deterministic timing. Unsuitable for hard real-time systems.
- **Example:**
```c
uint8_t* buffer = (uint8_t*)pvPortMalloc(512);
```

**3. Memory Pool (Object/Block Allocation):**
- Pre-allocated fixed-size blocks.
- Eliminates fragmentation while preserving flexibility.
- Used in FreeRTOS via `xQueueCreate()` and `xTaskCreate()`.

**4. Memory Protection Unit (MPU):**
- Hardware-enforced region isolation.
- Prevents one task from accessing another's memory.
- Present in Cortex-M3/M4/M7 (8 regions typical).

| Feature | Static | Dynamic | Pool | MPU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Determinism | High | Low | High | High |
| Flexibility | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| Fragmentation | None | Possible | None | None |
| Hardware Cost | None | None | None | Required |

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the various states of a task in an RTOS. How does priority inversion occur, and how is it solved using the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)? [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Task States (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**
A task in an RTOS exists in one of five states:
1. **Created** — initialized but not yet ready to run.
2. **Ready** — waiting in the scheduler's ready queue for CPU allocation.
3. **Running** — actively executing on the CPU (only one task per core).
4. **Blocked** — waiting for an event (IPC, delay, I/O).
5. **Terminated/Deleted** — resources freed, TCB removed.

**Priority Inversion Problem:**
Consider three tasks:
- **Task H** (High priority, period = 5 ms)
- **Task M** (Medium priority, period = 20 ms)
- **Task L** (Low priority, period = 100 ms) — holds a shared resource (e.g., I2C bus).

If $H$ tries to acquire the resource held by $L$, and $M$ becomes runnable in between, $M$ preempts $L$. $H$ is now indirectly blocked by $M$, even though $H$ has higher priority than $M$. This **unbounded priority inversion** is the famous Mars Pathfinder bug (1997).

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Solution:**
When a high-priority task $H$ blocks on a resource held by low-priority task $L$:
- $L$ **temporarily inherits** the priority of $H$.
- Any medium-priority task $M$ cannot preempt $L$ during this period.
- When $L$ releases the resource, its priority reverts to the original.
- $H$ then acquires the resource and runs to completion.

This bounds the inversion duration to the critical section length of $L$ only.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Diagram missing** (–2 marks): Always draw the state transition diagram with **5 states** and **labeled arrows**.
> 2. **Vague PIP explanation** (–2 marks): You must explicitly state the **three steps** (inherit → run → restore). Don't just say "priority is increased."
> 3. **Confusing MPU with MMU** (–1 mark): MPU has **no virtual memory**; MMU has page tables. State this clearly.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numericals** (–1 mark): Always write µs, KB, MHz, etc.

---

#### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the inter-process communication (IPC) mechanisms in an RTOS. Compare message queues, mailboxes, and semaphores with examples. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

IPC mechanisms allow independent tasks to synchronize and exchange data without race conditions.

**1. Binary Semaphores:**
- Used for **signaling** between tasks or between an ISR and a task.
- State: 0 (locked) or 1 (unlocked).
- **Example (FreeRTOS):**
```c
SemaphoreHandle_t xSem = xSemaphoreCreateBinary();
xSemaphoreGiveFromISR(xSem, NULL);  // From ISR
xSemaphoreTake(xSem, portMAX_DELAY); // From task
```

**2. Counting Semaphores:**
- Counter tracks multiple identical resources.
- **Example:** 3 UART channels shared among 5 tasks.
```c
xSemaphoreCreateCounting(3, 3); // 3 UART ports
```

**3. Mutexes (with Priority Inheritance):**
- For **mutual exclusion** of a shared resource.
- **Must not** be used from ISRs.
- **Example:** Protecting a global configuration struct.
```c
xSemaphoreTake(xMutex, 10 / portTICK_PERIOD_MS);
config.regA = newValue;
xSemaphoreGive(xMutex);
```

**4. Message Queues:**
- FIFO buffer of **fixed-size items**.
- Producer-consumer pattern.
- **Example:** Sensor task → controller task (sends 4-byte float readings).
```c
xQueueSend(xSensorQ, &reading, 10);
xQueueReceive(xSensorQ, &reading, portMAX_DELAY);
```

**5. Mailboxes:**
- Pass **pointers** to variable-sized messages.
- Lighter overhead than copying large structs.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Binary Sem | Counting Sem | Mutex | Queue | Mailbox |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Transfer | No | No | No | Yes (fixed) | Yes (ptr) |
| ISR-Safe | Yes | Yes | **No** | Send: Yes; Recv: No | No |
| PIP Support | No | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| Typical Use | Event flag | Resource pool | Critical section | Producer-consumer | Variable msg |

**(b) Three periodic tasks run on a Rate Monotonic scheduler. Compute the Liu-Layland utilization bound and verify if the task set is schedulable. [7 marks]**

**Problem Statement:**

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 ms | 4 ms |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 ms | 6 ms |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 ms | 10 ms |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute actual CPU utilization.**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate each fraction.**

$$U = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 = 0.8833$$

**Step 3: Compute Liu-Layland bound for $n = 3$.**

$$U_{LL}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

**Step 4: Apply the sufficient condition.**

Since $U = 0.8833 > U_{LL}(3) = 0.7798$, the **sufficient** test **fails**.

**Step 5: Apply the exact Response Time Analysis (RTA).**

For the lowest-priority task $\tau_3$, the worst-case response time is the smallest $R_3$ satisfying:

$$R_3 = C_3 + \sum_{i=1}^{2} \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i$$

Iterate $R_3^{(0)} = 3$:

$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$$

$$R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

$$R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \; (\text{converged})$$

**Step 6: Compare to deadline $D_3 = T_3 = 10$ ms.**

$$R_3 = 10 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = 10 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 7: Conclusion.**

The task set **is schedulable** under RMS, even though the Liu-Layland sufficient test failed. This shows that the LL bound is **sufficient but not necessary** — exact RTA is required for utilization between $0.7798$ and $1.0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in RTA Derivations:**
> 1. Forgetting the ceiling brackets $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (–2 marks). The ceiling is **mandatory**.
> 2. Stopping iteration too early (–1 mark). Always iterate until $R^{(k+1)} = R^{(k)}$.
> 3. Confusing deadline $D_i$ with period $T_i$ (–1 mark). In RMS, $D_i = T_i$ **by definition**; the RTA test breaks if $D_i < T_i$ for some task.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4: Basic OS Services):**

- **Eight Core Services:** Task Mgmt, Memory Mgmt, Interrupt Mgmt, Timer Mgmt, IPC, I/O Mgmt, Error/Fault Mgmt, File System (optional).
- **TCB** is the kernel's per-task context storage. Five states: Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated.
- **Liu-Layland Bound:** $U_{LL}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$. For $n \to \infty$, $U_{LL} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.
- **RMS sufficient test** fails for $U > U_{LL}(n)$, but exact RTA may still prove schedulability.
- **Interrupt Latency** on Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz $\approx 12$ cycles ($\approx 71$ ns).
- **Static vs Dynamic Memory:** Static is deterministic (AUTOSAR); Dynamic is flexible but causes fragmentation.
- **IPC Primitives:** Semaphores = signal, Mutex = exclusion, Queues = data transfer, Mailbox = pointer transfer.
- **Mutex must NEVER be used in ISRs** (use `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR` instead).
- **Priority Inversion** is solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — bind inversion duration to critical section length only.
- **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** formula: $T_{WDT} = \frac{C_{prescaler} \cdot C_{reload}}{f_{clk}}$.
- **Memory Budget Equation:** $M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (S_{stack,i} + S_{heap,i}) + S_{kernel}$.
- **Difference: MPU vs MMU** — MPU has fixed regions, no virtual memory; MMU has page tables, supports virtual memory. Cortex-M uses MPU; Cortex-A uses MMU.
- **Common RTOS:** FreeRTOS (open source), VxWorks (aerospace), QNX (automotive), Zephyr (IoT), ThreadX/Azure RTOS (medical).
- **SysTick** is the standard ARM Cortex-M timer source for OS ticks. Typical tick period: 1 ms or 10 µs.
- **AUTOSAR** mandates **OSEK OS** services for automotive ECUs — static configuration, 4 task states, 3 IPC primitives (events, messages, resources).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Handling in RTOS Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT637 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND ITS APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Handling in RTOS Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt Handling in an RTOS Environment** is the deterministic, time-bounded mechanism by which the Real-Time Operating System kernel temporarily suspends the currently executing task (or the idle task), saves its execution context, transfers control to a predefined **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** — also called the *Interrupt Handler* or *Top-Half* — services the asynchronous event from an internal/external hardware source, performs deferred processing in a *Bottom-Half* (DPC/IST/Tasklet) if required, and finally resumes the interrupted task or invokes a higher-priority ready task.

The three strictly bounded timing metrics that govern every RTOS interrupt transaction are:

1. **Interrupt Latency ($T_{latency}$)** — the maximum time elapsed between the assertion of an Interrupt ReQuest (IRQ) line and the start of execution of the first instruction of the corresponding ISR.
2. **Interrupt Response Time ($T_{response}$)** — the time from IRQ assertion to the start of execution of the *task* that actually processes the buffered interrupt data.
3. **Interrupt Recovery Time ($T_{recovery}$)** — the time taken to restore the saved CPU context and resume the preempted task.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Emergency Room Doctor"

Imagine a senior doctor in a hospital emergency ward. The doctor is busy performing a routine check-up on Patient A (the *currently running task*). Suddenly, the doorbell/buzzer rings — a critical emergency case arrives (the *hardware interrupt request*).

| Doctor's Action | RTOS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pauses the current check-up (records notes) | Saves CPU context (PC, PSW, registers) onto stack |
| Walks to the emergency door | Vector fetch + branch to ISR |
| Quickly triages the critical patient | **Top-Half ISR** — acknowledge device, read essential data |
| Hands the case file to a junior doctor | **Bottom-Half DPC/IST** — deferred processing in task context |
| Returns to Patient A and resumes | Context restore + `IRET` instruction |

The doctor's check-up is *never abandoned* — it is paused deterministically and resumed. The buzzer (interrupt) is *never ignored* — it is serviced within a known upper bound. **This determinism is the soul of RTOS interrupt handling.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just let the task poll the device?** Because polling wastes CPU cycles, wastes power, and — most critically in real-time systems — *cannot guarantee* a bounded worst-case response. An interrupt **pushes** the response time down to a hard upper bound dictated only by the kernel's interrupt-disable windows.

## 1.3 Classification of Interrupts (Foundation Vocabulary)

- **Hardware Interrupts (IRQs)** — Generated by external peripherals (UART, Timer, ADC, GPIO edge, DMA completion). **Asynchronous** to instruction flow.
- **Software Interrupts (Traps/Exceptions/SWI)** — Synchronous, generated by the executing instruction itself (e.g., division by zero, system call `SVC` in ARM Cortex-M).
- **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)** — Cannot be suppressed by the `I-bit` (interrupt mask) in the CPSR/PSR. Reserved for catastrophic events (watchdog, power-fail).
- **Maskable Interrupts (IRQ)** — Can be temporarily blocked by the processor's interrupt-enable flag.

## 1.4 Why Interrupts are Treated *Differently* in an RTOS

In a *General-Purpose OS* (Linux, Windows), interrupts may take microseconds-to-milliseconds of non-deterministic latency. In an **RTOS**, the kernel architecture is specifically designed so that the **maximum interrupt latency is bounded, documented, and verifiable** — often as low as **< 1 µs** in hard real-time kernels (e.g., VxWorks, μC/OS-III, FreeRTOS on Cortex-M).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Students must clearly distinguish between **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** execution and **task-level processing**. An RTOS kernel typically *forbids* most system calls inside an ISR to keep the Top-Half as short as possible (the *"keep the ISR short"* rule).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Level (Split) Interrupt Processing Model

Modern RTOS kernels almost universally adopt a **Top-Half / Bottom-Half** architecture. This split is the single most important concept in this topic.

### 2.1.1 Top-Half (The ISR Itself)
Runs in **interrupt context** — *not* a task context. It executes with interrupts either fully enabled, fully disabled, or with nesting controlled by priority levels depending on the architecture.

Mandatory Top-Half duties:
1. Save any CPU registers it will clobber.
2. Acknowledge the interrupting device (write to the device's interrupt-acknowledge register) so the device de-asserts the IRQ line.
3. Read essential data from the device FIFO/register (e.g., UART RX byte, ADC sample).
4. **Buffer** the data into a kernel-managed queue (e.g., a ring buffer, message queue, or semaphore post).
5. Wake up the Bottom-Half / a waiting high-priority task.
6. Return as quickly as possible.

### 2.1.2 Bottom-Half (DPC, IST, Tasklet, Work-Item, SoftIRQ)
Runs in **task / process context** with the full kernel API available. Examples:

| Kernel | Bottom-Half Mechanism |
|---|---|
| μC/OS-II / III | ISR posts to a message queue / signals a semaphore; a high-prio task waits and processes |
| VxWorks | `intConnect()` + `kernelState` IST (Interrupt Service Thread) |
| FreeRTOS | `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR()` wakes a deferred-processing task; `xTaskNotifyFromISR()` |
| Linux (for reference) | Tasklets, Workqueues, SoftIRQs |
| RTEMS | RTEMS interrupt manager + task-mode handler |

## 2.2 The Canonical ISR Processing Pipeline

The complete, ordered sequence executed by the RTOS when an IRQ fires:

| Step | Action | Performed By |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Device asserts IRQ line on the bus | Hardware peripheral |
| 2 | Interrupt Controller (e.g., NVIC on Cortex-M, PIC on x86, VIC on ARM7) latches & prioritises the request | Interrupt Controller HW |
| 3 | CPU completes the current instruction (pipeline drain) | CPU |
| 4 | CPU samples the IRQ input; if `I-bit = 0` and priority is acceptable, accepts the interrupt | CPU |
| 5 | CPU automatically pushes `PC`, `PSR/CPSR`, sometimes `LR` onto the current stack | CPU hardware |
| 6 | CPU fetches the ISR **vector** from the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) at a fixed offset | CPU hardware |
| 7 | CPU loads the vector into `PC` and begins ISR execution | CPU hardware |
| 8 | ISR saves any additional registers it needs (e.g., R0–R3, R12 on ARM) — *callee-saved* are preserved automatically | ISR software |
| 9 | ISR services the device (read FIFO, ack) and posts a notification to a kernel object | ISR software |
| 10 | ISR restores the manually-saved registers | ISR software |
| 11 | `IRET` instruction pops PC + PSR; CPU resumes the preempted task (or invokes a higher-prio ready task) | CPU hardware |
| 12 | Bottom-Half task runs to completion with full kernel API access | RTOS scheduler |

## 2.3 Rules an ISR MUST Follow in an RTOS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These rules are **exam-favourite bullet points** — expect a 6–7 mark direct question.

1. **Keep the ISR as short as possible.** No loops, no printf, no floating-point math, no lengthy computations.
2. **Do not call blocking kernel services** (`Pend`, `Wait`, `Delay`, `malloc`).
3. **Do not enable interrupts unconditionally** (avoid `sei`/`cpsie i` unless explicitly required) — it breaks priority ordering.
4. **Do not perform I/O that may block** (file system, network sockets).
5. **Use the `FromISR` / `_from_isr()` variants** of kernel APIs (e.g., `xQueueSendFromISR`, `OSSemPost()` in μC/OS).
6. **Save and restore ONLY the registers the ISR actually modifies** — full-context save is wasteful.
7. **Set the ISR's interrupt priority carefully** — on Cortex-M, ISRs running kernel-aware code must be at a priority **above** `configMAX_SYSCALL_INTERRUPT_PRIORITY` (i.e., numerically lower in FreeRTOS convention).
8. **Acknowledge the interrupting device before exiting** the ISR to prevent the same IRQ from re-firing instantly.
9. **Avoid recursive entry into the kernel** — never call a service that itself may cause scheduling.
10. **Defer non-critical work** to a task-context Bottom-Half.

## 2.4 Critical Sections and Interrupt Disabling

A **critical section** is a region of code that accesses a shared resource and must be executed *atomically* with respect to other tasks *and* ISRs that may also touch the same resource.

RTOS provides three progressively more aggressive protection levels:

| Level | Mechanism | Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Task-only lock | `OSSchedLock()` / `vTaskSuspendAll()` | Cheapest; ISRs still run | Protect a resource from other tasks only |
| 2 — Interrupt disable | `OS_ENTER_CRITICAL()` / `taskENTER_CRITICAL()` | Disables maskable IRQs | Protect a small region shared with an ISR |
| 3 — Interrupt lockout + scheduler lock | Combined | Highest; **never** in hard real-time | Last resort; debugging only |

> [!NOTE]
> A *critical section* in an RTOS is **not** a *critical region* in the sense of deadlock theory. Here, it is the **window during which interrupts (and/or preemption) are disabled** to protect shared data integrity.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table layout.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interrupt Latency | $T_{latency} = T_{recognition} + T_{dispatch}$ | µs | IRQ assertion → first ISR instruction |
| 2 | Upper Bound on Latency | $T_{latency} \leq T_{max\_int\_disabled} + T_{vector\_fetch}$ | µs | Bounded by longest IRQ-off window |
| 3 | ISR Execution Time | $T_{ISR} = f(\text{device},\,\text{code})$ | µs | Worst-case top-half duration |
| 4 | Interrupt Response Time | $T_{response} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{sched} + T_{dispatch\_task}$ | µs | IRQ → first task-level processing |
| 5 | Interrupt Recovery Time | $T_{recovery} = T_{context\_restore} + T_{iret}$ | µs | ISR exit → preempted task resumes |
| 6 | Total ISR Overhead | $T_{total} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{recovery}$ | µs | Time CPU is "stolen" from tasks |
| 7 | Max Sustainable IRQ Rate | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{latency}^{max} + T_{ISR}^{max} + T_{recovery}^{max}}$ | Hz | Highest rate the kernel can service |
| 8 | Nested ISR Depth Cost | $T_{nest} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{latency,i} + T_{ISR,i})$ | µs | Sum over $N$ nested levels |
| 9 | CPU Overhead Fraction | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{ISR} \cdot f_{irq}}{T_{CPU\_cycle}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of CPU consumed by ISRs |
| 10 | Minimum Interrupt Pulse Width | $T_{pulse} \geq T_{latency} + T_{ack}$ | µs | Device must hold IRQ for at least this long |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR OS):** The OS specification mandates a *Category-1 ISR* (does not use kernel services) and *Category-2 ISR* (uses kernel services). This two-category split is exactly the Top-Half / Bottom-Half model.
- **Avionics (DO-178C, ARINC 653):** Interrupt latency must be provably bounded for certification.
- **Motor Control (BLDC / FOC):** Hall-sensor / encoder interrupts must be serviced within a few µs to maintain commutation accuracy.
- **Industrial PLCs (IEC 61131-3):** High-speed counter interrupts bound the scan cycle.
- **Medical Devices (pacemakers, infusion pumps):** Interrupt-driven safety timers must meet hard deadlines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Interrupt Response Time

We will derive the complete response-time expression from first principles, tracking every sub-component.

### 3.1.1 Symbol Table

Let us define the following symbols used throughout the derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{irq}      &:= \text{Instant when the device asserts the IRQ line} \\[2pt]
T_{recog}    &:= \text{Time for the interrupt controller to detect and prioritize the request} \\[2pt]
T_{vec\_fetch} &:= \text{Time for the CPU to fetch the vector address from the IVT} \\[2pt]
T_{isr\_start} &:= \text{Instant when the first instruction of the ISR begins execution} \\[2pt]
T_{isr\_end}   &:= \text{Instant when the last instruction of the ISR completes} \\[2pt]
T_{task\_start} &:= \text{Instant when the Bottom-Half task first runs} \\[2pt]
T_{ctx\_save}   &:= \text{Time to push registers onto the stack (hardware + software)} \\[2pt]
T_{ctx\_restore} &:= \text{Time to pop registers and execute IRET} \\[2pt]
T_{sched}     &:= \text{Time for the RTOS scheduler to select the next task} \\[2pt]
T_{dispatch}  &:= \text{Time to load the new task's context} \\[2pt]
T_{preempted\_resume} &:= \text{Instant when the preempted task resumes} \\[2pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Interrupt Latency

The interrupt latency is measured from the moment the IRQ line goes active to the moment the first instruction of the corresponding ISR executes. This duration consists of two components: (a) the time the interrupt controller needs to latch and arbitrate, and (b) the time the CPU needs to complete the current instruction, sample the IRQ, and fetch the vector from the Interrupt Vector Table.

$$
T_{latency} = T_{irq \rightarrow isr\_start} = (T_{isr\_start} - T_{irq})
$$

Expanding this into the recognition and dispatch sub-components:

$$
T_{latency} = T_{recog} + T_{vec\_fetch}
$$

In a worst-case analysis, $T_{recog}$ is bounded above by the longest interval during which interrupts are *masked* in the CPU's PSR (Program Status Register), plus the interrupt controller's own arbitration delay:

$$
T_{latency}^{max} = T_{max\_int\_disabled} + T_{arb} + T_{vec\_fetch}
$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $T_{max\_int\_disabled}$ — The maximum continuous time the running code has set the `I-bit` to disable maskable IRQs. This is the *dominant* contributor and is governed by the longest critical section in the kernel.
- $T_{arb}$ — The time the interrupt controller (e.g., NVIC) spends prioritising between multiple simultaneously-pending IRQs.
- $T_{vec\_fetch}$ — One or more memory reads to obtain the ISR address from the IVT.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — ISR Execution Time

The time spent inside the ISR itself is purely a function of the device's data path and the code written. For a polled UART receiver that reads $N$ bytes:

$$
T_{ISR} = T_{entry} + N \cdot T_{byte\_service} + T_{ack} + T_{exit}
$$

Where:
- $T_{entry}$ — Time for the ISR prologue (save extra registers beyond what the hardware auto-saved).
- $T_{byte\_service}$ — Time to read one byte from the data register and copy it to a ring buffer.
- $T_{ack}$ — Time to write the interrupt-acknowledge / clear-pending bit.
- $T_{exit}$ — Time for the ISR epilogue (restore registers) before `IRET`.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Interrupt Response Time (End-to-End)

The **response time** extends beyond the ISR completion to the moment the *task* that processes the buffered data actually begins to run. This includes any post-ISR kernel work and the scheduler's decision.

$$
T_{response} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{sched} + T_{dispatch\_task}
$$

**Logical justification for each term:**
- $T_{latency}$ — Wait until the ISR can finally start.
- $T_{ISR}$ — Time the ISR needs to consume the device's data.
- $T_{sched}$ — Time for the kernel's ready-queue walker to pick the highest-priority runnable task.
- $T_{dispatch\_task}$ — Time to load the new task's stack pointer, restore its registers, and start its first instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> If the Bottom-Half task was already the *highest-priority* runnable task before the IRQ fired, the kernel may bypass a full re-schedule and simply return to it — in which case $T_{sched} + T_{dispatch\_task}$ collapses to a small constant (e.g., one or two MOV instructions on Cortex-M).

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Interrupt Recovery Time

The recovery time is the CPU cost to *undo* the interrupt and resume the preempted task. It begins at the `IRET` instruction and ends when the preempted task executes its next instruction.

$$
T_{recovery} = T_{ctx\_restore} + T_{iret}
$$

In an architecture with hardware auto-restore (e.g., ARM Cortex-M with its `LR`, `PC`, `xPSR` automatic stacking), $T_{ctx\_restore}$ is a single load-multiple instruction. In architectures with software-managed restore (e.g., ARM7 with IRQ mode having its own banked `LR`), $T_{recovery}$ may be longer because the kernel must `MOVS PC, LR` carefully to undo the link register's dual usage.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Total CPU Time Stolen by the Interrupt

Adding the three intervals that the CPU is *not* executing application tasks:

$$
T_{total\_overhead} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{recovery}
$$

If the interrupt fires at a frequency $f_{irq}$ (in Hz), the **fraction of CPU time consumed by interrupt overhead** is:

$$
\eta = f_{irq} \cdot T_{total\_overhead}
$$

For correctness, $\eta$ must remain strictly less than **1.0** (i.e., 100% of CPU time); in practice, a healthy real-time system keeps $\eta \leq 0.25$–$0.40$ to leave headroom for application tasks and the idle task.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Maximum Sustainable Interrupt Frequency

Solving $\eta = 1$ for $f_{irq}$:

$$
f_{irq}^{max} = \frac{1}{T_{latency}^{max} + T_{ISR}^{max} + T_{recovery}^{max}}
$$

This is the **hard ceiling** beyond which the system will begin to lose interrupts (drop UART bytes, miss encoder pulses, etc.) because the CPU cannot finish servicing one IRQ before the next arrives.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem.** A Cortex-M3 RTOS controls a CNC machine. The quadrature encoder produces IRQs at a peak rate of **50 kHz**. The encoder ISR is measured to have: $T_{latency}^{max} = 2.5 \,\mu s$, $T_{ISR}^{max} = 4.0 \,\mu s$, $T_{recovery}^{max} = 1.5 \,\mu s$. 
> **(a)** Compute the maximum sustainable interrupt rate. **(b)** Find the CPU overhead fraction at the peak rate. **(c)** Comment on whether the system is viable.

### Solution — (a) Maximum Sustainable Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{irq}^{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{latency}^{max} + T_{ISR}^{max} + T_{recovery}^{max}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2.5 \;\mu s + 4.0 \;\mu s + 1.5 \;\mu s} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{8.0 \;\mu s} \\[6pt]
&= 125{,}000 \;\text{Hz} \;=\; 125 \;\text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $f_{irq}^{max} = 125 \,\text{kHz}$.

### Solution — (b) CPU Overhead at 50 kHz

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= f_{irq}^{peak} \times T_{total\_overhead} \\[6pt]
&= (50 \times 10^{3} \;\text{Hz}) \times (8.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.40 \;=\; 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\eta = 40\%$ — i.e., **40% of all CPU cycles are consumed by the encoder interrupt path**.

### Solution — (c) Viability Commentary

Since $50 \,\text{kHz} \ll 125 \,\text{kHz}$, the kernel can comfortably keep up. However, $40\%$ CPU overhead is high; a designer should consider (i) reducing $T_{ISR}$ by deferring heavy processing to a Bottom-Half task, or (ii) using the encoder's hardware quadrature counter (with DMA) to lower the IRQ rate to, say, 1 kHz. The hard-real-time schedulability test would also require confirming that the remaining $60\%$ of CPU is sufficient for all periodic tasks under worst-case execution times.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why a *Short* ISR Improves *All* Timing Metrics

| Design Decision | Effect on $T_{latency}$ | Effect on $T_{ISR}$ | Effect on $T_{recovery}$ | Net Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Move printf() out of ISR | none | **↓↓↓ large** | ↓ small | Dramatically better headroom |
| Use DMA instead of polled I/O | none | **↓↓ medium** | none | Fewer interrupts, less overhead |
| Use `FromISR` kernel APIs | ↓ small | none | none | Cleaner bottom-half activation |
| Disable IRQs in long loops | **↑↑ large** | unchanged | unchanged | **Latency worst-case degrades** |
| Lower ISR priority | ↑ small | unchanged | unchanged | Higher-priority devices preempt first |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End RTOS Interrupt Handling Flow

> This is the canonical *Top-Half / Bottom-Half* processing pipeline. Use this exact diagram in your exam answers for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Hardware Asserts IRQ Line] --> B[Interrupt Controller NVIC Latches and Prioritises Request]
    B --> C{Is I-bit Set in PSR and Priority Acceptable?}
    C -- No --> Z[IRQ Stays Pending Until Unmasked]
    Z --> C
    C -- Yes --> D[CPU Finishes Current Instruction and Pipeline Drain]
    D --> E[CPU Hardware Auto-Pushes PC and xPSR onto Active Stack]
    E --> F[CPU Reads Vector Address from Interrupt Vector Table IVT]
    F --> G[CPU Loads Vector into PC and Branches to ISR]
    G --> H[ISR Prologue Saves Additional Clobbered Registers]
    H --> I[ISR Reads Data from Device FIFO and Writes to Ring Buffer]
    I --> J[ISR Acknowledges Device by Writing Ack Register]
    J --> K[ISR Posts Notification via FromISR API e.g. xSemaphoreGiveFromISR]
    K --> L[ISR Epilogue Restores Saved Registers]
    L --> M[ISR Executes IRET Instruction]
    M --> N[RTOS Scheduler Decides Next Run Task]
    N --> O{Bottom-Half Task Highest Priority Ready?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Context Switch to Bottom-Half DPC or IST Task]
    O -- No --> Q[Restore Preempted Task Context and Resume It]
    P --> R[Bottom-Half Performs Full Processing with Kernel API Access]
    R --> S[Bottom-Half Signals Final User Task via Semaphore or Queue]
    S --> T[Bottom-Half Task Blocks or Loops Waiting for Next IRQ]
    Q --> U[Preempted Task Resumes from Exact Interrupted Instruction]
    T --> V[Idle Task Runs Until Next IRQ]
    U --> V
    V --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Top-Half vs Bottom-Half Architectural Split (Block View)

> This complements the flowchart with a *structural* view emphasising which context each half runs in.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HW[Hardware Layer]
        DEV[Peripheral Device e.g. UART RX]
    end

    subgraph TOP[Top-Half Context = Interrupt Context]
        ISR1[ISR Entry]
        ISR2[Read Device Data]
        ISR3[Acknowledge IRQ]
        ISR4[Post ToISR Kernel API]
        ISR5[ISR Exit via IRET]
        ISR1 --> ISR2 --> ISR3 --> ISR4 --> ISR5
    end

    subgraph BOT[Bottom-Half Context = Task Context]
        BHQ[High Priority Task Waiting on Semaphore]
        BHP[Process Buffered Data Fully]
        BHR[Signal Application Task]
        BHQ --> BHP --> BHR
    end

    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        APT[Application Task Doing Heavy Work]
    end

    DEV -- IRQ Line --> ISR1
    ISR4 -- FromISR Notify --> BHQ
    BHR -- Semaphore Give --> APT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Nested Interrupt Preemption

> When a higher-priority IRQ arrives *during* the execution of a lower-priority ISR, the kernel may allow nesting (on architectures that permit it). This diagram traces the context save/restore chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Task T1 Running at Priority 5] --> S1[IRQ-A Arrives at Priority 4]
    S1 --> S2[Push T1 Context onto Stack]
    S2 --> S3[Execute ISR-A Top-Half]
    S3 --> S4{IRQ-B Arrives at Priority 2 During ISR-A}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Push ISR-A Context onto Stack]
    S5 --> S6[Execute ISR-B Top-Half]
    S6 --> S7[ISR-B Completes and Executes IRET]
    S7 --> S8[Pop ISR-A Context from Stack]
    S8 --> S9[Resume ISR-A From Interrupted Instruction]
    S9 --> S10[ISR-A Completes and Executes IRET]
    S10 --> S11[Pop T1 Context from Stack]
    S11 --> S12[Resume T1 From Exact Interrupted Instruction]
    S4 -- No --> S10
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — RTOS Critical Section vs ISR Interaction (Shared Resource)

> Shows how the `OS_ENTER_CRITICAL` / `taskENTER_CRITICAL` macro protects a shared variable from being corrupted by an ISR that may fire at any time.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task T1 Begins Shared Resource Access] --> B[Disable Maskable Interrupts via EnterCritical Macro]
    B --> C[Read or Modify Shared Variable X Atomically]
    C --> D[Enable Maskable Interrupts via ExitCritical Macro]
    D --> E[Task T1 Continues Non-Critical Code]

    F[Device IRQ Fires] --> G{Interrupts Currently Masked?}
    G -- Yes --> H[IRQ Stays Pending in NVIC]
    H --> I[After ExitCritical Mask is Lifted]
    I --> J[NVIC Immediately Invokes ISR]
    G -- No --> J
    J --> K[ISR Reads Same Shared Variable X]
    K --> L[ISR Writes to Local Buffer Only]
    L --> M[ISR Returns and Acknowledges Device]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define interrupt latency and interrupt response time. How do they differ?

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Interrupt Latency (1 mark):** The maximum time elapsed from the moment an Interrupt ReQuest (IRQ) line is asserted by a hardware device to the moment the first instruction of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) begins execution. Mathematically $T_{latency} = T_{recog} + T_{vec\_fetch}$. It is bounded above by the longest interval during which the kernel has maskable interrupts disabled.

- **Interrupt Response Time (1 mark):** The time from IRQ assertion to the moment the *task* that actually processes the buffered interrupt data starts executing. It extends the latency definition by including the ISR execution time plus the scheduler and dispatch time. $T_{response} = T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{sched} + T_{dispatch}$.

- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Latency measures time-to-ISR-entry; response time measures time-to-task-processing. The former is bounded by kernel internals; the latter is bounded by kernel internals *plus* the application-design choice of how much work the ISR itself does. A well-designed RTOS minimises latency in the kernel, while a well-designed application minimises response time by keeping the ISR short and deferring heavy work to a task.

---

### Q2. List any six rules that an Interrupt Service Routine must follow in an RTOS environment.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per rule):**

1. The ISR must be kept **as short as possible** to minimise $T_{ISR}$ and $T_{response}$.
2. The ISR must **not call any blocking kernel service** such as `Pend`, `Wait`, `Delay`, or `malloc`/`free`.
3. The ISR must use the **`FromISR` variant** of kernel APIs (e.g., `xQueueSendFromISR`, `OSQPost`, `OSSemPost`) and respect the kernel's interrupt-priority rules.
4. The ISR must **save and restore only the registers it actually modifies** — full-context save inside the ISR is wasteful.
5. The ISR must **acknowledge the interrupting device** (write to the ack register or clear the pending bit) before returning, otherwise the same IRQ will re-trigger immediately.
6. The ISR must **defer all non-essential processing** to a Bottom-Half task (DPC/IST) that runs in task context with full kernel API access.
7. *(Bonus)* The ISR must be assigned an appropriate **interrupt priority** — on Cortex-M, kernel-aware ISRs run above `configMAX_SYSCALL_INTERRUPT_PRIORITY`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — *(14 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the two-level interrupt processing model (Top-Half and Bottom-Half) used in modern RTOS kernels. Why is the split necessary? List the responsibilities of each half. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Need for the Split (2 marks):**
A single, monolithic ISR that performs all interrupt-related work (read data, process data, update application state) leads to three serious problems in an RTOS:

- **Unbounded worst-case execution time:** Long ISRs violate the *fundamental real-time guarantee* because they hold the CPU away from higher-priority tasks for an unbounded duration.
- **Loss of kernel service usage:** Many kernel APIs (`Pend`, `Delay`, dynamic memory) assume task context. Calling them from an ISR leads to undefined behaviour, deadlock, or scheduler corruption.
- **Priority inversion within ISRs:** If a low-priority ISR runs a long loop, no higher-priority device can preempt it on architectures that don't support nested interrupts.

The two-level model is the engineered solution: do the bare minimum in interrupt context, and do the rest in a well-behaved task.

**2. Top-Half — The ISR (3 marks):**
Runs in **interrupt context**. Responsibilities are strictly limited to:
- Save only the registers it will modify.
- Read the essential data from the device (e.g., one byte from UART RX, one sample from ADC, one count from encoder).
- Acknowledge the device so the IRQ line is de-asserted.
- Buffer the data into a kernel-managed queue / ring buffer.
- Wake the Bottom-Half task using a `FromISR` kernel API (e.g., `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR`, `OSQPost`, `OSFlagPost`).
- Restore the saved registers and execute `IRET`.

The Top-Half typically executes in a few microseconds and **does not** use blocking kernel services.

**3. Bottom-Half — The Deferred Procedure (2 marks):**
Runs in **task context** as a regular (often high-priority) RTOS task. Responsibilities:
- Wait (block) on the semaphore or queue populated by the Top-Half.
- Process the buffered data using the full kernel API — can call any kernel service, use dynamic memory, perform I/O, take semaphores, etc.
- Signal the final user/application task once processing is complete.
- Loop back to wait for the next interrupt.

This split keeps ISRs short, preserves kernel invariants, and allows the heavy work to be scheduled with full preemption and priority semantics.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, describe the complete interrupt handling sequence in an RTOS from the moment an IRQ is asserted to the moment the interrupted task is resumed. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (refer to Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4 for the figure):**

**Sequence (1 mark per major phase):**

1. **Hardware assertion:** The peripheral device drives its IRQ line active.
2. **Controller latching:** The interrupt controller (NVIC/PIC/VIC) latches the request, assigns it a priority, and arbitrates against any other pending IRQs.
3. **CPU acceptance:** The CPU samples the IRQ input at the end of the current instruction. If the mask bit is clear and the IRQ's priority is high enough, the CPU accepts the interrupt.
4. **Automatic context save:** The CPU hardware pushes the **return address (PC)** and the **processor status register (xPSR/CPSR)** onto the active stack. Some architectures also auto-save `LR`.
5. **Vector fetch:** The CPU reads the ISR's address from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** at a fixed offset corresponding to the IRQ number, and loads it into `PC`.
6. **ISR prologue:** The ISR's first instructions save any additional registers it will clobber (callee-saved vs caller-saved convention).
7. **Device service:** The ISR reads the device data, copies it into a ring buffer or message queue, and acknowledges the device.
8. **Kernel notification:** The ISR calls a `FromISR` API to wake the Bottom-Half task (e.g., `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR`).
9. **ISR epilogue:** The ISR restores the saved registers.
10. **`IRET` instruction:** The CPU pops `PC` and `xPSR` from the stack. If a higher-priority task is now ready (the Bottom-Half), the scheduler triggers a context switch; otherwise, the CPU resumes the preempted task.
11. **Bottom-Half execution:** The deferred task runs in task context with full kernel API access, processes the buffered data, and signals the user task.
12. **Resumption of preempted task:** The original task continues from the exact instruction where it was interrupted — no instruction is re-executed and none is skipped.

The exact diagram in Section 4.1 should be reproduced in the answer booklet for full marks.

---

### Question B — *(14 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the maximum sustainable interrupt frequency in an RTOS. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Interrupts are serviced sequentially with no nested preemption (worst-case single-queue model).
2. The RTOS is in a steady state where every IRQ is fully serviced before the next arrives.
3. The IRQ source is periodic at frequency $f_{irq}$.
4. $T_{latency}$, $T_{ISR}$, and $T_{recovery}$ are the respective *worst-case* values (upper bounds) of the latency, ISR execution, and recovery times.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $T_{period}$ be the inter-arrival time of two consecutive interrupts. Since the system is sustainable only if every IRQ is fully serviced before the next one arrives:

$$
T_{period} \;\geq\; T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{recovery}
$$

Substituting $T_{period} = 1 / f_{irq}$:

$$
\frac{1}{f_{irq}} \;\geq\; T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{recovery}
$$

Inverting both sides (and reversing the inequality because $1/x$ is monotonically decreasing for $x > 0$):

$$
f_{irq} \;\leq\; \frac{1}{T_{latency} + T_{ISR} + T_{recovery}}
$$

The maximum sustainable frequency is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,f_{irq}^{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{latency}^{max} + T_{ISR}^{max} + T_{recovery}^{max}}\,}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The kernel can service IRQs no faster than the reciprocal of the worst-case total ISR overhead. Beyond this rate, interrupts will be lost, the device's data buffer will overflow, and the system's real-time guarantees are violated.

---

**(b) What is a critical section in the context of an RTOS? Explain how critical sections are implemented using interrupt disabling. Discuss why critical sections must be kept as short as possible. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A **critical section** is a small region of code that accesses a shared resource (e.g., a global variable, peripheral register, linked-list head pointer) and must be executed *atomically* with respect to both other tasks *and* any ISRs that may also access the same resource. The atomicity requirement means that no other context switch or interrupt preemption may split the region.

**2. Why Interrupt Disabling is Needed (1 mark):**
