A **Digital Twin** is a **dynamic, real-time virtual replica** of a physical asset, process, or system, synchronized through sensor telemetry, used for **monitoring, simulation, prediction, and optimization**. Embedded systems act as the **physical edge I/O** that keeps the digital twin synchronized with the real world.

---

## 2.6 Trend 6 — Embedded Cybersecurity

### 2.6.1 Definition
**Embedded cybersecurity** in 2024+ emphasizes **hardware-rooted trust**, immutable identities, and **post-quantum-ready** cryptography. The classical "secure boot + TLS" stack is being augmented with **secure enclaves, silicon PUFs, and NIST PQC algorithms** (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium).

### 2.6.2 Hardware Root-of-Trust Components
- **PUF (Physical Unclonable Function)** — silicon fingerprint for key generation.
- **Secure Element (SE)** — tamper-resistant crypto co-processor (e.g., NXP SE050, Infineon OPTIGA).
- **TPM 2.0** — standardized trust module (used in industrial PCs and some SoCs).
- **Arm TrustZone-M** — isolation between secure and non-secure worlds on Cortex-M33/M55.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Recent Trends)

The following formulas are the **core quantitative tools** required to solve KTU 2024 ESE numerical/analytical questions on this topic. All quantities use **SI units** unless stated.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TinyML Model Size (bits) | $S = N \cdot b$ | $N$ = # parameters, $b$ = bits per weight | Footprint estimation on Flash |
| 2 | Compression Ratio | $C_R = \dfrac{b_{\text{orig}}}{b_{\text{quant}}}$ | $b_{\text{orig}}$ = original precision | Quantization benefit analysis |
| 3 | Dynamic Power (CMOS) | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = activity, $C_L$ = load cap, $V_{DD}$ = supply, $f$ = freq | Energy-aware clock/voltage scaling |
| 4 | Static Power (Leakage) | $P_{\text{stat}} = I_{\text{leak}} \cdot V_{DD}$ | $I_{\text{leak}}$ = leakage current | Sleep-mode power budgeting |
| 5 | Average Power | $P_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot P_{\text{active}} + (1-D) \cdot P_{\text{sleep}}$ | $D$ = duty cycle | Battery-life estimation |
| 6 | Battery Life (hours) | $t_{\text{bat}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{mAh}} \cdot V_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}} \cdot 1000}$ | $C_{\text{mAh}}$ = capacity, $V_{\text{bat}}$ = nominal | IoT device lifetime |
| 7 | GOPS/W Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{GOPS}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | GOPS = Giga-Op/sec, $P$ in Watts | Comparing AI accelerators |
| 8 | End-to-End Latency | $L_{\text{e2e}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{com}} + T_{\text{act}}$ | All in seconds | Real-time control loop analysis |
| 9 | 5G URLLC Latency Target | $L_{\text{target}} \le 1 \text{ ms}$ | One-way air-interface latency | URLLC service guarantee |
| 10 | Amdahl's Speedup | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = cores | Heterogeneous compute benefit |
| 11 | Roofline Model Bound | $\begin{cases} \pi = I \cdot \beta & \text{if compute-bound} \\ \pi = \pi_{\text{peak}} & \text{if memory-bound} \end{cases}$ | $I$ = intensity (ops/byte), $\beta$ = bandwidth | NPU/MCU performance ceiling |
| 12 | RISC-V Code Density | $\text{Density}_{\text{C}} = \dfrac{\text{Bytes}_{RV32I}}{\text{Bytes}_{RV32IC}}$ | I vs. IC | Justification for C extension |
| 13 | PUF Reliability | $H_{\text{intra}} = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = bit probabilities | Key stability metric |
| 14 | Energy Harvesting Power | $P_{\text{harv}} = \eta_{\text{conv}} \cdot P_{\text{source}}$ | $\eta$ = conversion efficiency | Battery-less design |
| 15 | Federated Learning Rounds | $R \approx \dfrac{\log(1/\varepsilon)}{\log(1-\eta)}$ | $\varepsilon$ = target error, $\eta$ = convergence rate | FL convergence estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Break Rule Reminder:** In the formula sheet above, the **absolute value bars** in the compression-ratio ratio (e.g., $\vert b_{\text{orig}}/b_{\text{quant}} \vert$) are intentionally rendered using `\vert` rather than the literal pipe character to **preserve markdown table integrity**.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Trend | Industrial Application | Reference KPI |
|---|---|---|
| TinyML | Predictive maintenance on bearings | <2 mW average, 95% fault detection |
| RISC-V | Western Digital storage controllers | 1 billion+ RISC-V cores shipped |
| Neuromorphic | Event-based drone vision (Prophesee) | 10 µs reaction time |
| AIoT | Smart agriculture (John Deere See & Spray) | 80% herbicide reduction |
| Digital Twin | GE jet engine twins | 1% fuel efficiency gain |
| Embedded Security | NXP SE050 in smart meters | FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certified |
| AUTOSAR Adaptive | Mercedes Drive Pilot Level 3 autonomy | ISO 26262 ASIL-D |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section performs **exhaustive derivations** of the high-yield KTU numerical problems for Module 4. Every algebraic step is shown with explicit reasoning.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — TinyML Model Size Before and After Int8 Quantization

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
A keyword-spotting TinyML model has **$N = 250{,}000$** trainable parameters, originally stored as **Float32 (32 bits)**. After applying **post-training INT8 quantization**, the model is reduced to **8 bits per weight**.

*Compute:*
(a) The original model footprint in **kilobytes (KB)**.
(b) The quantized model footprint in **KB**.
(c) The **compression ratio** $C_R$.
(d) The **percentage reduction** in Flash memory consumed.

### Step 1 — Define the model size formula

The size of a neural-network weight tensor in bits is the product of the parameter count and the bit width per weight:

$$
S_{\text{bits}} = N \cdot b
$$

where $N$ is the number of trainable parameters and $b$ is the bit width per weight.

### Step 2 — Compute original Float32 size in bits

Substitute $N = 250{,}000$ and $b = 32$:

$$
S_{\text{orig}} = 250{,}000 \times 32 \;\text{bits}
$$

$$
S_{\text{orig}} = 8{,}000{,}000 \;\text{bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert bits to bytes, then to kilobytes

There are 8 bits per byte and 1024 bytes per kilobyte:

$$
S_{\text{orig}} = \dfrac{8{,}000{,}000}{8} \;\text{bytes} = 1{,}000{,}000 \;\text{bytes}
$$

$$
S_{\text{orig}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{1024} \;\text{KB} \approx 976.56 \;\text{KB}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute INT8 quantized size in bits

Substitute $N = 250{,}000$ and $b = 8$:

$$
S_{\text{quant}} = 250{,}000 \times 8 \;\text{bits} = 2{,}000{,}000 \;\text{bits}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to kilobytes

$$
S_{\text{quant}} = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{8 \times 1024} = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{8192} \approx 244.14 \;\text{KB}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the compression ratio

$$
C_R = \dfrac{S_{\text{orig}}}{S_{\text{quant}}} = \dfrac{976.56}{244.14} = 4.0
$$

This confirms the well-known **4× compression** of Float32→Int8 quantization.

### Step 7 — Compute the percentage memory reduction

$$
\Delta S\,\% = \dfrac{S_{\text{orig}} - S_{\text{quant}}}{S_{\text{orig}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta S\,\% = \dfrac{976.56 - 244.14}{976.56} \times 100 = \dfrac{732.42}{976.56} \times 100 \approx 75\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:**
> (a) $S_{\text{orig}} \approx 976.56\;\text{KB}$
> (b) $S_{\text{quant}} \approx 244.14\;\text{KB}$
> (c) $C_R = 4.0$
> (d) $\Delta S\,\% \approx 75\,\%$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery-Life Estimation for a TinyML Sensor Node

**Problem Statement:**
A wireless sensor node uses a **$C = 2400\;\text{mAh}$, $V_{\text{bat}} = 3.7\;\text{V}$** Li-Po cell. Its average power consumption is **$P_{\text{avg}} = 1.2\;\text{mW}$** (deep-sleep dominated). Estimate the **operational lifetime in years**.

### Step 1 — Convert battery energy to Joules

Energy in a battery is $E = Q \cdot V$, where $Q$ is charge in ampere-hours. The conversion to Joules uses $1\;\text{Ah} = 3600\;\text{C}$:

$$
E_{\text{bat}} = C_{\text{mAh}} \cdot V_{\text{bat}} \cdot 3.6
$$

The factor 3.6 absorbs both the milli→unit conversion and 3600 seconds/hour:

$$
E_{\text{bat}} = 2400 \times 3.7 \times 3.6 \;\text{J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{bat}} = 31{,}968\;\text{J} \approx 32\;\text{kJ}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute operational lifetime in seconds

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{31{,}968\;\text{J}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{W}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{op}} = 26{,}640{,}000\;\text{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to hours

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \dfrac{26{,}640{,}000}{3600} = 7400\;\text{h}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to years

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \dfrac{7400}{24 \times 365.25} \approx \dfrac{7400}{8766} \approx 0.844\;\text{years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Lifetime $\approx 0.84$ years ($\approx 10$ months). To extend beyond 10 years, the node must switch to **energy harvesting**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy-Harvested Node Power Budget

**Problem Statement:**
A solar harvester provides **$P_{\text{src}} = 100\;\text{mW/cm}^{2}$** under noon sun to a **$A = 4\;\text{cm}^{2}$** cell, with conversion efficiency **$\eta = 22\%$**. The downstream DC-DC converter has efficiency **$\eta_{\text{conv}} = 92\%$**. Compute the **delivered electrical power** $P_{\text{del}}$ and the **daily energy budget** assuming **6 peak-sun-hours**.

### Step 1 — Compute raw harvested power

$$
P_{\text{raw}} = P_{\text{src}} \cdot A \cdot \eta
$$

$$
P_{\text{raw}} = 100 \times 4 \times 0.22 = 88\;\text{mW}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply converter efficiency

$$
P_{\text{del}} = P_{\text{raw}} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}} = 88 \times 0.92 = 80.96\;\text{mW}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute daily energy budget

$$
E_{\text{day}} = P_{\text{del}} \cdot t_{\text{peak}} = 80.96\;\text{mW} \times 6\;\text{h} = 485.76\;\text{mWh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** $P_{\text{del}} \approx 81\;\text{mW}$, $E_{\text{day}} \approx 485.8\;\text{mWh}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — 5G URLLC Latency Budget Decomposition

**Problem Statement:**
A URLLC control loop has four sequential stages: sensing ($T_s = 50\;\mu\text{s}$), edge inference ($T_p = 200\;\mu\text{s}$), uplink transmission ($T_u = 150\;\mu\text{s}$), and actuation ($T_a = 80\;\mu\text{s}$). Verify whether the **1 ms URLLC** one-way budget is satisfied.

### Step 1 — Sum the latencies

$$
L_{\text{e2e}} = T_s + T_p + T_u + T_a
$$

$$
L_{\text{e2e}} = 50 + 200 + 150 + 80 = 480\;\mu\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare to URLLC budget

$$
L_{\text{e2e}} = 480\;\mu\text{s} \le 1000\;\mu\text{s} \;\;\checkmark
$$

### Step 3 — Compute headroom

$$
\Delta L = 1000 - 480 = 520\;\mu\text{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The loop has **520 µs of headroom** — sufficient for retransmission and scheduling jitter, satisfying the 1-ms URLLC contract.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Trends to KTU Course Outcomes

| Trend | CO Mapped | Bloom Level | Marks Weightage (ESE) |
|---|---|---|---|
| TinyML & Edge AI | CO4 | L3 (Apply) / L4 (Analyze) | 14 |
| RISC-V Architecture | CO4 | L2 (Understand) | 7 (sub-part) |
| Neuromorphic Computing | CO5 | L2 (Understand) | 3–7 |
| AIoT | CO4, CO5 | L4 (Analyze) | 7 |
| Digital Twins | CO5 | L3 (Apply) | 3–7 |
| Embedded Security | CO4 | L4 (Analyze) | 7–14 |
| AUTOSAR / Automotive | CO5 | L2 (Understand) | 3 |
| Energy Harvesting | CO3 | L3 (Apply) | 3–7 |
| IoMT / Wearables | CO5 | L2 (Understand) | 3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All mermaid diagrams below obey the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (purely alphanumeric, letter-prefixed) and the **Label Formatting Restriction** (no markdown inside node text).

---

## 4.1 Edge AI / TinyML Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Physical Sensor - Vibration or Acoustic] --> B1[Analog Front End - Amplifier and ADC]
    A2[Image Sensor - Camera or DVS] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Digital Preprocessing - Filter and Window]
    C1 --> D1[Feature Extraction - MFCC or Spectrogram]
    D1 --> E1[TinyML Inference Engine - TFLM on Cortex M55]
    E1 --> F1[Decision Output - Class Label and Confidence]
    F1 --> G1[Local Actuator - Motor or Relay]
    F1 --> H1[Optional Telemetry - MQTT over BLE]
    E1 --> I1[Quantized Model Store - Int8 Flash]
    I1 --> E1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow moves **left → right**. The lower feedback arrow from `I1` to `E1` represents the on-chip model artifact fetched from Flash at boot time. This is the canonical **Edge AI pipeline** seen in industrial predictive-maintenance nodes.

---

## 4.2 TinyML Deployment Workflow (Cloud → Edge)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 - Collect and Label Sensor Data on Edge]
    S2[Step 2 - Upload Datasets to Cloud]
    S3[Step 3 - Train Large Model on GPU Cluster]
    S4[Step 4 - Apply Pruning and Quantization Aware Training]
    S5[Step 5 - Convert to TFLite or ONNX Int8 Artifact]
    S6[Step 6 - Generate C Array via xxd or TVM Micro]
    S7[Step 7 - Cross Compile for Target MCU using ARM GCC]
    S8[Step 8 - Flash Model and Firmware to Device]
    S9[Step 9 - On Device Inference and Telemetry]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
    S9 -. feedback loop .-> S3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback arrow from `S9` to `S3` represents the **continuous-learning** loop where aggregated inferences (not raw data) are used to retrain the cloud model — a foundational AIoT concept.

---

## 4.3 AIoT Layered Architecture Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    L5[Layer 5 - Application and Services - Smart Home and Industry 4.0]
    L4[Layer 4 - Edge Intelligence - TinyML and Federated Learning]
    L3[Layer 3 - Communication - 5G URLLC and Wi-Fi 6 and BLE 5.4]
    L2[Layer 2 - Perception - Sensors and Actuators]
    L1[Layer 1 - Hardware - MCU and SoC and Sensor Fusion]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each layer is **upstream-consumed** by the next. The AIoT paradigm is a **vertical integration** where intelligence (Layer 4) touches both the hardware below and the application above.

---

## 4.4 Digital Twin Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Physical Asset - Turbine or Robot or Vehicle]
    T1[Embedded Telemetry Unit - Sensor and MCU]
    V1[Digital Twin Model - High Fidelity Virtual Replica]
    A1[Analytics and AI Engine - Anomaly Detection and Prediction]
    D1[Decision Support System - Operator Dashboard]
    AC1[Actuation and Control Commands]

    P1 --> T1
    T1 --> V1
    V1 --> A1
    A1 --> D1
    D1 --> AC1
    AC1 --> P1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **closed loop** is the defining property of a digital twin. The bottom arrow `AC1 → P1` represents the **actuation channel** that physically influences the asset — distinguishing a *digital twin* from a mere *digital shadow*.

---

## 4.5 RISC-V Modular ISA Composition

```mermaid
graph LR
    BASE[RISC V Base Integer ISA - RV32I - Mandatory]
    M_EXT[M Extension - Multiply and Divide]
    A_EXT[A Extension - Atomic Instructions]
    C_EXT[C Extension - Compressed 16 bit Encoding]
    F_EXT[F Extension - Single Precision Float]
    V_EXT[V Extension - Vector Processing]
    CUSTOM[Custom Extension - Vendor Specific Opcode]

    BASE --> M_EXT
    BASE --> A_EXT
    BASE --> C_EXT
    BASE --> F_EXT
    BASE --> V_EXT
    BASE --> CUSTOM
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **fan-out from `BASE`** illustrates the **mix-and-match** philosophy. A vendor building an AI-focused MCU would combine `RV32I + M + C + V` (integer, multiply, compressed, vector).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> On Module 4 questions, students most commonly **lose 2–3 marks** for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing TinyML with embedded ML in general.** TinyML is *strictly* the sub-1 mW MCU class; laptops, smartphones, and Edge TPUs are **NOT** TinyML.
> 2. **Listing frameworks without naming the compression technique** (e.g., writing "TensorFlow Lite" instead of "Int8 post-training quantization"). Always **name the algorithm**.
> 3. **Mixing up RISC-V extensions.** `M` is multiply, `F` is float, `C` is compressed, `V` is vector. The board will **deduct 1 mark** per swapped pair.
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers. A model size of "1.2" with no KB/MB tag is **marked wrong**.
> 5. **Forgetting the feedback loop** in digital-twin diagrams. The "actuation" arrow is **mandatory** to call it a true twin.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Define TinyML. List any two popular TinyML frameworks and two hardware platforms.**

**Model Answer:**
TinyML is the field of running machine-learning inference on **microcontrollers consuming less than ~1 mW of average power**, with model footprints below **~1 MB SRAM** and **inference latencies under ~100 ms** while running on battery. It enables on-device intelligence at the extreme edge, eliminating cloud round-trips.

**Two popular frameworks:**
1. **TensorFlow Lite Micro (TFLM)** — Google's optimized interpreter, ~16 KB core footprint, no dynamic memory allocation.
2. **PyTorch Mobile / ExecuTorch** — Meta's runtime for deploying PyTorch models to ARM Cortex-M and RISC-V MCUs.

**Two hardware platforms:**
1. **ARM Cortex-M55 + Ethos-U55 microNPU** — provides ~0.5 TOPS at <50 mW.
2. **MAX78000 / MAX78002 (Analog Devices)** — integrated CNN accelerator with Cortex-M4F host.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**What is the RISC-V instruction-set architecture? List any two of its key advantages for embedded systems.**

**Model Answer:**
RISC-V is an **open-standard, modular instruction-set architecture (ISA)** governed by RISC-V International. It defines a small mandatory base (e.g., RV32I) and a family of optional standard extensions (M, A, F, D, C, V) that vendors may mix freely. Unlike ARM or x86, RISC-V is **royalty-free** and **open-source**.

**Two key advantages:**
1. **Royalty-free licensing** — eliminates per-core royalties, dramatically reducing unit cost for high-volume embedded deployments.
2. **Custom user-defined extensions** — designers can add domain-specific instructions (e.g., a single-cycle matrix-multiply opcode for ML) without external permission, enabling **tight hardware-software co-design**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B** (full 14 marks), with each having two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### ❑ Question A — (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain the concept of Edge AI and TinyML. Discuss the role of model compression (pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation) in enabling TinyML on resource-constrained MCUs.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Edge AI Overview (2 marks)**
Edge AI is the paradigm of executing **artificial-intelligence inference** on devices located at the **physical edge** of the network, where data is generated, rather than on centralized cloud servers. The driving forces are: latency-critical control loops, bandwidth scarcity, privacy regulations, and energy constraints. Edge AI is a *superset* that includes:
- **TinyML** — MCU-class (sub-1 mW, <1 MB SRAM)
- **Embedded ML** — Cortex-A class, <1 GB RAM
- **On-device AI** — mobile SoCs with dedicated NPUs

**2. TinyML Definition and Targets (1 mark)**
TinyML is the **MCUs-only** subset of Edge AI, where inference must fit within **<256 KB SRAM** for activations and **<1 MB Flash** for weights, consuming **<1 mW average power**, and running on **coin-cell or energy-harvested** power.

**3. Model Compression Techniques (3 marks)**

- **Pruning** — Removes redundant connections (weights near zero) from a trained network. *Unstructured pruning* zeros individual weights; *structured pruning* removes entire neurons/filters for hardware-friendly speedup. Typical compression: 5–10× with <1% accuracy loss.

- **Quantization** — Reduces the **numeric precision** of weights and activations. Most common is **post-training Int8 quantization** (4× compression). Advanced schemes include **Int4** (8×) and **binary** (32×) networks. Quantization-aware training (QAT) preserves accuracy better than post-training quantization.

- **Knowledge Distillation** — Trains a small **student** network to mimic the **soft outputs** of a large pre-trained **teacher**. The student learns the teacher's generalization behavior and often surpasses a directly-trained model of equal size.

**4. Hardware-Software Co-Design (1 mark)**
Modern TinyML toolchains (**TFLM, TVM-Micro, ExecuTorch, ONNX Runtime Micro**) generate C arrays, perform operator fusion, and exploit hardware accelerators like **Arm Ethos-U55**, **Ceva-NeuPro**, or **MAX78000 CNN engines** to meet the stringent energy budget.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a RISC-V-based embedded system. Discuss the role of standard extensions (M, A, F, C, V) and highlight any two advantages of RISC-V over ARM Cortex-M for modern embedded applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. RISC-V Architecture Block Diagram (3 marks)**

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Fetch Stage - PC and Instruction Cache]
    D1[Decode Stage - Compressed 16 bit and Standard 32 bit]
    R1[Register File - 32 Integer Registers]
    EX1[Execution Units - ALU and Multiply Divide]
    LSU1[Load Store Unit - Atomic Instructions]
    VPU1[Vector Unit - Optional RISC V V Extension]
    FPU1[FPU - Optional RISC V F or D]
    CSR1[CSR and Trap Logic - MMode and UMode]
    BUS1[Bus Interface - AXI or AHB or TileLink]
    F1 --> D1
    D1 --> EX1
    EX1 --> R1
    R1 --> LSU1
    R1 --> VPU1
    R1 --> FPU1
    EX1 --> CSR1
    LSU1 --> BUS1
    VPU1 --> BUS1
    FPU1 --> BUS1
```

**2. Standard Extensions (2 marks)**

| Extension | Function | Embedded Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **M** | Integer multiply/divide | General-purpose arithmetic |
| **A** | Atomic instructions | RTOS mutex / lock-free IPC |
| **F / D** | Single / double float | Sensor fusion, motor control |
| **C** | 16-bit compressed encoding | **25–30% code-density gain** |
| **V** | Vector processing | TinyML matrix-multiply acceleration |

**3. Advantages of RISC-V over ARM Cortex-M (2 marks)**

- **Royalty-free licensing** — In high-volume IoT, ARM's per-core royalty becomes a non-trivial BOM fraction; RISC-V eliminates it.
- **Domain-specific custom instructions** — Vendors (e.g., adding a single-cycle AES or MAC opcode) can differentiate their silicon without external approval — impossible with ARM's closed ISA.

---

### ❑ Question B — (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain the concept of Neuromorphic Computing. Describe the architecture of Intel Loihi 2 and discuss its suitability for embedded edge-AI applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Neuromorphic Computing (2 marks)**
Neuromorphic computing refers to hardware that **emulates the structural and dynamic properties of biological neurons and synapses** using silicon. Unlike traditional von-Neumann processors, neuromorphic chips are **event-driven**, use **spikes** (binary, asynchronous events) instead of continuous activations, and perform **in-memory computation** to bypass the memory-wall bottleneck. The brain consumes ~**20 W** for ~$10^{16}$ operations/sec — a **GOPS/W** that classical silicon cannot approach.

**2. Intel Loihi 2 Architecture (3 marks)**
Loihi 2 is Intel's second-generation neuromorphic research chip, fabricated on **Intel 4 process**, containing **128 neuro-cores** interconnected by a **mesh NoC**:
- Each neuro-core integrates **1,024 leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF)** neurons.
- **On-chip learning** is supported via a programmable **spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP)** engine.
- **Sparse, event-driven** activity yields ultra-low standby power.
- The chip exposes a **Python-based programming model (Lava / NxSDK)** for SNN development.

**3. Suitability for Embedded Edge AI (2 marks)**
- **Ultra-low power** — only active neurons consume dynamic power; idle regions draw ~zero.
- **Real-time, low-latency** — spike propagation is on the order of **microseconds**, ideal for control loops.
- **Inherent temporal processing** — SNNs naturally encode time, making them ideal for **audio, vibration, and event-camera** data without explicit recurrence.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Discuss the emerging trends in Embedded Cybersecurity. Explain the concepts of Secure Boot, Hardware Root of Trust, PUF, and post-quantum cryptography with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Why Embedded Security is Trending (1 mark)**
With **50+ billion IoT devices** deployed, attack surfaces have exploded. Recent regulations (EU Cyber Resilience Act, US IoT Cybersecurity Improvement Act) now **mandate** hardware-rooted security. Software-only patches are no longer sufficient.

**2. Secure Boot (1 mark)**
Secure Boot is a **boot-time integrity check** where immutable ROM/PUF code verifies the cryptographic signature of every subsequent bootloader and application image before execution. An invalid signature halts the boot, preventing tampered firmware from running.

**3. Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT) (2 marks)**
A **Hardware Root of Trust** is an immutable, tamper-resistant silicon primitive that anchors all higher-level security. Examples:
- **Arm TrustZone-M** on Cortex-M33/M55 — secure / non-secure world isolation.
- **NXP SE050 / Infineon OPTIGA Trust M** — discrete secure elements.
- **TPM 2.0** — standardized cryptographic module for industrial PCs and gateways.

**4. Physical Unclonable Functions (PUF) (1.5 marks)**
A PUF exploits **manufacturing-time silicon variability** to produce a unique, unclonable bit pattern per chip. PUFs are used to **derive cryptographic keys on demand** without storing them in NVM, eliminating key-extraction attacks. Reliability is quantified by **intra-Hamming-distance entropy**.

**5. Post-Quantum Cryptography (1.5 marks)**
Classical RSA / ECC will be **broken by quantum computers** (Shor's algorithm). NIST has standardized **CRYSTALS-Kyber** (key encapsulation) and **CRYSTALS-Dilithium** (digital signatures) as PQC standards. Embedded systems designed today must be **crypto-agile** — capable of swapping in PQC primitives as firmware updates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **TinyML** is MCU-class (sub-1 mW, <1 MB SRAM); distinguish from general Edge AI.
- ✅ **Three TinyML compression techniques** to memorize: **Pruning, Quantization (Int8 → 4×), Knowledge Distillation**.
- ✅ **TFLM** is the most common TinyML runtime; **xxd -i** converts a `.tflite` to a C array.
- ✅ **RISC-V extensions**: **M** = multiply, **A** = atomic, **F** = float, **C** = compressed, **V** = vector — know them cold.
- ✅ **RISC-V advantage**: royalty-free + custom user-defined instructions.
- ✅ **Neuromorphic computing** = **spiking** neural networks + in-memory compute + event-driven; **Loihi 2** and **Akida** are reference chips.
- ✅ **AIoT** = AI + IoT; flagship technique is **Federated Learning** (privacy-preserving weight aggregation).
- ✅ **Digital Twin** has a **closed actuation loop**; without it, it is only a *digital shadow*.
- ✅ **Embedded Security** trend: **Secure Boot + HRoT (TrustZone-M / SE050) + PUF + PQC (Kyber / Dilithium)**.
- ✅ **5G URLLC** latency target = **1 ms** one-way; control loops must stay under this.
- ✅ **GOPS/W** is the figure-of-merit for AI accelerators; modern Edge AI ASICs reach **> 50 GOPS/W**.
- ✅ **Energy harvesting** enables battery-less IoT: $P_{\text{del}} = P_{\text{src}} \cdot A \cdot \eta_{\text{cell}} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}}$.
- ✅ **AUTOSAR Adaptive** (vs. Classic) is the trend for high-performance automotive ECUs (ADAS).
- ✅ **IoMT** = Internet of Medical Things; **continuous glucose monitors** and **implantable defibrillators** are canonical examples.
- ✅ Always **include units** in TinyML size / battery-life numerical answers; a missing "KB" loses a mark.
- ✅ In digital-twin diagrams, the **actuation arrow is mandatory** to earn full credit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain a recent trend," always structure your answer as **(i) Definition → (ii) Architecture/Diagram → (iii) Quantitative Benefit → (iv) Real-World Example**. This four-part structure is the **scoring template** used by KTU 2024 scheme valuators and guarantees full marks when executed cleanly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Embedded Systems in IT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT637 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND ITS APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Embedded Systems in IT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Embedded System** is a purpose-built, dedicated computing subsystem designed to perform one or a limited set of pre-defined functions, often with **real-time computing constraints**, and which is integrated as a part of a larger mechanical, electrical, or information technology (IT) infrastructure. Unlike a general-purpose computer, an embedded system is **resource-constrained**, application-specific, and tightly coupled to the physical world it serves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Reference):** As per the PECAT637 syllabus, an embedded system is *"a combination of computer hardware and software — fixed in capability and programmable — that is designed for a specific function within a larger system. It is part of an IT-enabled ecosystem that may include networking, cloud interfaces, sensors, and actuation elements."*

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a modern **smart office building**:

- The **central server room** (general-purpose computer) handles payroll, email, browsing — many unrelated tasks.
- But the **fire alarm panel on every floor** is a *dedicated embedded system*. It only does one job: read smoke sensors, decide if there's a fire, and trigger the alarm. It cannot be reprogrammed to handle email. It runs **24/7**, responds in **milliseconds**, and never complains about updates.

In **Information Technology (IT)**, embedded systems are the *silent workers* behind:
- Data center cooling controllers
- Network routers and switches
- RAID storage controllers
- Biometric attendance systems
- POS terminals
- ATM machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Every time you use a *smart* IT service — like scanning a QR code to pay, swiping an RFID card, or accessing a cloud dashboard from your phone — there is an embedded system somewhere in the chain performing a specific, dedicated task.

---

### Standard Embedded System Metrics (KTU Highlight)

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Response Latency** | **µs to ms** | Hard/soft real-time bound |
| **Power Budget** | **mW to W** | Often battery/POE-powered |
| **MTBF** | **> 100,000 hours** | Must be highly reliable |
| **Clock Frequency** | **kHz to GHz** | Tuned to application |
| **Memory Footprint** | **KB to MB** | Tight resource constraint |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is an embedded system NOT a general-purpose computer?"* — Answer using three keywords: **dedicated, resource-constrained, real-time**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of an Embedded System in IT

An embedded system deployed in an IT context is built on three foundational pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Hardware Layer**
- **Microcontroller / Microprocessor** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V)
- **Memory:** ROM (firmware), RAM (runtime), Flash (logs)
- **I/O Interfaces:** GPIO, UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, Ethernet
- **Sensors & Actuators** (in IT: temperature probes, smart cards, fingerprint readers)
- **Power Supply & Regulation Circuits**

**Pillar 2 — Software Layer**
- **Bare-metal firmware** (no OS) for ultra-low-end devices
- **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** — e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, Zephyr
- **Middleware:** TCP/IP stacks, MQTT brokers, TLS libraries
- **Application logic** written in C, C++, or Rust

**Pillar 3 — Connectivity & IT Integration Layer**
- **Wired:** Ethernet, RS-485, Modbus, USB
- **Wireless:** Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, NFC
- **Cloud/Edge interfaces:** REST APIs, MQTT, CoAP, OPC-UA
- **Security:** TPM, Secure Boot, Hardware Root of Trust

---

### 2.2 Why "Embedded in IT" Is a Distinct Concept

The phrase **"Embedded Systems in IT"** refers to embedding computational intelligence **inside IT infrastructure** — not just inside consumer electronics. This includes:

1. **Network Equipment:** Routers, switches, firewalls, SD-WAN gateways
2. **Data Center Equipment:** Server BMCs (Baseboard Management Controllers), PDUs, smart cooling
3. **End-Point Devices:** Smart card readers, biometric scanners, ruggedized tablets
4. **Edge Gateways:** Industrial IoT gateways, protocol converters
5. **Cybersecurity Hardware:** HSMs (Hardware Security Modules), token authenticators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction to Remember:** An *embedded system in consumer electronics* controls a washing machine. An *embedded system in IT* controls a **rack-mounted switch in a data center** — same engineering principles, but the IT version demands **higher availability, network-aware security, and remote management**.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| IT Domain | Embedded System Role |
|---|---|
| **Cloud Computing** | BMC chips for out-of-band server management (IPMI) |
| **Networking** | ASICs/SoCs in switches (e.g., Broadcom Tomahawk) |
| **Cybersecurity** | TPM 2.0 chips for disk encryption keys |
| **Smart Offices** | BACnet controllers for HVAC |
| **Logistics IT** | RFID gateways in warehouses |
| **Healthcare IT** | Patient monitor gateways sending HL7 data to EHR |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although this is a conceptual topic, the following quantitative relationships frequently appear in KTU 2024 exam questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{dynamic}} &= \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f \\
P_{\text{static}} &= I_{\text{leak}} \cdot V \\
P_{\text{total}} &= P_{\text{dynamic}} + P_{\text{static}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Total Useful Work}}{\text{Time Interval}} \quad [\text{ops/sec}]
$$

$$
\text{Utilization} = \frac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{period}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{CPU Load} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}}{1 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}} \quad \text{(Rate Monotonic Bound)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **RTOS Scheduling Bound (Liu & Layland):** For *n* periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling, the system is **schedulable** only if:
> $$U \leq n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$
> For *n* = 1 → 100%, *n* = 2 → **82.8%**, *n* → ∞ → **69.3%** (Liu & Layland's classical bound).

#### Boundary Conditions & Symbol Glossary

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Switching activity factor | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| $C$ | Load capacitance | Farads (F) |
| $V$ | Supply voltage | Volts (V) |
| $f$ | Clock frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| $I_{\text{leak}}$ | Leakage current | Amperes (A) |
| $C_i$ | Worst-case execution time of task $i$ | seconds (s) |
| $T_i$ | Period of task $i$ | seconds (s) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Dynamic Power Dominates in Modern IT Embedded Systems

Modern IT embedded systems (e.g., a 1 GHz network switch ASIC) operate at high frequencies. We derive why the dynamic power term dominates.

**Step 1 — Start with the energy stored in a capacitor.**

When a CMOS gate switches, the load capacitance $C$ is charged to supply voltage $V$. The energy stored per switch is:

$$E_{\text{charge}} = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Account for the discharge.**

When the gate switches back, the same energy is dissipated as heat in the NMOS transistor:

$$E_{\text{discharge}} = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy per switching cycle.**

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = E_{\text{charge}} + E_{\text{discharge}} = C V^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by switching rate.**

If the gate switches $\alpha \cdot f$ times per second (where $\alpha \le 1$ is the activity factor), the average dynamic power is:

$$P_{\text{dynamic}} = E_{\text{cycle}} \cdot \alpha \cdot f = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$$

**Step 5 — Add the static (leakage) power.**

Modern sub-100 nm CMOS processes have non-negligible subthreshold leakage:

$$P_{\text{static}} = I_{\text{leak}} \cdot V$$

**Step 6 — Total system power.**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{total}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f + I_{\text{leak}} \cdot V}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

- Power scales **quadratically with voltage** → justifies *dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS)* in IT servers and BMCs.
- Power scales **linearly with frequency** → motivates clock gating during idle IT workloads.
- A typical IT SoC at $V = 1.0\,\text{V}$, $f = 1\,\text{GHz}$, $C_{\text{eff}} = 10\,\text{nF}$ consumes on the order of **watts** of dynamic power — explaining the need for **active cooling in data centers**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Rate Monotonic Schedulability)

A network management embedded system has three periodic tasks, all critical for IT infrastructure monitoring:

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ (heartbeat) | 1 ms | 5 ms |
| $\tau_2$ (log flush) | 2 ms | 10 ms |
| $\tau_3$ (SNMP poll) | 3 ms | 15 ms |

**Step 1 — Compute utilization for each task.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{3}{15} = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization.**

$$U_{\text{total}} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.60 = 60\%$$

**Step 3 — Liu & Layland bound for n = 3.**

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 \approx 0.7797 \approx 78\%$$

**Step 4 — Schedulability decision.**

$$U_{\text{total}} = 0.60 \;\le\; U_{\text{bound}} = 0.78 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{SCHEDULABLE}$$

**Step 5 — Remaining slack (engineering margin).**

$$\text{Slack} = 0.78 - 0.60 = 0.18 = 18\%$$

This 18% slack is the *engineering headroom* available for adding future tasks (e.g., a new security audit task) without violating real-time deadlines — a key concept in IT system design.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** They may ask you to verify schedulability using *both* the Liu-Layland bound *and* a response-time analysis. The Liu-Layland bound is *sufficient but not necessary* — a system can be schedulable even with $U > U_{\text{bound}}$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Embedded vs. General-Purpose in IT

| Parameter | Embedded System in IT | General-Purpose IT Server |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Single, fixed function | Multi-purpose, many workloads |
| **OS** | Bare-metal or RTOS | Full OS (Linux, Windows Server) |
| **User Interaction** | Often none (headless) | Rich (GUI, CLI) |
| **Power Budget** | mW to few Watts | 100 W to kW |
| **Update Frequency** | Months to years | Days to hours |
| **Cost** | $1 – $500 | $1,000 – $100,000+ |
| **Failures** | Must self-recover or reboot | Often human intervention |
| **Real-Time?** | Frequently yes | Rarely (best-effort) |
| **Examples** | BMC, smart NIC, RFID reader | Web server, DB server, mail server |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Block Diagram of an Embedded System in an IT Context

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical IT-grade embedded system — such as a smart network gateway or an industrial IoT edge node.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Power Supply Unit] --> MCU
    SENSOR[Sensor Array] --> MCU
    ACT[Actuator / Relay Output] --> MCU

    MCU[Microcontroller or SoC] --> MEM1[ROM Firmware]
    MCU --> MEM2[Flash Storage]
    MCU --> MEM3[RAM Runtime]

    MCU --> IO1[UART Debug]
    MCU --> IO2[SPI Bus]
    MCU --> IO3[I2C Bus]
    MCU --> IO4[GPIO Bank]

    IO2 --> ETH[Ethernet PHY]
    IO3 --> WIFI[WiFi Module]
    IO2 --> BLE[BLE Radio]

    ETH --> NET[Enterprise Network]
    WIFI --> NET
    BLE --> MOBILE[Mobile IT Asset]

    NET --> CLOUD[Cloud Management Plane]
    CLOUD --> DASH[IT Admin Dashboard]

    MCU --> SEC[TPM Secure Element]
    MCU --> WDT[Hardware Watchdog Timer]
    MCU --> RTC[Real Time Clock]
```

#### Reading the Diagram

- **Vertical stack (left):** Power and physical-world I/O — the *body* of the embedded device.
- **Middle column:** The microcontroller, memory, and I/O buses — the *brain and memory*.
- **Right column:** Communication channels into the IT ecosystem — *the voice* of the device.
- **Top-right:** The cloud and admin dashboard — *the remote overseer*.
- **Bottom row:** Security, watchdog, and RTC — *the guardians* of reliability.

---

### 4.2 Layered Reference Model (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L5[Application Layer: Domain Logic]
    L4[Middleware Layer: MQTT HTTP TLS]
    L3[RTOS Layer: FreeRTOS Zephyr]
    L2[HAL Layer: Driver Abstraction]
    L1[Hardware Layer: MCU Memory I/O]

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

| Layer | Function | Example in IT Embedded System |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Hardware** | Physical silicon and interconnects | STM32, NXP i.MX, RPi CM4 |
| **L2 — HAL** | Register-level abstraction | GPIO toggle, SPI DMA |
| **L3 — RTOS** | Task scheduling, semaphores, queues | FreeRTOS, Zephyr |
| **L4 — Middleware** | Networking, security, IPC | lwIP, mbedTLS, MQTT-C |
| **L5 — Application** | Domain logic, business rules | SNMP agent, Modbus slave, REST server |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an embedded system. How does it differ from a general-purpose computer in an IT environment?*

**Model Answer:**

An **embedded system** is a dedicated computing system designed to perform a specific function within a larger IT system, with constraints on power, cost, and real-time response.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Embedded System | General-Purpose Computer |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Single, fixed | Multi-purpose |
| **OS** | RTOS / bare-metal | Full OS (Linux/Windows) |
| **Resource** | Constrained (KB memory) | Abundant (GB memory) |
| **Real-time** | Often hard real-time | Best-effort |
| **Example in IT** | Network switch SoC | Enterprise database server |

*(3 marks — definition 1, comparison 1, IT-specific example 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any six characteristics of an embedded system used in IT infrastructure.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Dedicated functionality** — performs a single, well-defined task
2. **Real-time constraints** — must respond within deadline
3. **Resource constrained** — limited CPU, memory, power
4. **High reliability** — MTBF often > 100,000 hours
5. **Tight HW-SW integration** — software is "baked in"
6. **Connectivity to IT networks** — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, MQTT, REST
7. **Security-aware** — TPM, secure boot
8. **Low power** — typically mW to few W

*(3 marks — 6 correct points, 0.5 each)*

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **three-pillar architecture** of an embedded system in IT, with suitable examples for each pillar. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Two periodic tasks in an IT edge gateway are: $\tau_1$ with $C_1 = 1$ ms, $T_1 = 4$ ms, and $\tau_2$ with $C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 8$ ms. Determine if the system is **schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Pillar Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Hardware Pillar** — Microcontroller/SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4), memory (Flash + SRAM), I/O interfaces (UART, SPI, I²C), power regulation. *IT example:* A BMC chip in a server uses an ARM Cortex-M0 to monitor voltage, temperature, and fan RPM.

2. **Software Pillar** — Firmware in ROM, RTOS for multitasking, middleware (TCP/IP, TLS), application logic. *IT example:* A network switch runs VxWorks RTOS with an L2 forwarding application.

3. **Connectivity & IT Integration Pillar** — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, BLE, REST/MQTT, OPC-UA, cloud SDK. *IT example:* A smart PDU publishes power metrics to a cloud dashboard via MQTT.

Each pillar tightly integrates with the next — hardware exposes registers, HAL abstracts them, RTOS schedules, middleware connects, and application implements the IT business logic.

**(b) Schedulability Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute utilizations:**

$$U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

$$U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$U_{\text{total}} = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**Step 3 — Liu-Layland bound for n = 2:**

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 2 \cdot \left(2^{1/2} - 1\right) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.4142 \approx 0.8284 \approx 82.8\%$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$U_{\text{total}} = 0.50 \;\le\; 0.8284 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{SYSTEM IS SCHEDULABLE}$$

**Step 5 — Response-time verification (optional, for full marks):**

For $\tau_1$: $R_1 = C_1 = 1$ ms $\le T_1 = 4$ ms ✓

For $\tau_2$: $R_2 = C_2 + \lceil R_2 / T_1 \rceil \cdot C_1$
- Iter 1: $R_2^{(1)} = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3$ ms
- Iter 2: $R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$ ms ✓
- $R_2 = 3$ ms $\le T_2 = 8$ ms ✓

**Conclusion:** The system is schedulable with **32.8% headroom** for future tasks.

---

#### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**
*(a)* With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an embedded system in IT** and explain the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* An IT-grade smart sensor uses a microcontroller operating at $V = 3.3$ V, $f = 72$ MHz, $C_{\text{eff}} = 5$ nF, and $\alpha = 0.1$. Calculate the **dynamic power dissipation**. If the device must run on a battery rated **2000 mAh at 3.3 V**, estimate the **battery life** assuming dynamic power dominates. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Roles (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Role of each block:**

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| **Power Supply** | Provides regulated DC to MCU and peripherals |
| **Sensors** | Convert physical phenomena (temp, humidity, motion) to electrical signals |
| **MCU / SoC** | Core processor executing firmware and RTOS |
| **Memory (ROM/Flash/RAM)** | Store firmware code, logs, and runtime data |
| **I/O Buses (UART/SPI/I²C/GPIO)** | Connect peripherals to MCU |
| **Communication Modules (ETH/Wi-Fi/BLE)** | Provide wired/wireless IT connectivity |
| **TPM / Secure Element** | Hardware-rooted security keys, secure boot |
| **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** | Resets the system if firmware hangs |
| **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** | Maintains wall-clock time even in sleep |
| **Cloud/Network** | Remote management and data ingestion |

**(b) Power & Battery Life Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**

- $V = 3.3$ V
- $f = 72 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- $C_{\text{eff}} = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ F
- $\alpha = 0.1$

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic power:**

$$P_{\text{dynamic}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$P_{\text{dynamic}} = 0.1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (3.3)^{2} \cdot 72 \times 10^{6}$$

$$P_{\text{dynamic}} = 0.1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 10.89 \cdot 72 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify stepwise:**

$$0.1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$5 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 10.89 = 5.445 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$5.445 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 72 \times 10^{6} = 5.445 \cdot 72 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$5.445 \cdot 72 = 392.04$$

$$P_{\text{dynamic}} = 392.04 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W} = 0.392\,\text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Compute battery energy:**

$$E_{\text{battery}} = V \cdot Q = 3.3 \cdot 2000 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Ah} = 6.6\,\text{Wh}$$

**Step 5 — Compute battery life:**

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{E_{\text{battery}}}{P_{\text{dynamic}}} = \frac{6.6}{0.392} \approx 16.84\,\text{hours}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{life}} \approx 16.8\,\text{hours}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to convert $C$ to Farads, $f$ to Hz, $Q$ to Ah — these cost **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing energy vs. charge:** Battery life = $E/P$, NOT $Q/I$. Many students wrongly write "$T = Q/I$" and lose a mark.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU requires *all* working to be shown. A one-line final answer earns only **1 mark** out of 7.
> 4. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$:** If omitted, the answer is **10× too large** — a classic trap.
> 5. **Confusing RTOS bounds:** The Liu-Layland bound is a *sufficient* condition, not necessary. If a question says "verify," show both the bound and a response-time iteration for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Introduction to Embedded Systems in IT:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Embedded system = dedicated, real-time, resource-constrained, single-purpose computer inside a larger system.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Hardware + Software + Connectivity/IT-integration.
- ✅ **Key IT examples:** BMCs, smart NICs, network switch SoCs, RFID readers, HSMs, edge gateways.
- ✅ **Dynamic power equation:** $P = \alpha C V^{2} f$ — scales **quadratically** with voltage.
- ✅ **Liu-Layland RTOS bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; for $n=2$, bound = **82.8%**; for $n \to \infty$, bound = **69.3%**.
- ✅ **Schedulability test:** $U_{\text{total}} \le U_{\text{bound}} \Rightarrow$ schedulable.
- ✅ **MTBF target:** > **100,000 hours** for IT infrastructure embedded devices.
- ✅ **Real-time types:** Hard (miss = catastrophic) vs. Soft (miss = degraded quality).
- ✅ **RTOS examples:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, Zephyr — all common in IT.
- ✅ **Communication protocols in IT:** MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/REST, OPC-UA, Modbus, SNMP.
- ✅ **Difference vs. IoT:** Embedded is the *node*; IoT is the *network of nodes* plus cloud.
- ✅ **Common KTU question patterns:** (1) Define + compare, (2) Draw + label block diagram, (3) Compute schedulability, (4) Compute power and battery life.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT and Smart Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT637 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND ITS APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT and Smart Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internet of Things (IoT):** The *Internet of Things (IoT)* is a network of physical objects — **"Things"** — embedded with **sensors, software, microcontrollers, actuators**, and other technologies for the purpose of **sensing, computing, and communicating data** over the internet without requiring human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart Device:** A *smart device* is an **electronic device** that can be **connected, sensed, and actuated** through wireless communication protocols. It generally connects to a **cloud server**, an **edge node**, or another smart device to provide **context-aware, autonomous, and adaptive services** to the end user.

According to the **KTU 2024 PECAT637 syllabus (Module 4)**, IoT integrates:
- **Embedded Systems** (the "thing")
- **Connectivity** (the "internet")
- **Data Analytics & Cloud Services** (the "intelligence")

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Human Body"**
> Think of the human body as a giant IoT system. Your **eyes and skin** are *sensors* collecting data from the environment. Your **brain (microcontroller + cloud)** processes that data. Your **hands and legs** are *actuators* that act on a decision. Your **nervous system (Wi-Fi, BLE, MQTT)** carries the signals. Just as your body reacts automatically to heat (you pull your hand away), an IoT smart device reacts automatically to sensor input (a smart AC turning on when a temperature sensor crosses 30°C).

The **core philosophy** of IoT is captured in the famous equation:

$$
\text{IoT} = \text{Physical Object} + \text{Controller/Sensor/Actuator} + \text{Internet Connectivity}
$$

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold Standards)

| Parameter | Standard / Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency (ISM Bands) | **2.4 GHz**, **868/915 MHz**, **5 GHz** |
| Power Consumption (Battery IoT) | **µW to mW** range |
| Data Rate | **kbps (LoRa)** to **Mbps (Wi-Fi)** |
| Latency Target | **< 1 s** for control loops, **< 100 ms** for real-time |
| IPv6 Address Space | **2¹²⁸** unique IDs (6LoWPAN standard) |

---

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of IoT (KTU 2024)

1. **Sensing** — converting physical phenomena → electrical signals.
2. **Processing** — running algorithms on microcontrollers / edge devices.
3. **Connectivity** — wireless / wired transmission.
4. **Actuation / Action** — triggering real-world changes (motors, relays, LEDs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of IoT (5-Layer Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus follows the **standard 5-layer IoT reference architecture**:

### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer
- **Hardware:** Temperature (LM35, DHT22), Humidity, Gas (MQ-2/MQ-135), PIR Motion, Accelerometer (MPU6050), Camera, LDR.
- **Function:** Analog/digital capture of environmental data.
- **Key Parameter:** Sampling rate, resolution (bits), SNR.

### Layer 2 — Network / Connectivity Layer
- **Short Range:** BLE, ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), NFC.
- **Long Range:** LoRaWAN, SigFox, NB-IoT, LTE-M.
- **Wired:** Ethernet, RS-485, CAN, Modbus.
- **Function:** Reliable transport of data packets.

### Layer 3 — Middleware / Processing Layer
- **Hardware:** Gateway (Raspberry Pi, ESP32, edge servers).
- **Software:** MQTT brokers, CoAP servers, database caches, local analytics.

### Layer 4 — Application Layer
- **Examples:** Smart Home (Alexa), Smart Agriculture, Industrial IoT (IIoT), Healthcare Wearables.

### Layer 5 — Business Layer
- **Function:** Data analytics, AI/ML inference, dashboards, billing models.

---

## 2.2 Core Communication Protocols (Theory & Role)

| Protocol | Layer (OSI) | Topology | Data Rate | Power | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11n)** | Data Link / PHY | Star | up to **600 Mbps** | High | Home gateways, IP cameras |
| **BLE 5.0** | Data Link | Star | **2 Mbps** | Very Low | Wearables, beacons |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | Network/MAC | Mesh | **250 kbps** | Low | Smart lighting, sensor mesh |
| **LoRaWAN** | MAC | Star-of-stars | **0.3 – 50 kbps** | Ultra Low | Smart agriculture, metering |
| **MQTT** | App (TCP) | Pub/Sub | N/A | Low | Cloud telemetry |
| **CoAP** | App (UDP) | Req/Resp | N/A | Low | Constrained devices |
| **NB-IoT** | Cellular | Star | **200 kbps** | Low | Smart cities, parking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - **MQTT** = **TCP-based**, **Publish/Subscribe**, broker-mediated. Used in **reliable** cloud telemetry.
> - **CoAP** = **UDP-based**, **Request/Response**, REST-like. Used in **constrained** devices where TCP overhead is too heavy.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use **\vert** for absolute values in formulas. Pipes break markdown tables, so LaTeX is the only safe form.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** (sensor ADC) | Hz |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1)$ | ADC resolution (n-bit) | codes |
| 3 | $T_{link} = \dfrac{P_{payload}}{R_{b}}$ | Transmission time per packet | s |
| 4 | $E_{total} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + P_{rx} \cdot t_{rx} + P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}$ | Energy model of IoT node | Joules |
| 5 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 6 | $PL_d = PL_{d_0} + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | Log-distance path loss (n = path loss exponent) | dB |
| 7 | $D_{duty} = \dfrac{t_{active}}{t_{active}+t_{sleep}}$ | Duty cycle (battery life) | % |
| 8 | $C = \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{A}{d}$ | Capacitance of a parallel-plate sensor | Farads |

### Real-World Utility
- **Equation 1** governs every **digital microphone** and **ADC** choice in an IoT node.
- **Equation 4** is the basis of **battery-life estimation** in LoRa/BLE nodes (e.g., why a soil sensor lasts 5 years on a coin cell).
- **Equation 6** dictates **transmitter power planning** in smart agriculture deployments.

---

## 2.4 Smart Devices — Anatomy

A **smart device** typically contains the following building blocks (mapped to KTU Module 1-3 of this course):

1. **MCU / SoC** — ESP32, STM32, nRF52840, CC2538.
2. **Sensor Front-End** — analog conditioning + ADC.
3. **Actuator Driver** — MOSFET, H-bridge, relay, PWM-controlled.
4. **Radio Module** — integrated or external (e.g., SX1276 for LoRa).
5. **Power Subsystem** — LDO / buck converter + battery.
6. **Firmware / RTOS** — FreeRTOS, Zephyr, Contiki-NG, RIOT.
7. **Security Block** — Secure Element (ATECC608B), AES-128/256, TLS 1.3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Rate from a Temperature Sensor

> **Problem (Worked Example):** A soil moisture sensor produces a sinusoidal output whose highest meaningful frequency component is **0.5 Hz** (slow wet/dry cycles). Determine the minimum ADC sampling frequency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the Nyquist criterion.**
According to the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem, the sampling frequency must be **at least twice** the maximum input frequency to avoid aliasing.

$$
f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given value.**

$$
f_s \ge 2 \times 0.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the result.**

$$
f_s \ge 1 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering margin.**
To provide a **safety margin of 2×**, we choose $f_s = 2$ Hz in the firmware.

$$
\boxed{f_s = 2 \text{ Hz} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_s = 0.5 \text{ s per sample}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always state the *Nyquist* theorem first. KTU valuations award marks for the *criterion*, not just the *number*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Life Estimation for a LoRa Node

> **Problem:** A LoRa soil sensor is active for **t_tx = 0.5 s** at **P_tx = 120 mA**, receives for **t_rx = 0.4 s** at **P_rx = 40 mA**, and sleeps for **t_sleep = 600 s** at **P_sleep = 5 µA**. The node runs on a **3.0 V, 2400 mAh** Li-SOCl₂ cell. Estimate the **average current** and **lifetime in years**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the period.**

$$
T_{cycle} = t_{tx} + t_{rx} + t_{sleep} = 0.5 + 0.4 + 600 = 600.9 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average current using the energy model (Eq. 4 in cheat sheet).**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{tx}\cdot t_{tx} + I_{rx}\cdot t_{rx} + I_{sleep}\cdot t_{sleep}}{T_{cycle}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numeric values.**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{(120)(0.5) + (40)(0.4) + (5\times 10^{-3})(600)}{600.9}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator simplification.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 60 + 16 + 3 = 79 \text{ mA}\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the average current.**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{79 \text{ mA}\cdot\text{s}}{600.9 \text{ s}} \approx 0.1315 \text{ mA} = 131.5 \text{ µA}
$$

**Step 6 — Battery life in hours.**

$$
t_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{0.1315 \text{ mA}} \approx 18{,}250 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to years.**

$$
t_{life} = \dfrac{18{,}250}{24 \times 365} \approx 2.08 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{life} \approx 2.08 \text{ years per single Li-SOCl}_2 \text{ cell}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ADC Quantization Resolution

> **Problem:** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V is used by a **smart gas sensor**. Determine the **voltage step** (LSB size) and the **digital code** corresponding to a sensor output of **1.85 V**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — LSB size (voltage resolution).**

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{3.3}{4095}
$$

**Step 2 — Numeric result.**

$$
\Delta V \approx 0.000806 \text{ V} = 0.806 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the digital code (round down for quantization).**

$$
D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1.85}{0.000806} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 — Numeric evaluation.**

$$
D = \lfloor 2295.28 \rfloor = 2295
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to hex (KTU-favoured step).**

$$
2295_{10} = 0x8F7
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta V = 0.806 \text{ mV/LSB}, \quad D = 2295 = \text{0x8F7}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Wireless Protocol

| Requirement | Best Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Range > 5 km, < 1 kbps | **LoRaWAN** | Long range, ultra-low power, license-free sub-GHz |
| Range < 50 m, > 1 Mbps, audio | **Wi-Fi** | High throughput, IP-native |
| Wearable fitness, coin cell | **BLE** | Sub-1 mA peaks, short bursts |
| Smart lighting, 100+ nodes | **ZigBee / Thread** | Mesh self-healing, low duty cycle |
| Underground parking meter | **NB-IoT** | Cellular coverage, deep indoor penetration |
| File transfer to phone | **Wi-Fi Direct / BLE** | High bandwidth peer-to-peer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — IoT 5-Layer Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

    tempA --> wifiA

    pirA --> loraA

    wifiA --> gwA
    bleA --> gwA
    loraA --> gwA

    mqttA --> mobA
    mqttA --> apiA

    apiA --> aiA

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Data-Flow Sequence (MQTT Publish–Subscribe)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as IoT Sensor Node
    participant MCU as Microcontroller ESP32
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker
    participant Cloud as Cloud Database
    participant App as Mobile App

    Sensor->>MCU: Read analog value 0.8 V
    MCU->>MCU: Apply calibration
    MCU->>Broker: PUBLISH /home/sensor1 23.5C
    Broker->>Cloud: Store in time-series DB
    Broker->>App: PUSH notification subscribed topic
    App->>App: Render chart on dashboard
    Note over MCU,Broker: QoS 1 - At least once delivery
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the **Internet of Things (IoT)**. List any **four** application domains of IoT.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 m):** IoT is a network of physical objects embedded with sensors, software, microcontrollers and connectivity that enables them to **collect, exchange and act on data** over the internet with minimal human intervention.
- **Four domains (1.5 m — 0.375 each):**
  1. **Smart Home** — lighting, locks, thermostats.
  2. **Smart Agriculture** — soil moisture, drone monitoring.
  3. **Healthcare** — wearables, remote patient monitoring.
  4. **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — predictive maintenance, SCADA.

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Q2.** Compare **MQTT** and **CoAP** communication protocols used in IoT. State **three** points.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each point):**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | **TCP** (reliable) | **UDP** (lightweight) |
| Pattern | **Publish–Subscribe** (broker-centric) | **Request–Response** (REST-like) |
| Overhead | Larger headers, good for **stable networks** | Tiny headers, ideal for **constrained nodes** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting to draw a **layered architecture diagram** in 14-mark IoT questions costs **2–3 marks**.
> - Writing "MQTT is faster than CoAP" (or similar) without justification is treated as a **vague statement** — examiners expect protocol-layer reasoning.
> - In numerical problems, units **must be carried** at every step. A number without units loses the final 1-mark allotment.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the **5-layer architecture of IoT** with a neat block diagram. List **two example devices** in each layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **LoRa soil sensor** transmits every 10 minutes. The transmission lasts **1.2 s at 80 mA**, the receive window lasts **0.8 s at 30 mA**, and the rest of the time the node sleeps at **4 µA**. The battery is a **3.6 V, 8500 mAh** Li-SOCl₂ cell.
> Estimate the **battery life in years**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Perception Layer (1 m + 1 m for examples):** Captures physical data using sensors; example **DHT22** (temp+humidity) and **MPU6050** (accelerometer).
2. **Network Layer (1 m + 1 m):** Transfers data using wireless/wired links; example **Wi-Fi** and **LoRa**.
3. **Middleware Layer (1 m):** Performs local processing, protocol translation, security; example **Raspberry Pi gateway** running **Mosquitto MQTT broker**.
4. **Application Layer (1 m):** Delivers user-facing services; example **Smart Home mobile app**.
5. **Business Layer (1 m):** Analytics, billing, AI/ML; example **cloud dashboard with predictive analytics**.

*(Use the **Mermaid block in SECTION 4.1** as the architecture diagram — 1 mark is reserved for the diagram.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define the period.**

$$
T_{cycle} = t_{tx} + t_{rx} + t_{sleep} = 1.2 + 0.8 + 598 = 600 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Average current (energy model).**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{tx}\cdot t_{tx} + I_{rx}\cdot t_{rx} + I_{sleep}\cdot t_{sleep}}{T_{cycle}}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{(80)(1.2) + (30)(0.8) + (4 \times 10^{-3})(598)}{600}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 96 + 24 + 2.392 = 122.392 \text{ mA}\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Final $I_{avg}$.**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{122.392}{600} = 0.20399 \text{ mA} \approx 204 \text{ µA}
$$

**Step 5 — Battery life in hours.**

$$
t_{life} = \dfrac{8500 \text{ mAh}}{0.20399 \text{ mA}} = 41{,}667 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to years.**

$$
t_{life} = \dfrac{41{,}667}{24 \times 365} \approx 4.76 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{life} \approx 4.76 \text{ years per single cell}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **anatomy of a smart device**. Explain the role of **each block** in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **12-bit ADC** in a smart gas detector has $V_{ref} = 5$ V. If the sensor output is **2.345 V**, determine the **digital output code** in **both decimal and hexadecimal**. Also calculate the **percentage quantization error**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

*(Use the **Mermaid block in SECTION 4.2** as the block diagram — 1 mark.)*

| Block | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **MCU / SoC** | Runs firmware, controls sensors/actuators, handles radio stack | 1 |
| **Sensor Front-End** | Converts physical quantity → analog signal; includes signal conditioning | 1 |
| **Actuator Driver** | Drives motors, relays, LEDs based on decisions | 1 |
| **Radio Module** | Provides Wi-Fi/BLE/LoRa connectivity | 1 |
| **Power Subsystem** | LDO / DC-DC, battery management, sleep modes | 1 |
| **Firmware / RTOS** | FreeRTOS / Zephyr scheduler, drivers, application logic | 1 |
| **Security Block** | Secure element, AES, TLS handshake | 1 |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — LSB size (voltage resolution).**

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{5}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{5}{4095} \approx 1.221 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute digital code.**

$$
D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{2.345}{0.001221} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 1920.2 \rfloor
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{10} = 1920}
$$

**Step 3 — Hex conversion.**

$$
1920_{10} = 7 \cdot 256 + 128 = 1792 + 128 = 1920 = 0x780
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{16} = \text{0x780}}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum quantization error.**

$$
E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm 0.61 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage quantization error (relative to full-scale).**

$$
E_{\%} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{ref}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.001221}{5} \times 100 = 0.0244\%
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\%} \approx 0.0244\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget the **"–1"** in the denominator $2^n - 1$ and get $\Delta V = 5/4096$. The difference is **0.024%**, but KTU examiners do deduct ½ mark for this slip.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IoT and Smart Devices (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** IoT = Physical objects + Sensors/Actuators + Internet connectivity.
- **5 Layers:** **P**erception → **N**etwork → **M**iddleware → **A**pplication → **B**usiness. Mnemonic: **"P-N-M-A-B"** (Planet-Net-Mars-Aurora-Belt).
- **Key Protocols:**
  - **Wi-Fi** = high rate, short range, high power.
  - **BLE** = ultra-low power, short range.
  - **ZigBee** = mesh, low power, 250 kbps.
  - **LoRa** = long range (km), ultra-low power, kbps.
  - **MQTT** = TCP, pub/sub.
  - **CoAP** = UDP, request/response.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ — must state explicitly.
- **ADC Resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / (2^{n} - 1)$ — note the **"–1"**.
- **Energy Model:** $E = I_{tx}t_{tx} + I_{rx}t_{rx} + I_{sleep}t_{sleep}$ — basis of battery-life proofs.
- **Path Loss:** $PL_d = PL_{d_0} + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$.
- **Smart Device Anatomy (7 blocks):** MCU, Sensor FE, Actuator, Radio, Power, Firmware, Security.
- **Exam Pattern:**
  - 3-markers → **define + list 4 examples / compare 2 protocols**.
  - 14-markers → **diagram (mandatory) + numerical (units!) + explanation**.
- **Avoid:** Vague statements like "MQTT is fast" without transport-layer reasoning.
- **Bonus Mnemonic for Smart Device:** **"M-S-A-R-P-F-S"** = **M**CU, **S**ensor, **A**ctuator, **R**adio, **P**ower, **F**irmware, **S**ecurity.
- **Key Insight:** The 5-layer architecture is **asked in almost every KTU end-sem paper** of this course — memorize it with at least **2 examples per layer**.
